```mermaid
    WL --> LPWAN["LPWAN LORAWAN SIGFOX NB IOT"]
    WL --> CELL["CELLULAR 5G URLLC LTE M"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CPS Data Transaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    SENS["SENSOR TRANSDUCER ADC"] -->|"SAMPLE F S"| ENC["ENCODER FRAMER"]
    ENC -->|"PACKET N BITS"| MAC["MAC ARBITRATION TDMA CSMA TOKEN"]
    MAC -->|"MODULATED FRAME"| PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER WIRE OR RF"]
    PHY -->|"PROPAGATION L PROP"| RX["RECEIVER DEMOD DECODE"]
    RX --> CTRL["CONTROL ALGORITHM PID MPC"]
    CTRL --> ACT["ACTUATOR DRIVE PHYSICAL PLANT"]
    CTRL -.->|"FEEDBACK L TOTAL MUST BE LESS THAN D MAX"| SENS
```

### 4.4 Timing Flow — Real-Time CPS Control Loop Deadline

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0["T0 SENSE"] --> T1["T1 ENCODE"]
    T1 --> T2["T2 TRANSMIT L TRANS"]
    T2 --> T3["T3 RECEIVE L RX"]
    T3 --> T4["T4 COMPUTE CONTROL LAW"]
    T4 --> T5["T5 ACTUATE"]
    T5 -.->|"D MAX DEADLINE"| T0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1.** Define a *Communication Protocol* in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List the three primary design goals of CPS communication.
*(Module 1, CO1, Bloom: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Communication Protocol* in CPS is a formally specified set of rules, message formats, timing semantics, and state transitions that govern the deterministic and reliable exchange of data among distributed sensors, controllers, and actuators.
**Three primary design goals:**
1. **Determinism** – bounded worst-case latency.
2. **Reliability** – robustness against noise, interference, and faults.
3. **Real-Time Guarantees** – meeting control-loop deadlines (hard/firm/soft).

> **Marking key:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming goals, 1 mark for brief justification. Award full marks only if all three goals are explicitly enumerated.

---

**Q2.** Differentiate between *Hard Real-Time* and *Soft Real-Time* communication. Give one protocol example for each class.
*(Module 1, CO1, Bloom: Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline Miss Impact** | Catastrophic system failure | Quality degradation only |
| **Examples** | EtherCAT, TSN, FlexRay, CAN-FD | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP |
| **Application** | Airbag, ABS, robotic arm control | Telemetry, dashboard updates |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q(a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **five-layer CPS protocol stack**. Compare the *application-layer* protocols **MQTT, CoAP, and OPC UA** in terms of transport, message pattern, QoS, and typical use case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A CPS protocol stack adapts the OSI model by adding *real-time* and *safety* semantics.

| Feature | MQTT | CoAP | OPC UA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683) | TCP/HTTP, Binary |
| **Message Pattern** | Pub/Sub via Broker | Request/Response + Observe | Pub/Sub + Client/Server |
| **QoS Levels** | 0 (at most), 1 (at least), 2 (exactly) | Confirmable / Non-confirmable | Guaranteed / Best-effort |
| **Encoding** | Binary (compact) | Binary (CBOR-like) | Binary, optimized |
| **Use Case** | IoT telemetry | Constrained sensor/actuator | Industry 4.0, IIoT, SCADA |

MQTT follows a **central broker** model suitable for high-latency WANs. CoAP is designed for **RFC 7252 constrained nodes** over UDP, using **RESTful verbs** (GET, PUT, POST, DELETE). OPC UA provides **rich information modeling** with built-in security, addressing, and supports **Pub/Sub** in newer specs.

**Mark split:** 3 marks for stack diagram, 4 marks for comparison table + brief explanation.

---

**Q(b).** A CAN 2.0B bus operates at **$B = 1$ Mbps**. A standard frame carries **$N = 8$ bytes** payload, with a total of **$N_{total} = 130$ bits** (including overhead). Compute the **(i) transmission delay**, **(ii)** the **propagation delay** for a 25 m cable ($v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s), and **(iii)** the **maximum bus utilization** if a control loop runs at **$f_s = 1$ kHz**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Transmission Delay**

$$L_{trans} = \frac{N_{total}}{B} = \frac{130}{1 \times 10^6} = 1.3 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.13\ \text{ms}$$

**(ii) Propagation Delay**

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{25}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 0.000125\ \text{ms}$$

**(iii) Maximum Bus Utilization**

The control loop period is $T_{cycle} = 1/f_s = 1$ ms. One frame consumes $0.130125$ ms.

$$U_{bus} = \frac{L_{trans} + L_{prop}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{0.130125}{1.0} = 0.1301 \approx 13.01\%$$

Thus, the bus can theoretically support up to $\lfloor 1/0.13 \rfloor = 7$ such frames per control cycle.

**Mark split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks including unit clarity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q(a).** Describe the **IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee** protocol stack used in Wireless CPS. Explain the **CSMA/CA channel access mechanism** and its superframe structure (beacon-enabled mode) with a diagram. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

IEEE 802.15.4 defines the **Physical (PHY)** and **MAC** layers for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs). ZigBee adds **Network (NWK)** and **Application (APL)** layers on top.

**Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode):**

- **Beacon Interval (BI)** = $aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{BO}$, where $BO$ = Beacon Order.
- **Superframe Duration (SD)** = $aBaseSuperframeDuration \times 2^{SO}$, where $SO$ = Superframe Order.
- Divided into **16 equally-sized time slots**:
  - **Active Portion:** Contention Access Period (CAP) using slotted CSMA/CA, plus optional Contention-Free Period (CFP) with Guaranteed Time Slots (GTS).
  - **Inactive Portion:** Coordinator may enter sleep mode.

**CSMA/CA Procedure:**

1. Wait for a random backoff (0 to $2^{BE}-1$ unit backoff periods, where $BE$ = Backoff Exponent, initially 3).
2. Perform Clear Channel Assessment (CCA) twice.
3. If channel idle → transmit. If busy → increment $BE$ (up to $aMaxBE=5$) and retry, up to $macMaxCSMABackoffs=4$ times.

**Mark split:** 3 marks for layered stack description, 4 marks for superframe + CSMA/CA mechanism.

---

**Q(b).** Three periodic CPS tasks have the following parameters: $T_1 = 8$ ms, $C_1 = 1$ ms; $T_2 = 16$ ms, $C_2 = 3$ ms; $T_3 = 32$ ms, $C_3 = 6$ ms. Using **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling**, test schedulability using **(i) Utilization Bound Test** and **(ii) Exact Response-Time Test** for $\tau_3$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Utilization Bound Test**

$$U = \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{16} + \frac{6}{32} = 0.125 + 0.1875 + 0.1875 = 0.500$$

$$U_{RM}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.77976$$

$$U = 0.500 \leq 0.77976 \Rightarrow \textbf{Schedulable (sufficient).}$$

**(ii) Exact Response-Time Test for $\tau_3$**

$$R_3 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{8} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{16} \right\rceil (3)$$

- **Iteration 1:** $R_3^{(0)} = 6$. $\lceil 6/8 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil 6/16 \rceil = 1$.
$$R_3^{(1)} = 6 + 1 + 3 = 10\ \text{ms}$$
- **Iteration 2:** $R_3^{(1)} = 10$. $\lceil 10/8 \rceil = 2$, $\lceil 10/16 \rceil = 1$.
$$R_3^{(2)} = 6 + 2(1) + 1(3) = 11\ \text{ms}$$
- **Iteration 3:** $R_3^{(2)} = 11$. $\lceil 11/8 \rceil = 2$, $\lceil 11/16 \rceil = 1$.
$$R_3^{(3)} = 6 + 2 + 3 = 11\ \text{ms} \quad \text{(converged)}.$$

Since $R_3 = 11$ ms $\leq D_3 = 32$ ms, **$\tau_3$ is schedulable**. $\blacksquare$

**Mark split:** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 4 marks for the iteration table.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing bit-rate units:** Writing "500 kbps = 500 × 1024 bps". The correct SI definition is **500 × 10³ = 500,000 bps**. Examiners strictly penalise this.
> 2. **Forgetting header overhead in CAN/Ethernet:** Always re-read the question; if it says "**N total bits = 130**", do not recompute overhead.
> 3. **Skipping the iteration step in the Response-Time Test:** The first guess $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ is mandatory; showing only the final value yields 0 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Hard/Soft Real-Time definitions:** Hard real-time ≠ "fast"; it means **bounded deadline**. Use the word "**catastrophic**" in your answer.
> 5. **Writing MQTT as "CoAP over TCP":** MQTT uses TCP, CoAP uses UDP. Tabular comparison questions explicitly test this.
> 6. **Ignoring units in final answers:** Always end with **ms** or **µs** in latency problems.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Communication Protocols & Standards for CPS**
> - **CPS = Computation + Networking + Physical Processes**; communication is the *nervous system*.
> - **Five-layer CPS stack:** App Domain → Info Modeling → Middleware → Transport/Network → PHY/Link.
> - **Three pillars:** Determinism, Reliability, Real-Time; add **Security/Safety** for the fourth.
> - **Hard vs Soft vs Firm real-time** — categorise by consequence of deadline miss.
> - **Latency budget:** $L_{total} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$; bandwidth = inverse of transmission time.
> - **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$; practical CPS protocols operate far below Shannon limit.
> - **RM Utilization bound:** $U_{RM}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
> - **WCRT iterative formula** — must converge before declaring schedulability.
> - **CAN 2.0 = 1 Mbps, 8-byte payload, < 1 ms typical latency**; **EtherCAT < 100 µs**; **TSN < 100 µs jitter**; **5G URLLC < 1 ms**.
> - **MQTT = TCP, Pub/Sub, Broker-based, IoT telemetry.**
> - **CoAP = UDP, RESTful, RFC 7252, Constrained Nodes.**
> - **OPC UA = Industry 4.0 standard, rich information modeling, binary encoding.**
> - **DDS = Data Distribution Service, real-time, autonomous vehicles.**
> - **ZigBee = IEEE 802.15.4 PHY/MAC, 250 kbps, 2.4 GHz, slotted CSMA/CA + superframe + GTS.**
> - **LoRaWAN = sub-GHz, LPWAN, long range, very low data rate.**
> - **5G URLLC = ultra-reliable low-latency communication, < 1 ms, 99.999% reliability.**
> - **Standards bodies to remember:** IEEE, IETF, IEC, ISO, 3GPP, ODVA, PI, OPC Foundation.
> - **Key safety standards:** IEC 61508 (industrial), ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62443 (industrial security).
> - **Remember:** A protocol is *not* just a wire format — it includes **timing, addressing, error handling, and security**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Topologies and Architecture for CPS (TCP/IP, HTTP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Topologies and Architecture for CPS (TCP/IP, HTTP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) – Communication Context

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (cyber) are tightly integrated with physical processes via sensors and actuators. The **communication network** acts as the nervous system that bridges the cyber and physical domains, enabling real-time monitoring, control, and coordination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCPT602 – Module 1):**  
> *CPS communication encompasses the network topologies, protocol stacks, and architectural patterns used to interconnect sensors, actuators, controllers, and computing nodes across the physical and cyber layers.*

## 1.2 Network Topologies – Formal Definition

A **network topology** is the arrangement or layout of nodes (devices) and links (communication channels) in a CPS communication network. It defines **how devices are interconnected** and **how data flows** between them.

### Conceptual Analogy – "The Highway System"
Think of a CPS network like a city’s road map:
- **Nodes** = Cities (sensors, controllers, actuators)
- **Links** = Roads (wired/wireless channels)
- **Topology** = The road network design (which cities connect directly)
- **Protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP)** = The traffic rules that govern how vehicles (data packets) move

A **mesh topology** is like a city where every city has direct roads to several others — multiple paths exist, so if one road is blocked, traffic still flows. A **star topology** is like a hub-and-spoke airport system where all flights pass through one central airport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CPS, topology choice is **not arbitrary** — it directly impacts **latency, reliability, fault-tolerance, and real-time determinism**, which are critical for safety-critical applications (e.g., autonomous vehicles, industrial automation, smart grids).

## 1.3 Standard Topology Types in CPS

| Topology | Physical/Logical Layout | CPS Use Case |
|----------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Bus** | All devices share a single backbone cable | Legacy industrial CAN/LIN buses |
| **Star** | All devices connect to a central hub/switch | Small IoT clusters, lab CPS |
| **Ring** | Devices form a closed loop | Token Ring, industrial Fieldbus |
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to multiple peers | Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), Zigbee |
| **Tree (Hierarchical)** | Star networks connected in a tree | Smart building automation |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more topologies | Industrial IoT (IIoT), Smart Grids |

## 1.4 The TCP/IP Protocol Suite – Overview

**TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** is the foundational communication protocol suite of the Internet and the de-facto standard for CPS networking. It is a **4-layer architecture** that abstracts communication from physical signaling to application semantics.

### Intuition: "The Postal System"
Imagine sending a parcel internationally:
1. You write the **letter** (Application – HTTP data)
2. You pack it in a **sealed envelope with tracking** (Transport – TCP ensures delivery)
3. The envelope gets a **global postal address** (Internet – IP addressing/routing)
4. The postal truck drives it on **local roads** (Link – Ethernet/Wi-Fi/Serial)

Each layer adds its own **header** (metadata) to deliver the parcel correctly — this is **encapsulation**.

## 1.5 HTTP – HyperText Transfer Protocol

**HTTP** is an **application-layer request-response protocol** of the TCP/IP suite, used for transferring hypermedia documents (HTML, JSON, XML). In CPS, HTTP underpins **RESTful APIs** that allow cloud controllers to interact with field devices, sensors, and actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HTTP is **stateless**, **text-based**, and runs over **TCP port 80** (or **443** for HTTPS). In CPS, it is typically used for **non-real-time, cloud-tier communication** (e.g., telemetry uploads, dashboard updates), not for hard real-time control loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Topologies – Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Bus Topology
- **Mechanism:** All nodes tap into a single shared communication medium (e.g., RS-485, CAN bus).
- **Signal propagation:** Both directions along the backbone; **terminating resistors** prevent reflection.
- **Collision handling:** Uses CSMA/CD-style arbitration (CAN uses CSMA/CR – Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution via message priority).
- **Fault tolerance:** Single point of failure — backbone break disables entire network.
- **CPS relevance:** Automotive CAN, industrial Modbus RTU.

### 2.1.2 Star Topology
- **Mechanism:** Central switch/hub coordinates all communication; nodes communicate only via the center.
- **Performance:** Dependent on central node capacity.
- **Fault tolerance:** Failure of one leaf node does not affect others; central node failure = full network failure.
- **CPS relevance:** PLC-centered control cabinets, Wi-Fi-based IoT clusters.

### 2.1.3 Ring Topology
- **Mechanism:** Data travels in one (or both) direction(s) around a closed loop; each node acts as a repeater.
- **Access control:** Token passing ensures deterministic access.
- **Fault tolerance:** Single ring = single point of failure; **dual-ring** (e.g., FDDI, ERPS) provides redundancy.
- **CPS relevance:** Industrial Fieldbus, optical token rings in substations.

### 2.1.4 Mesh Topology
- **Mechanism:** Each node connects to **N** neighbors; data can take multiple paths.
- **Routing:** Dynamic protocols (AODV, RPL for IoT) find optimal paths.
- **Fault tolerance:** **Highest** — multiple redundant paths.
- **CPS relevance:** Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), Zigbee, Thread, smart grid AMI.
- **Sub-types:** Full mesh (every node-to-node link) vs. partial mesh.

### 2.1.5 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology
- **Mechanism:** Groups of star networks connected in a parent-child hierarchy.
- **CPS relevance:** SCADA systems, building automation (BACnet), hierarchical industrial control.

### 2.1.6 Hybrid Topology
- **Mechanism:** Combines two or more topologies (e.g., star-bus, star-ring).
- **CPS relevance:** Industrial IoT gateways, smart city infrastructure.

## 2.2 Comparative Analysis – Topology Selection Matrix

| Criterion | Bus | Star | Ring | Mesh | Tree |
|-----------|-----|------|------|------|------|
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Limited | Moderate | Limited | High | High |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low | Medium | Medium | **Very High** | Medium |
| **Latency (deterministic)** | Medium | Low | **High** | Variable | Medium |
| **Cable Complexity** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | **High** | Moderate |
| **CPS Suitability** | Automotive CAN | PLC hubs | Industrial | WSN/IoT | SCADA |

## 2.3 TCP/IP Four-Layer Architecture – Detailed Analysis

### Layer 1: Link (Network Access) Layer
- **Function:** Frames data for transmission over the physical medium; defines hardware addressing (MAC).
- **Protocols/PHYs:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), CAN, RS-485, 6LoWPAN (for IoT).
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** **Frame**
- **Why in CPS:** Connects sensors/actuators to local network; supports deterministic Ethernet variants like **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking, IEEE 802.1Qbv)** for hard real-time CPS.

### Layer 2: Internet (Network) Layer
- **Function:** Logical addressing (IP) and routing of packets across networks.
- **Protocols:** **IPv4, IPv6** (essential for IoT — IPv6 enables massive address space), ICMP, routing protocols (OSPF, BGP, RPL).
- **PDU:** **Packet (Datagram)**
- **CPS note:** **6LoWPAN** allows IPv6 over low-power IEEE 802.15.4 networks, bridging WSN to the Internet.

### Layer 3: Transport Layer
- **Function:** End-to-end communication, flow control, reliability, multiplexing.
- **Core Protocols:**
  - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, byte-stream. Uses 3-way handshake. **Higher overhead, higher latency** — suitable for non-real-time CPS (e.g., firmware updates, cloud telemetry).
  - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless, unreliable, low overhead. **Suitable for real-time CPS** (e.g., sensor streaming, control commands).
- **PDU:** TCP → **Segment**; UDP → **Datagram**
- **Ports:** 16-bit identifiers (0–6553); HTTP uses **TCP 80**, HTTPS uses **443**.

### Layer 4: Application Layer
- **Function:** Provides network services to end-user applications.
- **Protocols:** **HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, DNS, DHCP, MQTT, CoAP, SNMP, Modbus TCP**.
- **PDU:** **Message / Data**
- **CPS note:** RESTful HTTP APIs dominate cloud-tier CPS communication; MQTT/CoAP dominate edge-tier IoT.

## 2.4 Data Encapsulation & Decapsulation in TCP/IP

**Encapsulation** (sender side, top-down):
1. Application generates **Data** (e.g., HTTP request)
2. Transport adds TCP/UDP header → **Segment / Datagram**
3. Internet adds IP header → **Packet**
4. Link adds MAC header + trailer → **Frame**
5. Physical layer transmits as **bits**

**Decapsulation** (receiver side, bottom-up) is the reverse process, with each layer stripping its header.

## 2.5 HTTP – Protocol Mechanics

### 2.5.1 HTTP Request/Response Model
- **Client** (e.g., CPS gateway) sends an HTTP request.
- **Server** (e.g., cloud controller) processes it and returns a response.

### 2.5.2 HTTP Request Structure
```
<METHOD> <URI> HTTP/<version>\r\n
Host: <hostname>\r\n
User-Agent: <client>\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: <bytes>\r\n
\r\n
[BODY]
```

### 2.5.3 HTTP Response Structure
```
HTTP/<version> <status-code> <reason-phrase>\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: <bytes>\r\n
\r\n
[BODY]
```

### 2.5.4 HTTP Methods (Verbs) in CPS

| Method | Purpose | CPS Example |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **GET** | Retrieve resource | Fetch latest sensor reading from `/api/sensors/temp` |
| **POST** | Create new resource | Send new actuator command `/api/actuators/pump` |
| **PUT** | Update/replace resource | Update device firmware config `/api/device/123` |
| **DELETE** | Remove resource | Deregister a sensor `/api/sensors/42` |
| **PATCH** | Partial update | Adjust setpoint of PID controller |

### 2.5.5 HTTP Status Codes (Essential for CPS)
- **1xx** – Informational
- **2xx** – Success (`200 OK`, `201 Created`, `204 No Content`)
- **3xx** – Redirection
- **4xx** – Client error (`400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `429 Too Many Requests`)
- **5xx** – Server error (`500 Internal Server Error`, `503 Service Unavailable`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CPS, handling of **5xx** errors is critical — a failed response in a control loop can lead to actuator staleness. Always implement **timeouts, retries with exponential backoff, and fallback behavior**.

## 2.6 REST Architecture for CPS

**REST (Representational State Transfer)** is an architectural style for designing networked applications, defined by Roy Fielding (2000). RESTful HTTP APIs are the de-facto standard for cloud-CPS integration.

### REST Constraints
1. **Client-Server** separation
2. **Statelessness** – each request contains all info
3. **Cacheability** – responses can be marked cacheable
4. **Uniform Interface** – standard methods + URIs
5. **Layered System** – intermediaries (proxies, gateways) allowed
6. **(Code on Demand – optional)**

### CPS Application
- **Resources** = sensors, actuators, controllers, telemetry streams
- **URIs** = `https://api.cps.example.com/v1/devices/{id}/telemetry`
- **Representations** = JSON (most common), XML, CBOR

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Quick Reference

### KTU Formula & Metric Sheet – TCP/IP & HTTP for CPS

| Concept | Key Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary | Notes |
|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|-------|
| **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Data Delivered}}{\text{Time}}$ | bits/s (bps) | End-to-end useful data rate |
| **Latency** | $L = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$ | seconds (s) | Sum of propagation + transmission + queue + processing |
| **Propagation Delay** | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | s | $d$ = distance, $v$ = signal speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber) |
| **Transmission Delay** | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{packet}}}{R}$ | s | $L_{\text{packet}}$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $\text{RTT} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc,total}}$ | s | Critical for HTTP request-response |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $\text{BDP} = R \cdot \text{RTT}$ | bits | Max unacknowledged data in-flight |
| **TCP 3-Way Handshake Cost** | $1.5 \cdot \text{RTT}$ to first data byte | s | HTTP over TCP pays this overhead |
| **HTTP Request Size (approx.)** | Header $\approx 500$–$800$ B + Body | bytes | Optimize with keep-alive, compression |
| **IPv4 Address Space** | $2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^{9}$ addresses | integer | Insufficient for IoT |
| **IPv6 Address Space** | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ addresses | integer | Required for massive CPS/IoT |
| **TCP Port Range** | $0$–$65535$ ($2^{16}$) | integer | HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443 |
| **Mesh Redundancy** | Full mesh links $= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | integer | $n$ = number of nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes `|` inside table cells** — used `\vert` and `\approx` symbols where needed to maintain markdown compatibility.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grids:** TCP/IP + HTTP/MQTT for SCADA telemetry; mesh for last-mile AMI.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** CAN bus (in-vehicle) + 4G/5G TCP/IP (V2X cloud).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC UA over TCP, RESTful HTTP for cloud analytics.
- **Healthcare CPS:** HTTP REST APIs transmit patient vitals to cloud EHRs.
- **Smart Cities:** Hybrid (mesh + star) topology; HTTP for dashboards, CoAP for constrained sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total End-to-End Latency in a CPS HTTP Transaction

Let us derive the **total time** for a CPS sensor to send a JSON payload to a cloud server via HTTP over TCP/IP and receive an acknowledgment.

### Given (typical CPS scenario):
- Distance $d = 500$ km (sensor site to cloud data center)
- Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (fiber optic)
- Link rate $R = 100$ Mbps
- HTTP request body $L_{\text{req}} = 200$ bytes
- HTTP response body $L_{\text{res}} = 1500$ bytes (sensor + metadata)
- TCP header $H_{\text{TCP}} = 20$ bytes
- IP header $H_{\text{IP}} = 20$ bytes
- MAC header + trailer $H_{\text{LINK}} = 38$ bytes
- Number of intermediate routers $N_r = 8$
- Per-router processing delay $T_{\text{proc,hop}} = 0.5$ ms
- Queueing delay (average) $T_{\text{queue}} = 2$ ms per hop

### Step 1: Compute Frame Size for Request
The request packet after encapsulation:

$$
L_{\text{frame,req}} = L_{\text{req}} + H_{\text{TCP}} + H_{\text{IP}} + H_{\text{LINK}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{frame,req}} = 200\ \text{B} + 20\ \text{B} + 20\ \text{B} + 38\ \text{B} = 278\ \text{bytes}
$$

Converting to bits (multiply by 8):

$$
L_{\text{frame,req,bits}} = 278 \times 8 = 2224\ \text{bits}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Frame Size for Response
Similarly:

$$
L_{\text{frame,res}} = 1500 + 20 + 20 + 38 = 1578\ \text{bytes}
$$

$$
L_{\text{frame,res,bits}} = 1578 \times 8 = 12{,}624\ \text{bits}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Propagation Delay (one-way)
Using the formula $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}$:

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{3}\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 2.5\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Transmission Delay for Request
Using $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$:

$$
T_{\text{trans,req}} = \frac{2224\ \text{bits}}{100 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bps}} = 22.24\ \mu\text{s}
$$

For response:

$$
T_{\text{trans,res}} = \frac{12{,}624}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 126.24\ \mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Per-Hop Processing + Queueing Delay
Per-hop non-transmission delay:

$$
T_{\text{hop}} = T_{\text{proc,hop}} + T_{\text{queue}} = 0.5\ \text{ms} + 2\ \text{ms} = 2.5\ \text{ms}
$$

For $N_r = 8$ routers, summed across path (request + response traverse hops twice):

$$
T_{\text{hops,total}} = 2 \cdot N_r \cdot T_{\text{hop}} = 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 2.5\ \text{ms} = 40\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 6: Add TCP 3-Way Handshake Overhead
HTTP over TCP requires a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) before data. This adds approximately **1.5 RTT** (round-trip times):

$$
\text{RTT} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{prop}} = 2 \cdot 2.5\ \text{ms} = 5\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{handshake}} = 1.5 \cdot \text{RTT} = 1.5 \cdot 5\ \text{ms} = 7.5\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 7: Sum All Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{handshake}} \\
&\quad + 2 \cdot (T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans,req}}) \\
&\quad + 2 \cdot (T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans,res}}) \\
&\quad + T_{\text{hops,total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 7.5 + 2(2.5 + 0.02224) + 2(2.5 + 0.12624) + 40
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 7.5 + 5.04448 + 5.25248 + 40 = 57.797\ \text{ms}
$$

### Final Result
$$
\boxed{T_{\text{total}} \approx 57.8\ \text{ms}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This ~58 ms latency is **acceptable for monitoring CPS** (e.g., remote dashboard updates) but **NOT acceptable for hard real-time control** (which needs <10 ms). For real-time CPS, use UDP, TSN, or 5G URLLC.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Topology vs. CPS Requirement

| CPS Requirement | Best Topology | Justification | Supporting Metric |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Low Latency** | Star (with cut-through switch) | Minimal hops; deterministic | $L \approx T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{switch}}$ |
| **High Reliability** | Mesh (full or partial) | Multiple redundant paths | Failure survival = $1 - (1-r)^{k}$ |
| **Low Cost / Simple Wiring** | Bus | Single cable, easy install | Cable length $\propto n$ |
| **Deterministic Real-Time** | Ring (token-based) | Guaranteed token rotation | Max latency = $n \cdot T_{\text{token hold}}$ |
| **Massive Scalability (IoT)** | Tree / Hierarchical Mesh | Modular expansion | Supports thousands of nodes (RPL/6LoWPAN) |
| **Mobility (vehicles, drones)** | Hybrid (Mesh + Cellular) | Dynamic topology support | Handoff delay in cellular |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – TCP vs UDP for CPS

| Parameter | TCP | UDP | CPS Recommendation |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------------------|
| **Connection** | Connection-oriented (3WHS) | Connectionless | TCP for cloud; UDP for real-time |
| **Reliability** | ACK + retransmit | None | TCP for firmware update; UDP for streaming |
| **Ordering** | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed | TCP for log upload |
| **Overhead** | 20+ B header, handshake | 8 B header | UDP for constrained nodes |
| **Latency** | Higher | Lower | UDP for control loops |
| **Flow/Congestion Control** | Yes | No | TCP protects network; UDP needs app-level |
| **Use in CPS** | Cloud telemetry, OTA | Sensor streaming, control | **Both used contextually** |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: HTTP Request-Response Example in a CPS

**Scenario:** A smart factory CPS gateway queries a cloud analytics service for the current power consumption of a machine.

**Step 1 – Gateway constructs HTTP GET request:**

```
GET /api/v1/machines/M-007/power HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: api.smartfactory.example.com\r\n
Accept: application/json\r\n
User-Agent: CPSGateway/2.4\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

**Step 2 – TCP encapsulates** with source port (e.g., ephemeral 54321), destination port 80, sequence number, ACK flag, window size.

**Step 3 – IP encapsulates** with source IP (e.g., `203.0.113.45`), destination IP (e.g., `198.51.100.10`), TTL=64, protocol=6 (TCP).

**Step 4 – Link encapsulates** with source/destination MAC, EtherType 0x0800 (IPv4).

**Step 5 – Cloud server processes** the request, queries its database, builds JSON response.

**Step 6 – Server sends HTTP response:**

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: 87\r\n
\r\n
{"machine_id":"M-007","power_kW":42.7,"timestamp":"2025-09-14T10:32:18Z","status":"nominal"}
```

**Step 7 – Gateway decapsulates** bottom-up, parses JSON, and uses the value to update local HMI or trigger control action if power exceeds threshold.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Communication Architecture – High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER - Sensors and Actuators"]
        S1["Temperature Sensor"]
        S2["Pressure Sensor"]
        S3["Vibration Sensor"]
        A1["Motor Actuator"]
        A2["Valve Actuator"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["EDGE LAYER - Gateways and Controllers"]
        G1["IoT Gateway - Modbus to IP"]
        G2["Edge Controller - PLC / RTU"]
        G3["Edge Aggregator"]
    end

    subgraph FOG["FOG LAYER - Local Servers"]
        F1["SCADA Server"]
        F2["Local Historian DB"]
        F3["Edge Analytics Engine"]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD LAYER - Data Center"]
        C1["Cloud API Server - HTTP REST"]
        C2["Time Series Database"]
        C3["ML Analytics Platform"]
        C4["Dashboard and Visualization"]
    end

    S1 --> G1
    S2 --> G1
    S3 --> G2
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    A1 <-. control .-> G2
    A2 <-. control .-> G3
    G3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C1 <-. RESTful HTTP .-> C4
```

## 4.2 Network Topologies for CPS – Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUS["BUS TOPOLOGY"]
        B1[Node1] --- BUSLINE(("Shared Backbone"))
        B2[Node2] --- BUSLINE
        B3[Node3] --- BUSLINE
        B4[Node4] --- BUSLINE
    end

    subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        HUB[Central Switch]
        S1n[Node1] --- HUB
        S2n[Node2] --- HUB
        S3n[Node3] --- HUB
        S4n[Node4] --- HUB
    end

    subgraph RING["RING TOPOLOGY"]
        R1[Node1] --- R2[Node2]
        R2 --- R3[Node3]
        R3 --- R4[Node4]
        R4 --- R1
    end

    subgraph MESH["MESH TOPOLOGY"]
        M1[Node1] --- M2[Node2]
        M1 --- M3[Node3]
        M1 --- M4[Node4]
        M2 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
        M3 --- M4
    end
```

## 4.3 TCP/IP 4-Layer Encapsulation in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        DATA["HTTP JSON Payload - Sensor Reading"]
    end

    subgraph TRANS["TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP"]
        TH["TCP Header - SrcPort DstPort Seq Ack Win"]
        SEG["SEGMENT = TH + DATA"]
    end

    subgraph NET["INTERNET LAYER - IP"]
        IH["IP Header - SrcIP DstIP TTL Protocol"]
        PKT["PACKET = IH + SEG"]
    end

    subgraph LINK["LINK LAYER - Ethernet or Wi-Fi"]
        LH["MAC Header - SrcMAC DstMAC EtherType"]
        LT["MAC Trailer - FCS CRC32"]
        FRM["FRAME = LH + PKT + LT"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        BITS["Bits on Wire - Electrical Optical RF"]
    end

    DATA --> TH
    TH --> SEG
    SEG --> IH
    IH --> PKT
    PKT --> LH
    LH --> FRM
    FRM --> LT
    LT --> BITS
```

## 4.4 HTTP Request-Response Sequence in CPS

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as CPS Sensor/Gateway
    participant TCP as TCP Layer
    participant IP as IP Layer
    participant Net as Network Routers
    participant Server as Cloud HTTP Server

    Sensor->>TCP: HTTP GET /sensors/temp
    TCP->>IP: Add TCP header - SYN
    IP->>Net: Add IP header
    Net->>Server: Route packet
    Server->>Net: SYN-ACK back
    Net->>IP: Forward to sensor
    IP->>TCP: ACK completes handshake
    Note over Sensor,Server: 3-Way Handshake Complete
    Sensor->>Server: HTTP GET request data
    Server->>Sensor: HTTP 200 OK with JSON telemetry
    Sensor->>Sensor: Parse and act on reading
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["Start - Choose CPS Topology"]) --> Q1{"Need Hard Real-Time under 10ms?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> STAR["Use Star with TSN Ethernet"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is mobility required drones vehicles?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MESH["Use Wireless Mesh - Zigbee 6LoWPAN"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Number of nodes greater than 100?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> TREE["Use Tree/Hierarchical - RPL over 6LoWPAN"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Need high fault tolerance?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> FULLM["Use Full or Partial Mesh"]
    Q4 -- No --> RINGOPT["Use Ring - Token Passing Fieldbus"]
    STAR --> END(["Topology Selected"])
    MESH --> END
    TREE --> END
    FULLM --> END
    RINGOPT --> END
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between TCP and UDP in the context of Cyber-Physical System (CPS) communication. State one CPS scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Connection type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | ACK + retransmission (reliable) | No ACK (unreliable) |
| Ordering | Guaranteed in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee |
| Header size | 20 bytes (minimum) | 8 bytes |
| Overhead | High | Low |
| Latency | Higher | Lower |

**CPS preference:**
- **TCP preferred for:** Cloud telemetry upload, OTA firmware update (reliability critical).
- **UDP preferred for:** Real-time control loops, high-frequency sensor streaming (latency critical).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "TCP is faster than UDP" — this is **wrong**. TCP is reliable but has higher latency due to handshakes and retransmissions. UDP is the lower-latency option.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three network topologies used in CPS. Compare mesh and star topologies in terms of fault tolerance and scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

Three common CPS network topologies: **Bus, Star, Mesh** (also valid: Ring, Tree, Hybrid).

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Star | Mesh |
|--------|------|------|
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low – central switch failure = total network outage | **High** – multiple redundant paths; single link/node failure does not isolate the network |
| **Scalability** | Limited by central node port count | **High** – new nodes add multiple paths; suitable for large-scale WSN |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (more links) |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Requires routing protocols |

**Justification:** Mesh is preferred in **wireless sensor networks and smart grid deployments** where reliability outweighs cost; star is preferred in **small PLC-centered control cabinets**.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **four-layer TCP/IP architecture** used in Cyber-Physical Systems. State the function and one example protocol of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **HTTP request-response model** with a suitable CPS example. List and briefly describe any four HTTP methods relevant to CPS. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – TCP/IP Four-Layer Architecture**

The TCP/IP model is the foundational protocol suite for CPS communication, comprising four abstraction layers. Each layer performs specific functions and communicates with its peer layer on the other end through well-defined protocols.

**Layer 4 – Application Layer**
- **Function:** Provides network services to end-user CPS applications. Handles high-level protocols, data representation, and user-facing semantics.
- **Example Protocols:** HTTP/HTTPS, MQTT, CoAP, FTP, DNS, SNMP
- **PDU:** Message / Data

**Layer 3 – Transport Layer**
- **Function:** Provides end-to-end communication between CPS nodes. Manages multiplexing via port numbers, flow control, error recovery, and connection management.
- **Example Protocols:** **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented) and **UDP** (unreliable, connectionless)
- **PDU:** Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)

**Layer 2 – Internet (Network) Layer**
- **Function:** Logical addressing and routing of packets across interconnected networks. Enables packet forwarding from source CPS node to destination.
- **Example Protocols:** **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, RPL** (for IoT)
- **PDU:** Packet / Datagram

**Layer 1 – Link (Network Access) Layer**
- **Function:** Defines hardware (MAC) addressing, framing, and physical medium access. Interfaces with the physical layer.
- **Example Protocols/PHYs:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), CAN, RS-485, 6LoWPAN**
- **PDU:** Frame

*Refer to the encapsulation diagram in Section 4.3.*

**Part (b) – HTTP Request-Response Model with CPS Example**

HTTP operates on a **client-server request-response paradigm** over TCP. The client (e.g., CPS gateway) sends a structured request to the server (e.g., cloud API), which processes it and returns a structured response.

**CPS Example:** A smart agriculture gateway queries a cloud analytics service for soil moisture of field zone 5.

```
GET /api/v1/fields/5/moisture HTTP/1.1
Host: api.smartagri.example.com
Accept: application/json
```

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
{"field":"5","moisture_pct":27.3,"irrigation_needed":true}
```

**Four HTTP Methods Relevant to CPS:**

1. **GET** – Retrieves a resource representation. Example: `GET /api/sensors/temp` to fetch current temperature.
2. **POST** – Creates a new resource or triggers an action. Example: `POST /api/actuators/irrigation {"action":"start","duration_s":300}`.
3. **PUT** – Replaces an existing resource. Example: `PUT /api/devices/42 {"firmware_version":"2.1.0"}`.
4. **DELETE** – Removes a resource. Example: `DELETE /api/sensors/old-99` to deregister a decommissioned sensor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **POST and PUT**. **POST creates** a new resource; **PUT updates/replaces** an existing resource at a known URI. Misusing them violates REST semantics and may break idempotency assumptions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Describe **mesh, star, and ring network topologies** with neat diagrams. Compare them on the basis of fault tolerance, cost, and CPS suitability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **encapsulation in TCP/IP**? Explain the process with a CPS example, showing how an HTTP message from a sensor becomes bits on the wire. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – Mesh, Star, Ring Topologies**

**Star Topology:** All nodes connect to a central hub/switch. Data passes through the center.
- **Diagram:**

```
    N1     N2
      \   /
       \ /
       HUB
       / \
      /   \
    N3     N4
```

- **Fault tolerance:** Moderate (central node = SPOF).
- **Cost:** Moderate.
- **CPS suitability:** Small PLC clusters, lab setups.

**Ring Topology:** Nodes form a closed loop; data circulates in one/both directions; token passing for access.
- **Diagram:**

```
   N1 --- N2
   |       |
   N4 --- N3
```

- **Fault tolerance:** Single ring is fragile; **dual-ring** is robust (FDDI, ERPS).
- **Cost:** Moderate.
- **CPS suitability:** Industrial Fieldbus, substation automation (deterministic).

**Mesh Topology:** Every node connects to multiple peers; multiple paths exist.
- **Diagram:**

```
   N1 --- N2
   |\   /|
   | \ / |
   |  X  |
   | / \ |
   |/   \|
   N3 --- N4
```

- **Fault tolerance:** **Highest** (multiple redundant paths).
- **Cost:** Highest (more links/routing).
- **CPS suitability:** Wireless Sensor Networks, smart grid AMI, military CPS.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Criterion | Star | Ring | Mesh |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| Fault tolerance | Moderate | Moderate (high if dual) | **Very High** |
| Cost | Low–Moderate | Moderate | High |
| CPS suitability | Local control | Deterministic industrial | IoT / WSN / Smart grid |

**Part (b) – Encapsulation in TCP/IP with CPS Example**

**Encapsulation** is the process of adding protocol-specific **headers (and trailers)** at each layer of the TCP/IP stack as data moves from the application down to the physical medium. Each layer treats the upper layer's PDU as **payload** and prepends its own control information.

**CPS Example:** A temperature sensor wants to report `{"temp":23.5}` to a cloud server.

**Step 1 – Application Layer (HTTP):**
Payload = `{"temp":23.5}` (15 bytes JSON)

**Step 2 – Transport Layer (TCP):**
Adds a 20-byte TCP header (src port, dst port 80, seq, ack, flags, window, checksum):
- Segment size = 15 + 20 = 35 bytes

**Step 3 – Internet Layer (IP):**
Adds a 20-byte IPv4 header (src IP, dst IP, TTL, protocol=6, checksum):
- Packet size = 35 + 20 = 55 bytes

**Step 4 – Link Layer (Ethernet):**
Adds 14-byte MAC header (src/dst MAC, EtherType) + 4-byte FCS trailer:
- Frame size = 55 + 14 + 4 = 73 bytes

**Step 5 – Physical Layer:**
The 73-byte frame (584 bits) is converted to **electrical/optical/RF signals** and transmitted bit-by-bit on the medium.

```
[MAC HDR 14B][IP HDR 20B][TCP HDR 20B][HTTP DATA 15B][FCS 4B]
                  (PDUs nested inside each other)
```

At the receiver, **decapsulation** occurs in reverse order — each layer strips its header, passes the payload upward, until the application receives the original JSON.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit the trailer (FCS)** in the link layer encapsulation diagram. The Ethernet frame has both a **header and a 4-byte Frame Check Sequence (CRC32) trailer** — losing this mark is common.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 1: Network Topologies and Architecture for CPS (TCP/IP, HTTP)**

### ⭐ Network Topologies
- **Bus**: Cheap, single-cable, vulnerable to backbone failure (CAN, Modbus).
- **Star**: Centralized; SPOF at hub; common in PLC clusters.
- **Ring**: Deterministic token passing; dual-ring = high reliability (FDDI).
- **Mesh**: **Most fault-tolerant**; multiple paths; used in WSN/smart grid.
- **Tree**: Hierarchical; supports SCADA and large IoT deployments.
- **Hybrid**: Real-world CPS (e.g., smart city) uses combinations.

### ⭐ TCP/IP Four Layers (Top → Bottom)
1. **Application** – HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, FTP, DNS, SNMP → PDU: **Data/Message**
2. **Transport** – **TCP** (reliable, 3WHS) / **UDP** (fast, connectionless) → PDU: **Segment / Datagram**
3. **Internet** – **IPv4 / IPv6**, ICMP, RPL → PDU: **Packet**
4. **Link** – Ethernet, Wi-Fi, CAN, 6LoWPAN → PDU: **Frame** (+ FCS trailer)

### ⭐ Encapsulation Order
`Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bits` (each step adds a header; link layer also adds trailer)

### ⭐ HTTP Essentials
- **Port 80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS)
- **Stateless**, **text-based**, runs over **TCP**
- **Methods**: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (update/replace), DELETE (remove), PATCH (partial)
- **Status codes**: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error
- **REST**: Resource-based, URI-addressed, JSON payload, stateless
- HTTP is suitable for **cloud-tier** CPS, not hard real-time control

### ⭐ CPS-Specific Caveats
- **Hard real-time** (<10 ms) → use **TSN, UDP, 5G URLLC**, NOT HTTP/TCP.
- **Massive device scale** → use **IPv6 + 6LoWPAN**, not IPv4.
- **Constrained nodes** → prefer **CoAP/MQTT** over HTTP.
- **Latency formula**: $L = T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{proc}}$
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product**: $\text{BDP} = R \cdot \text{RTT}$ — determines TCP window sizing

### ⭐ Examiner's Quick-Recall Mnemonics
- **"A-T-I-L"** for TCP/IP layers: **A**pplication, **T**ransport, **I**nternet, **L**ink
- **"D-S-P-F-B"** for PDUs: **D**ata, **S**egment, **P**acket, **F**rame, **B**its
- **HTTP methods**: **"Get Post Put Delete Patch"** → *"Give People Proper Delete Privileges"*

### ⭐ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Forgetting the **FCS trailer** in link-layer encapsulation.
2. Confusing **POST vs PUT** semantics.
3. Claiming TCP is faster than UDP (it is reliable, not faster).
4. Using HTTP for hard real-time control (latency too high).
5. Not mentioning **3-way handshake overhead** (1.5 RTT) for HTTP/TCP.
6. Forgetting to specify units in latency/throughput calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Performance Matrices (e.g., Throughput, Latency, Jitter)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Network Performance Matrices (e.g., Throughput, Latency, Jitter) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview In the domain of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, where software processes directly interact with physical processes over a network, communication is not merely about *whether* a packet arrives, but *how reliably, how fast, and how predictably* it arrives. **Network Performance Matrices (Metrics)** are the quantitative tools engineers use to measure and validate the Quality of Service (QoS) of the communication channel that binds the cyber and physical worlds. ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Network Performance Matrix** (or metric) is a measurable quantity that characterizes the behavior, efficiency, and reliability of a communication network under specified operating conditions. In CPS contexts, these metrics determine whether the network can satisfy **hard real-time constraints** (e.g., a robotic arm receiving motion commands within 1 ms) or **soft real-time constraints** (e.g., a temperature dashboard updating every second). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** Performance matrices form the *first step* in CPS communication design. You CANNOT design a CPS without first specifying the throughput, latency, jitter, and reliability budgets. These act as the *contract* between the application layer and the network layer. ### The Three Core Performance Matrices #### 1. Throughput (Effective Bandwidth) - **Formal Definition:** The *actual* rate of successful data delivery over a communication channel, measured in **bits per second (bps)**, usually averaged over a specific time window. - **Intuitive Analogy (Highway Analogy):** Imagine a multi-lane highway with a posted speed limit of **100 km/h** (this is the *bandwidth* or *theoretical capacity*). However, due to toll booths, accidents, and slow trucks, the cars actually move at an *average effective speed* of **60 km/h**. That **60 km/h is the throughput.** Throughput is the *reality*; bandwidth is the *promise*. #### 2. Latency (Delay) - **Formal Definition:** The time taken for a single data unit (bit, byte, packet, or message) to travel from the source to the destination. Measured in **seconds (s)**, typically **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (µs)** in CPS. - **Intuitive Analogy (Pizza Delivery Analogy):** You order a pizza. The time from when you place the order to when the pizza arrives at your door is the **latency**. This includes: ordering time (transmission delay), pizza-making time (processing delay), oven time (queuing delay), and travel time (propagation delay). For CPS, low latency is non-negotiable. #### 3. Jitter (Delay Variation) - **Formal Definition:** The *variation* in latency from one packet to the next in a stream of packets. Mathematically, it is the **standard deviation** or the **difference between maximum and minimum** observed latencies. Measured in **ms** or **µs**. - **Intuitive Analogy (Bus Schedule Analogy):** A city bus is scheduled to arrive every 10 minutes. Sometimes it arrives after 8 minutes, sometimes after 12 minutes. That 4-minute fluctuation is the **jitter**. In Voice over IP (VoIP) or CPS control loops, high jitter causes *buffer overflows, dropped packets, or jerky robot motion*. ### Extended Performance Matrices (Beyond the Core Three) > [!IMPORTANT] > While the syllabus highlights throughput, latency, and jitter, a complete CPS-grade communication system also tracks: > > - **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR):** Fraction of transmitted packets that fail to arrive. Critical for UDP-based CPS protocols like CoAP. > - **Bit Error Rate (BER):** Number of bit errors per unit time. Critical in wireless CPS (e.g., Industrial IoT over 5G). > - **Availability / Reliability:** Probability that the network is operational when needed (e.g., **99.999%** "five-nines" reliability for medical CPS). > - **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** Time for a packet to go *and* come back. Used in TCP-based control loops. > - **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** Maximum amount of data that can be "in flight" on the network at any instant. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposition of Latency (The Four Sub-Delays) Total latency is **not** a single phenomenon. It is the sum of four distinct, physically-rooted delays. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU numerical problems. 1. **Transmission Delay ($D_t$):** Time to push all the packet's bits onto the physical medium. - Depends on **packet size (L)** and **link bandwidth (R)**. - Analogy: The time to load boxes onto a truck. Bigger truck or smaller boxes = faster. 2. **Propagation Delay ($D_p$):** Time for a signal to physically travel across the medium. - Depends on **distance (d)** and **signal propagation speed (s)**. - Analogy: The time the truck is on the road, regardless of how many boxes it carries. Speed of light in fiber is roughly $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. 3. **Processing Delay ($D_{proc}$):** Time for a router/switch to examine the packet header and decide where to forward it. - Depends on router hardware (ASIC, CPU, software stack). 4. **Queuing Delay ($D_q$):** Time a packet waits in a buffer before being transmitted. - **The most variable component** — directly causes **jitter**. - Depends on network congestion and queue scheduling (FIFO, Priority, WFQ). ### 2.2 Throughput Degradation Factors Raw bandwidth (e.g., 1 Gbps) is **never** equal to throughput because of: - **Protocol Overhead:** TCP/IP headers (typically **40 bytes** for IPv4, **60 bytes** for IPv6) consume capacity without carrying user data. - **Retransmissions:** Lost packets are resent, consuming additional bandwidth. - **Collisions (Wi-Fi/Shared Media):** CSMA/CA backoff reduces effective throughput. - **Cross-Traffic & Congestion:** Other flows compete for the same link. - **Flow Control & Windowing:** TCP's sliding window temporarily limits throughput. ### 2.3 Jitter: Why It Matters in CPS Jitter is **catastrophic** for *synchronous* CPS applications because: - Control theory assumes **periodic sampling**. If sample $k$ arrives at time $t_k$ but sample $k+1$ arrives at $t_{k+1} + \Delta$, the controller's integral and derivative terms become **erroneous**. - Audio/Video streams produce "glitches," "stutters," or "robotic voice" effects. - A **jitter buffer** at the receiver can absorb jitter, but it adds *additional fixed latency* — a classic engineering trade-off. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize the units and boundary conditions. | # | Metric | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Limiting Case | |---|--------|---------|-------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Transmission Delay | $D_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | $\to 0$ as $L \to 0$ or $R \to \infty$ | | 2 | Propagation Delay | $D_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = propagation speed (m/s) | $s \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber, $3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum | | 3 | Total One-Way Latency | $D_{total} = D_t + D_p + D_{proc} + D_q$ | All in seconds (s) | $D_q$ is the only stochastic term | | 4 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful data delivered}}{\text{Time taken}}$ | bits/second (bps) | $\eta \leq R$ always | | 5 | Throughput Efficiency | $\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Often expressed as a percentage | | 6 | Jitter (Statistical) | $J = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | $D_i$ = $i$-th delay sample, $\bar{D}$ = mean delay | Same unit as delay (s) | | 7 | Jitter (Peak-to-Peak) | $J_{pp} = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | Used in real-time systems | Lower bound is 0 for lossless FIFO | | 8 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times D_{prop}$ | bits | = max unacknowledged data on link | | 9 | Packet Loss Ratio | $PLR = \dfrac{N_{lost}}{N_{sent}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq PLR \leq 1$ | | 10 | Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 \times D_{total} + D_{ack}$ | seconds | Used in TCP throughput (Mathis formula) | | 11 | Utilization (Little's Law) | $U = \lambda \times \bar{D}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate (pkts/s), $\bar{D}$ = avg delay | $U < 1$ for stability | | 12 | Goodput | $G = \dfrac{\text{Application-level data}}{T_{total}}$ | bps | $G \leq \eta \leq R$ | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Autonomous Vehicles (V2V/V2X):** Latency budget $\leq$ **10 ms**, jitter $\leq$ **1 ms**. Throughput $\geq$ **10 Mbps** for sensor fusion. - **Smart Grid (IEC 61850):** Protection commands require latency $\leq$ **3 ms** (Type 1A messages). - **Industrial IoT (TSN — Time-Sensitive Networking):** Guarantees jitter $\leq$ **1 µs** over Ethernet using IEEE 802.1Qbv time-aware scheduling. - **Telemedicine / Remote Surgery:** RTT $\leq$ **200 ms** for haptic feedback; loss $\leq$ **0.1%**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency Composition We start with a single packet traveling across **$N$ hops** (links + routers) from source to destination. **Step 1 — Define the latency on a single hop $i$:** On hop $i$, the packet experiences all four sub-delays. The total time spent on hop $i$ is: $$ D_i = D_{t,i} + D_{p,i} + D_{proc,i} + D_{q,i} $$ **Step 2 — Sum over all hops:** Since the packet must traverse each hop sequentially (it cannot skip routers), the end-to-end latency is the **sum** of per-hop latencies: $$ D_{end-to-end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( D_{t,i} + D_{p,i} + D_{proc,i} + D_{q,i} \right) $$ **Step 3 — Group by delay type (engineering insight):** This can be re-grouped to reveal the structural components: $$ D_{end-to-end} = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{t,i}}_{\text{Transmission}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{p,i}}_{\text{Propagation}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{proc,i}}_{\text{Processing}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} D_{q,i}}_{\text{Queuing}} $$ **Engineering Insight:** Transmission and propagation are *deterministic* and *under designer control* (choose higher bandwidth, shorter cables). Processing is *nearly deterministic* for modern hardware. **Queuing is the only truly stochastic term** — this is why jitter is dominated by queue dynamics. --- ### Derivation 2: Relationship Between Bandwidth, Packet Size, and Transmission Delay **Problem Setup:** A CPS sensor node transmits a 1 KB telemetry packet over a 250 kbps LoRa link. **Step 1 — Convert units consistently:** Packet size: $L = 1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 8192 \text{ bits}$. Link rate: $R = 250 \text{ kbps} = 250{,}000 \text{ bps}$. **Step 2 — Apply the transmission delay formula:** $$ D_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{8192 \text{ bits}}{250{,}000 \text{ bits/s}} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the division:** $$ D_t = 0.032768 \text{ s} = 32.768 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret:** Just to *push the bits onto the wire* takes **~33 ms**. This is the dominant delay for low-bandwidth CPS links (LoRa, NB-IoT). It is *not* the speed of light limiting us — it is the sheer slowness of the modem. --- ### Derivation 3: Jitter Calculation for a Sampled Control Stream **Problem Setup:** A CPS controller receives 5 successive samples with the following end-to-end latencies: $D_1 = 10$ ms, $D_2 = 14$ ms, $D_3 = 9$ ms, $D_4 = 18$ ms, $D_5 = 11$ ms. **Step 1 — Compute the mean latency:** $$ \bar{D} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} D_i = \frac{10 + 14 + 9 + 18 + 11}{5} = \frac{62}{5} = 12.4 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the squared deviations from the mean:** $$ (D_1 - \bar{D})^2 = (10 - 12.4)^2 = (-2.4)^2 = 5.76 $$ $$ (D_2 - \bar{D})^2 = (14 - 12.4)^2 = (1.6)^2 = 2.56 $$ $$ (D_3 - \bar{D})^2 = (9 - 12.4)^2 = (-3.4)^2 = 11.56 $$ $$ (D_4 - \bar{D})^2 = (18 - 12.4)^2 = (5.6)^2 = 31.36 $$ $$ (D_5 - \bar{D})^2 = (11 - 12.4)^2 = (-1.4)^2 = 1.96 $$ **Step 3 — Sum the squared deviations:** $$ \sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2 = 5.76 + 2.56 + 11.56 + 31.36 + 1.96 = 53.20 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the standard deviation (RMS Jitter):** $$ J = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2} = \sqrt{\frac{53.20}{5}} = \sqrt{10.64} \approx 3.26 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the peak-to-peak jitter for completeness:** $$ J_{pp} = D_{max} - D_{min} = 18 - 9 = 9 \text{ ms} $$ **Engineering Verdict:** A mean delay of 12.4 ms is acceptable for many CPS loops, but a jitter of 3.26 ms (or peak-to-peak 9 ms) means the *worst-case* delay is **18 ms** — which may violate the deadline. The designer must either add a jitter buffer (increasing latency) or upgrade the network (reducing jitter). --- ### Derivation 4: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) and the "Pipe" Concept **Physical Intuition:** The link is a "pipe" with length $D_{prop}$ (in time) and cross-section $R$ (in bps). The pipe's *volume* is the maximum data that can be "in flight." **Step 1 — Start with link rate and propagation delay:** $$ R = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 10^8 \text{ bps}, \quad D_{prop} = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.02 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Multiply them (definition of BDP):** $$ BDP = R \times D_{prop} = 10^8 \text{ bps} \times 0.02 \text{ s} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ Mbits} = 250 \text{ KB} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret:** At any instant, **250 KB of data can be "in the pipe"** between sender and receiver. This is the minimum buffer size the receiver must allocate, and the minimum window size the sender must use, to fully utilize the link without stalls. If TCP's window is smaller than BDP, throughput collapses. --- ### Derivation 5: Throughput Efficiency of a Framed CPS Protocol **Problem Setup:** An Industrial IoT protocol uses a frame structure of **20 bytes overhead + 100 bytes payload**. The link runs at **1 Mbps**. Frames are sent back-to-back. **Step 1 — Compute the total frame size:** $$ L_{frame} = L_{overhead} + L_{payload} = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ bytes} = 960 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the efficiency ratio:** $$ \eta_{eff} = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{frame}} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333 = 83.33\% $$ **Step 3 — Compute the *useful* throughput:** $$ \eta = R \times \eta_{eff} = 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 0.8333 = 833{,}333 \text{ bps} \approx 833.3 \text{ kbps} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the *wasted* bandwidth:** $$ R_{waste} = R - \eta = 1{,}000{,}000 - 833{,}333 = 166{,}667 \text{ bps} \approx 166.7 \text{ kbps} $$ **Engineering Verdict:** 16.7% of the link is permanently consumed by protocol overhead. In a 10,000-sensor deployment, this overhead is amplified — a key reason protocols like **CoAP (4-byte header)** and **MQTT-SN (2-byte header)** are preferred over HTTP for CPS. --- ### Analytical Matrix: How the Metrics Interact | Interaction | Mathematical Relationship | Engineering Implication | |-------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | Throughput vs. Bandwidth | $\eta \leq R$ (always) | Bandwidth is the ceiling; throughput is the floor of reality. | | Latency vs. Distance | $D_p \propto d$ | Cannot be reduced without moving closer (or faster medium). | | Latency vs. Bandwidth | $D_t \propto \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Higher bandwidth reduces transmission delay, *not* propagation delay. | | Jitter vs. Queuing | $J \uparrow$ when $D_q \uparrow$ | Jitter is governed by network congestion and queue discipline. | | BDP vs. TCP Window | If $W_{TCP} < BDP$, then $\eta$ collapses | Set $W_{TCP} \geq BDP$ for line-rate throughput. | | Loss vs. Throughput | Mathis: $\eta \approx \dfrac{MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ where $p$ = loss prob. | Even 1% loss can halve TCP throughput on high-BDP links. | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Diagram: How the Four Sub-Delays Compose Into End-to-End Latency The following Mermaid block shows how a single packet's journey decomposes into the four canonical delay components at every hop, and how queuing — the stochastic term — becomes the dominant source of jitter. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Sensor Node
Source] -->|Bit Serialization| nodeA[Hop 1: Router A] nodeA -->|Wire 1 Propagation| nodeB[Hop 2: Router B] nodeB -->|Wire 2 Propagation| nodeC[Hop 3: Router C] nodeC -->|Wire 3 Propagation| dst[Controller Node
Destination] src -.->|Dt1 Transmission| nodeA nodeA -.->|Dproc1 Processing| nodeA nodeA -.->|Dq1 Queuing| nodeA nodeA -.->|Dp1 Propagation| nodeB nodeB -.->|Dt2 Transmission| nodeB nodeB -.->|Dproc2 Processing| nodeB nodeB -.->|Dq2 Queuing| nodeB nodeB -.->|Dp2 Propagation| nodeC nodeC -.->|Dt3 Transmission| nodeC nodeC -.->|Dproc3 Processing| nodeC nodeC -.->|Dq3 Queuing| nodeC nodeC -.->|Dp3 Propagation| dst style src fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style dst fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style nodeA fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style nodeB fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style nodeC fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 ``` > **Reading Guide:** The **solid arrows** are the packet's physical path. The **dashed arrows** represent the *temporal* delays experienced at each stage. Note that **Processing** and **Queuing** occur at the router (orange node), while **Transmission** occurs at the *exit* of each node and **Propagation** occurs *between* nodes. ### 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Performance Metric Acquisition Pipeline This block diagram shows how raw physical-layer events are transformed into the high-level performance metrics used by a CPS application. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHY[Physical Layer Events] s1[Bit sent timestamp ts] s2[Bit received timestamp tr] s3[Packet error flag] s4[Bytes acknowledged] end subgraph PROC[Metric Computation Engine] m1[Compute Dp = tr minus ts
Propagation Delay] m2[Compute Dt = L divided by R
Transmission Delay] m3[Compute PLR = lost divided by sent
Packet Loss Ratio] m4[Compute eta = bytes delivered divided by T
Throughput] m5[Compute J = StdDev of D total
Jitter] end subgraph APP[CPS Application Layer] a1[Control Loop
Deadline Check] a2[QoS Dashboard] a3[Adaptive Sampling
Rate Controller] a4[Alert System
SLA Violation] end s1 --> m1 s2 --> m1 s1 --> m2 s3 --> m3 s4 --> m4 m1 --> m5 m2 --> m5 m5 --> a1 m3 --> a1 m4 --> a2 m5 --> a3 m5 --> a4 style PHY fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc style PROC fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600 style APP fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933 ``` > **Reading Guide:** The **Physical Layer Events** block captures raw timestamps from the NIC/hardware. The **Metric Computation Engine** applies the formulas from Section 2. The **CPS Application Layer** consumes these metrics to make control decisions (e.g., if jitter > threshold, *reduce sampling rate*). ### 4.3 Decision Flow: Diagnosing Network Problems Using Performance Metrics ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Performance Anomaly Detected] --> q1{Which metric is
out of spec?} q1 -->|Latency too high| p1[Check Propagation:
Is link too long?] q1 -->|Latency too high| p2[Check Transmission:
Is bandwidth too low?] q1 -->|Latency too high| p3[Check Queuing:
Is traffic congested?] q1 -->|Jitter too high| p4[Check Queuing Discipline:
FIFO or Priority?] q1 -->|Throughput too low| p5[Check Loss:
Wireless interference?] q1 -->|Throughput too low| p6[Check Overhead:
Header too large?] q1 -->|Throughput too low| p7[Check Window Size:
W TCP less than BDP?] p1 --> fix1[Shorten link or upgrade medium] p2 --> fix2[Upgrade bandwidth] p3 --> fix3[Add QoS or backpressure] p4 --> fix4[Use TSN or DiffServ] p5 --> fix5[Improve RF or add FEC] p6 --> fix6[Migrate to CoAP or MQTT-SN] p7 --> fix7[Tune TCP window scaling] style start fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style q1 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style fix1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix3 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix4 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix5 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix6 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style fix7 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 ``` > **Reading Guide:** A network engineer diagnosing a CPS problem follows this decision tree. Each metric violation points to a specific root cause and a specific remedy. This is the *diagnostic thinking pattern* KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between **bandwidth** and **throughput** in a CPS communication network. Why is throughput always less than or equal to bandwidth? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Bandwidth | Throughput | |--------|-----------|------------| | **Definition** | Theoretical maximum data rate of a link (capacity) | Actual rate of *successful* data delivery | | **Nature** | Static, determined by physical medium | Dynamic, varies with load and conditions | | **Unit** | bps (bits per second) | bps (bits per second) | | **Analogy** | Width of a highway | Actual number of cars passing a point per hour | Throughput is always $\leq$ bandwidth because of: 1. **Protocol overhead** (headers, acknowledgments, framing bits) 2. **Retransmissions** due to packet loss 3. **Collisions and contention** in shared media (Wi-Fi, CAN bus) 4. **Flow-control mechanisms** (TCP sliding window pauses) 5. **Cross-traffic and congestion** reducing available capacity *(Award 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for correct analogy, 1 mark for the five causes.)* --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define **jitter** in the context of CPS communication. Explain why a non-zero jitter is problematic for a real-time control loop. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition:** Jitter is the variation in end-to-end latency observed across a sequence of consecutively transmitted packets. Mathematically, it is the standard deviation (RMS jitter) or the peak-to-peak difference of the delay samples. **Problem for Control Loops (1.5 marks):** A real-time CPS controller (e.g., a PID controller for motor speed) assumes **periodic sampling** at a fixed interval $T$. If sample $k$ arrives at time $t_0 + kT$ but sample $k+1$ arrives at $t_0 + (k+1)T + \Delta$, the controller's computations become erroneous: - The **derivative term** $de/dt$ uses $\Delta t$ in its denominator; a variable $\Delta t$ produces noisy derivatives. - The **integral term** accumulates over a non-uniform window, causing integrator windup. - **Worst-case** behavior: the deadline is missed, the controller outputs **stale** or **zero** values, and the physical system becomes unstable. **Remedy (0.5 marks):** Use a **jitter buffer** at the receiver (adds fixed latency) or upgrade to a deterministic network (e.g., TSN, FlexRay, Profinet IRT). --- ### PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCPT602, Module 1]** (a) **[7 Marks]** A Cyber-Physical System for smart manufacturing uses a wireless sensor network (WSN) to monitor 200 vibration sensors on a production line. Each sensor generates a 256-byte telemetry packet every 10 ms. The WSN gateway aggregates these packets and forwards them to a cloud controller over a TCP/IP link with a bandwidth of 5 Mbps and a one-way propagation delay of 5 ms. The TCP/IP stack adds a 40-byte header per packet. The TCP receive window is 64 KB. **Compute:** (i) The aggregate raw data rate generated by the sensors. (ii) The transmission delay for a single sensor's packet on the gateway's uplink. (iii) The Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) of the uplink. (iv) The throughput efficiency of the framed packet. (v) Will the TCP window limit throughput? Justify. (b) **[7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the four components of end-to-end latency. A CPS feedback loop receives 8 consecutive samples with latencies: **12, 15, 11, 22, 10, 14, 18, 13 ms**. Compute the mean latency, RMS jitter, and peak-to-peak jitter. If the control loop's hard deadline is **20 ms**, how many packets would be considered "on-time"? --- #### **QUESTION B — Option 2 / Internal Choice (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCPT602, Module 1]** (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **jitter** in CPS communication. Why is jitter more critical in a wireless CPS than in a wired one? Describe **three techniques** used at different layers (application, transport, network/link) to mitigate jitter. (b) **[7 Marks]** A V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) CPS uses a Dedicated Short-Range Communication (DSRC) link with a bandwidth of 6 Mbps. Safety messages are 500 bytes long, transmitted every 100 ms. The vehicles are 300 m apart, and the signal propagation speed is $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (effectively reduced by DSRC PHY to $2 \times 10^8$ m/s). (i) Compute the transmission delay per safety message. (ii) Compute the propagation delay between the two vehicles. (iii) Compute the one-way latency (ignoring processing and queuing). (iv) If the safety system requires an end-to-end latency of **≤ 2 ms**, can DSRC meet the requirement? What is the maximum allowable distance? (v) Comment on the suitability of this latency for collision-avoidance braking (typical human reflex is 250 ms; modern AEB systems act in 150 ms). --- ### COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS --- #### Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** **(i) Aggregate raw data rate:** Each sensor produces $256 \text{ bytes} = 2048 \text{ bits}$ every 10 ms. Rate per sensor: $$ R_{sensor} = \frac{2048 \text{ bits}}{0.01 \text{ s}} = 204{,}800 \text{ bps} = 204.8 \text{ kbps} $$ For 200 sensors (assuming no aggregation gain): $$ R_{agg} = 200 \times 204.8 \text{ kbps} = 40{,}960 \text{ kbps} \approx 40.96 \text{ Mbps} $$ **(ii) Transmission delay for one sensor's packet on 5 Mbps uplink:** Single sensor's frame: $L = 256 + 40 = 296 \text{ bytes} = 2368 \text{ bits}$. $$ D_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2368 \text{ bits}}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 4.736 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 0.474 \text{ ms} $$ **(iii) Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** $$ BDP = R \times D_{prop} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 25{,}000 \text{ bits} \approx 3.125 \text{ KB} $$ **(iv) Throughput efficiency:** $$ \eta_{eff} = \frac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + L_{header}} = \frac{256}{256 + 40} = \frac{256}{296} = 0.8649 = 86.49\% $$ **(v) TCP window analysis:** TCP window $W = 64 \text{ KB} = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}$. Since $W = 524{,}288 \text{ bits} \gg BDP = 25{,}000 \text{ bits}$, the TCP window is **NOT** the bottleneck. The link can be fully utilized. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(a):** > Students often confuse **per-sensor rate** with **aggregate rate** and forget to add the 40-byte header. KTU expects the 40-byte TCP/IP header to be *included* in the frame size for the transmission delay calculation. Forgetting it costs 1 full mark. Also, students frequently compare $W$ in KB with $BDP$ in bits without unit conversion — this is a silent 0.5-mark deduction. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Block Diagram of Four Latency Components:** ``` [SOURCE] --[Dt: Serialization on NIC]--> [TX WIRE: Dp Propagation] --> [ROUTER: Dproc Header check + Dq Queue wait] --> [TX WIRE: Dp] --> [ROUTER: Dproc + Dq] --> [TX WIRE: Dp] --> [DESTINATION] ``` | Component | Where it occurs | Deterministic? | Dominant in... | |-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------| | Transmission ($D_t$) | Source/Node NIC | Yes | Low-bandwidth links (LoRa, NB-IoT) | | Propagation ($D_p$) | Physical medium | Yes | Long-haul links, satellite | | Processing ($D_{proc}$) | Router/Switch | Nearly | Slow CPU-based routers | | Queuing ($D_q$) | Router buffer | **No — stochastic** | Congested networks | **Numerical Computation (8 samples: 12, 15, 11, 22, 10, 14, 18, 13 ms):** **Mean latency:** $$ \bar{D} = \frac{12+15+11+22+10+14+18+13}{8} = \frac{115}{8} = 14.375 \text{ ms} $$ **Squared deviations:** | $D_i$ | $D_i - \bar{D}$ | $(D_i - \bar{D})^2$ | |-------|-----------------|----------------------| | 12 | −2.375 | 5.6406 | | 15 | 0.625 | 0.3906 | | 11 | −3.375 | 11.3906 | | 22 | 7.625 | 58.1406 | | 10 | −4.375 | 19.1406 | | 14 | −0.375 | 0.1406 | | 18 | 3.625 | 13.1406 | | 13 | −1.375 | 1.8906 | Sum: $\sum = 5.6406 + 0.3906 + 11.3906 + 58.1406 + 19.1406 + 0.1406 + 13.1406 + 1.8906 = 109.875$ **RMS Jitter:** $$ J = \sqrt{\frac{109.875}{8}} = \sqrt{13.734} \approx 3.706 \text{ ms} $$ **Peak-to-Peak Jitter:** $$ J_{pp} = D_{max} - D_{min} = 22 - 10 = 12 \text{ ms} $$ **On-time packets (deadline 20 ms):** Packets with $D_i \leq 20$ ms: 12, 15, 11, 10, 14, 18, 13 → **7 out of 8** packets. The packet with 22 ms missed the deadline. **On-time ratio = 87.5%.** > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):** > Many students compute the jitter using only the **range** (peak-to-peak) and skip the RMS computation. KTU syllabus explicitly demands *both* metrics — RMS shows average behavior, peak-to-peak shows worst case. Forgetting one loses 1 mark. Also, students often miscount "on-time" packets because they confuse "≤ 20" with "< 20." --- #### Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Jitter in CPS is the *variation* in latency from packet to packet in a continuous stream. It directly impacts: - Sample timing accuracy in control loops - Audio/video playback smoothness - Synchronization between distributed CPS nodes **Why wireless CPS has higher jitter (2 marks):** 1. **Shared, contention-based medium:** Wi-Fi/DSRC use CSMA/CA — backoff times are random. 2. **Multipath fading and shadowing:** Signal strength varies, causing retransmissions. 3. **Interference from other devices:** Microwave ovens, Bluetooth, other Wi-Fi networks. 4. **Mobility:** Doppler shift, handovers between access points introduce variable delays. 5. **Variable link rate (rate adaptation):** The PHY rate drops in poor SNR, increasing $D_t$. **Three mitigation techniques (3 marks):** | Layer | Technique | Mechanism | |-------|-----------|-----------| | Application | **Jitter buffer / playout buffer** | Receiver buffers packets and plays them out at a steady rate, smoothing out variations. Adds fixed latency. | | Transport | **RTP/RTCP with timestamps** | Sender embeds timestamps; receiver reconstructs timing, detects and reorders late packets. | | Network / Link | **QoS tagging (IEEE 802.1Q, DiffServ, TSN time-aware scheduling)** | High-priority CPS traffic is placed in a separate queue with reserved bandwidth, eliminating contention-based queueing delay. | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(a):** > A common student error is to suggest "use a faster network" as a jitter mitigation. **Speed reduces latency, not jitter.** Jitter is about *consistency*, not *speed*. The examiner will deduct marks if you don't distinguish the two. Also, students often list the same layer's techniques multiple times — KTU expects a *spread* across layers. --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Given: $R = 6 \text{ Mbps} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$, $L = 500 \text{ bytes} = 4000 \text{ bits}$, $d = 300 \text{ m}$, $s = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $T_{period} = 100 \text{ ms}$, Deadline $T_{deadline} = 2 \text{ ms}$. **(i) Transmission delay:** $$ D_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{4000 \text{ bits}}{6 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 0.667 \text{ ms} $$ **(ii) Propagation delay:** $$ D_p = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{300 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.0015 \text{ ms} = 1.5 \text{ \mu s} $$ **(iii) One-way latency (no processing/queuing):** $$ D_{total} = D_t + D_p = 0.667 + 0.0015 \approx 0.6685 \text{ ms} \approx 0.67 \text{ ms} $$ **(iv) Can DSRC meet the 2 ms deadline at 300 m?** **Yes.** $D_{total} = 0.67 \text{ ms} < 2 \text{ ms}$. The deadline is met with a margin of $2 - 0.67 = 1.33$ ms. **Maximum allowable distance for the 2 ms deadline:** Set $D_{total} = 2$ ms. Since $D_t$ is fixed at 0.667 ms (depends only on $L$ and $R$): $$ D_p^{max} = 2 - 0.667 = 1.333 \text{ ms} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} $$ $$ d_{max} = s \times D_p^{max} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 266{,}667 \text{ m} \approx 266.7 \text{ km} $$ > **Note:** This is a *theoretical* maximum.
