| Coherence time | $T_c \approx 0.423 / f_m$ | seconds | Slow/fast classification |
| Coherence bandwidth | $B_c \approx 1 / (2\pi \sigma_\tau)$ | Hz | Flat/selective |
| Rayleigh PDF | $f(r) = (r/\sigma^{2}) \exp(-r^{2}/2\sigma^{2})$ | $r \geq 0$ | NLOS multipath |
| Rician PDF | $f(r) = (r/\sigma^{2})\exp[-(r^{2}+A^{2})/2\sigma^{2}] I_{0}(Ar/\sigma^{2})$ | $A$ = LOS amplitude, $I_0$ = Bessel-0 | LOS + multipath |
| Rician K-factor | $K = A^{2} / (2\sigma^{2})$ | dB or linear | LOS strength metric |
| Outage probability (Rayleigh, threshold $\gamma_{th}$) | $P_{out} = 1 - \exp(-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma})$ | $\bar{\gamma}$ = avg SNR | Threshold-based design |
| Average BER (Rayleigh, BPSK) | $\overline{P_b} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - \sqrt{\bar{\gamma}/(1+\bar{\gamma})})$ | — | Diversity gain analysis |
| CCI reuse factor | $Q = D/R$ | $D$ = co-channel distance | Cellular planning |
| Delay spread condition (flat fading) | $B \ll B_c$ or $T_s \gg \sigma_\tau$ | — | Classify fading type |
| Shannon capacity (AWGN) | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s | Upper-bound benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In a 14-mark derivation question, examiners will deduct marks if you write $B_c = 1/\sigma_\tau$ without the $2\pi$ factor, or forget that $f_d$ depends on $\cos\theta$. Always state the *assumed condition* (isotropic scattering, 2-D vs 3-D, Jakes spectrum) before writing a formula.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart factory CPS (5G URLLC):** Use Rician-K factor to model mmWave LOS between robotic arms; outage probability budgets drive reliability targets ($10^{-5}$ for 99.999%).
- **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) at 5.9 GHz:** Doppler spread $f_m$ can exceed **1 kHz** at 200 km/h — fast fading dominates. Coherence time $\approx 0.4$ ms forces very short OFDM symbols.
- **NB-IoT for smart metering:** Operates at 868 MHz with deep indoor shadowing; log-normal $X_\sigma$ up to **10 dB**; battery lifetime is *dominated* by retransmissions caused by shadowing.
- **Healthcare wearables (Body-Area Networks):** Rician-K factor near 6–10 dB for on-body LOS; deep fades during walking cause burst errors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Marks are awarded for *each* logical transition. Never combine steps. Show the substitution explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Received Power

**Goal:** Derive the power received by an antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ located at distance $d$ from an isotropic radiator of power $P_t$.

**Step 1 — Power density at distance $d$ for an isotropic radiator.**
The total transmitted power $P_t$ is uniformly spread over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$. The surface area of a sphere is $4\pi d^{2}$. Therefore, the power flux density (power per unit area) at the receiver is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{[W/m}^{2}\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — Effective aperture of the receiving antenna.**
By antenna theory, the effective area that captures the incident wave is related to the antenna gain $G_r$ and the wavelength $\lambda$ by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \quad \text{[m}^{2}\text{]}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply flux density by effective aperture.**
The total power delivered to the receiver's matched load is the captured flux:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Final Friis transmission equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where we generalized by introducing $G_t$, the transmit antenna gain (replacing the isotropic assumption).

**Step 5 — Convert to dB form (for KTU link-budget problems).**
Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides and using $\lambda = c/f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r \,[\text{dBm}] &= P_t \,[\text{dBm}] + G_t \,[\text{dBi}] + G_r \,[\text{dBi}] \\
&\quad - 20\log_{10}(d\,[\text{m}]) - 20\log_{10}(f\,[\text{MHz}]) - 32.44
\end{aligned}
$$

> The constant $\mathbf{32.44}$ comes from $20\log_{10}(4\pi c) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 3 \times 10^{8} \cdot 10^{-6}) = 32.44$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Two-Ray Ground Reflection Path Loss

**Goal:** Show that in a flat-earth two-ray model with transmit height $h_t$, receive height $h_r$, and ground reflection, the path loss exponent becomes **4** at long distance.

**Step 1 — Direct and reflected path lengths.**
Using the image method, the direct path length and reflected path length differ by a path difference $\Delta \approx 2 h_t h_r / d$ (for $d \gg h_t, h_r$).

**Step 2 — Phase difference at the receiver.**
The corresponding phase shift is $\Delta\phi = 2\pi \Delta / \lambda \approx 4\pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d)$.

**Step 3 — Electric field at the receiver.**
The total field is the sum of the direct ray and the reflected ray (assumed reflected with phase inversion, $\pi$ rad shift):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= E_{dir} - E_{ref} \cdot e^{-j \Delta\phi} \\
&\approx E_{dir}(1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}) \quad \text{(assuming } E_{ref} \approx E_{dir}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Vector addition of two equal-magnitude vectors with phase $\Delta\phi$.**
For small $\Delta\phi$ (large $d$), $\lvert 1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi} \rvert \approx \Delta\phi / \sqrt{...}$, but for moderate-to-large $\Delta\phi$ we use the chord of two unit phasors:

$$\lvert E_{total} \rvert = 2 \lvert E_{dir} \rvert \lvert \sin(\Delta\phi/2) \rvert$$

**Step 5 — At long distance, $\Delta\phi$ is small enough that $\sin(\Delta\phi/2) \approx \Delta\phi/2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lvert E_{total} \rvert &\approx 2 \lvert E_{dir} \rvert \cdot \frac{\Delta\phi}{2} = \lvert E_{dir} \rvert \cdot \Delta\phi \\
&\approx \lvert E_{dir} \rvert \cdot \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Received power scales as $|E|^{2}$, and $E_{dir} \propto 1/d$ from free-space spreading. Therefore:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r \propto \frac{1}{d^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{d^{2}} = \frac{1}{d^{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$PL_{2\text{-ray}}(d) = \frac{d^{4}}{h_t^{2}\, h_r^{2}} \cdot (\text{constant})$$

> The path loss exponent is **4** — characteristic of the two-ray model. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you say "4" without showing this geometric derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rayleigh Fading PDF

**Goal:** Derive the probability density function of the received envelope $r$ when there is **no line-of-sight (NLOS)** path, and the in-phase ($I$) and quadrature ($Q$) components are i.i.d. zero-mean Gaussian.

**Step 1 — In-phase and quadrature components.**
The complex baseband received signal is $r_c = I + jQ$, where, by the Central Limit Theorem applied to a large number of multipath contributions:

$$I \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2}), \qquad Q \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$$

**Step 2 — Envelope and phase.**

$$r = \sqrt{I^{2} + Q^{2}}, \qquad \phi = \arctan(Q/I)$$

**Step 3 — Joint PDF of $(I, Q)$.**

$$f_{I,Q}(i, q) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{i^{2} + q^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Change of variables to polar coordinates $(r, \phi)$, with Jacobian $J = r$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{r,\phi}(r, \phi) &= f_{I,Q}(r\cos\phi, r\sin\phi) \cdot r \\
&= \frac{r}{2\pi \sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Marginalize out $\phi$ (uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r(r) &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} f_{r,\phi}(r, \phi)\, d\phi \\
&= \frac{r}{2\pi \sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \\
&= \frac{r}{\sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right), \quad r \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **Rayleigh PDF**. KTU will accept this derivation line-by-line.

**Step 6 — Mean and variance of $r$** (often asked in Part A):
- $\mathbb{E}[r] = \sigma \sqrt{\pi/2}$
- $\mathbb{E}[r^{2}] = 2\sigma^{2}$ (which is the average received power $\bar{P_r}$)

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rician Fading PDF

**Goal:** Add a deterministic LOS component of amplitude $A$ to the Rayleigh model.

**Step 1 — Modified in-phase and quadrature components.**
$I \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^{2})$, $Q \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$.

**Step 2 — Transform to polar $(r, \phi)$ and integrate over $\phi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r(r) &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{r}{2\pi \sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r\cos\phi - A)^{2} + (r\sin\phi)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) d\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic in the exponent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(r\cos\phi - A)^{2} + r^{2}\sin^{2}\phi = r^{2} - 2Ar\cos\phi + A^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and separate $r$ terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r(r) &= \frac{r}{2\pi \sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2} + A^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) \int_{0}^{2\pi} \exp\!\left(\frac{Ar\cos\phi}{\sigma^{2}}\right) d\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the integral as $2\pi I_0(Ar/\sigma^{2})$,** where $I_0(\cdot)$ is the modified Bessel function of the first kind, order zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2} + A^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) I_{0}\!\left(\frac{Ar}{\sigma^{2}}\right), \quad r \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Rician K-factor** (LOS-to-multipath power ratio):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K = \frac{A^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \quad \text{(linear)}, \qquad K_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} K
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Limiting case:** As $A \to 0$ (NLOS), $I_0(0) = 1$ and the Rician PDF *reduces* to Rayleigh — a favourite KTU sub-question.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Outage Probability under Rayleigh Fading

**Goal:** Find the probability that the instantaneous SNR $\gamma$ falls below a threshold $\gamma_{th}$.

**Step 1 — SNR is proportional to $r^{2}$.** With average SNR $\bar{\gamma} = \mathbb{E}[r^{2}] / N_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_\gamma(\gamma) = \frac{1}{\bar{\gamma}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\gamma}{\bar{\gamma}}\right), \quad \gamma \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Outage probability is the CDF evaluated at $\gamma_{th}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} = P(\gamma < \gamma_{th}) = \int_{0}^{\gamma_{th}} f_\gamma(\gamma)\, d\gamma = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\gamma_{th}}{\bar{\gamma}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Insight:** In a 14-mark question, follow this with the *diversity gain* derivation: with $L$-branch maximal-ratio combining (MRC), the SNR is the sum of $L$ independent Rayleigh SNR's, giving $\bar{P}_{out} \approx (1/\bar{\gamma})^{L}$ — an $L$-fold power gain.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Classification Cheat-Sheet

| Channel Type | Condition on $B$ | Condition on $T_s$ | Distribution | Typical CPS Scenario |
|--------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Flat, slow fading | $B \ll B_c$ | $T_s \ll T_c$ | Rayleigh / Rician | Static sensor in factory |
| Flat, fast fading | $B \ll B_c$ | $T_s \gg T_c$ | Time-varying Rayleigh | High-speed V2V |
| Frequency-selective, slow | $B \gg B_c$ | $T_s \ll T_c$ | Rayleigh tapped-delay-line | Indoor Wi-Fi |
| Frequency-selective, fast | $B \gg B_c$ | $T_s \gg T_c$ | Doubly-selective | mmWave 5G mobile |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Channel Impairment Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wireless Channel Impairments] --> B[Interference]
    A --> C[Fading]
    A --> D[Noise]
    B --> B1[Co-Channel CCI]
    B --> B2[Adjacent Channel ACI]
    B --> B3[Inter-Symbol ISI]
    C --> C1[Large-Scale Fading]
    C --> C2[Small-Scale Fading]
    C1 --> C1a[Path Loss]
    C1 --> C1b[Shadowing Log-Normal]
    C2 --> C2a[Flat Fading]
    C2 --> C2b[Frequency-Selective Fading]
    C2a --> C2a1[Slow Fading]
    C2a --> C2a2[Fast Fading]
    C2b --> C2b1[Slow Fading]
    C2b --> C2b2[Fast Fading]
    C2a1 --> C2a1a[Rayleigh NLOS]
    C2a1 --> C2a1b[Rician LOS]
    D --> D1[Thermal AWGN]
    D --> D2[Quantization Noise]
```

## 4.2 Fading Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start Observed Signal] --> Q1{Compare B and Bc}
    Q1 -->|B less than Bc| R1[Flat Fading]
    Q1 -->|B greater than Bc| R2[Frequency Selective ISI]
    R1 --> Q2{Compare Ts and Tc}
    Q2 -->|Ts less than Tc| R3[Slow Flat Fading]
    Q2 -->|Ts greater than Tc| R4[Fast Flat Fading]
    R2 --> Q3{Compare Ts and Tc}
    Q3 -->|Ts less than Tc| R5[Slow Selective Fading]
    Q3 -->|Ts greater than Tc| R6[Fast Selective Fading]
    R3 --> Q4{LOS present?}
    Q4 -->|No| R7[Rayleigh Distribution]
    Q4 -->|Yes| R8[Rician K-Factor]
```

## 4.3 Link-Budget / Interference Mitigation Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source Bits] --> B[Channel Coding LDPC]
    B --> C[Interleaver]
    C --> D[Modulator BPSK QPSK]
    D --> E[Pulse Shaping RRC Filter]
    E --> F[Upconverter RF Frontend]
    F --> G[Wireless Channel]
    G --> H[Interference Sources]
    G --> I[Multipath Fading]
    G --> J[AWGN]
    H --> K[Receiver RF Frontend]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Matched Filter RRC]
    L --> M[Equalizer LMS DFE]
    M --> N[Demodulator]
    N --> O[De-interleaver]
    O --> P[Channel Decoder]
    P --> Q[Output Bits]
```

## 4.4 Diversity Combining Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transmitted Signal] --> B[Channel Branch 1 h1]
    A --> C[Channel Branch 2 h2]
    A --> D[Channel Branch L hL]
    B --> E[Receiver Antenna 1]
    C --> F[Receiver Antenna 2]
    D --> G[Receiver Antenna L]
    E --> H[Diversity Combiner]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> H1[Selection Combining SC]
    H --> H2[Equal Gain Combining EGC]
    H --> H3[Maximal Ratio Combining MRC]
    H1 --> I[Decision Output]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above follow a **block-level functional architecture** that maps how interference, fading, and noise enter the system and how diversity/equalization mitigate them. This is a higher-value schematic for KTU exam answers than attempting to draw a physical antenna radiation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between large-scale fading and small-scale fading. Give one example of each in a CPS context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Large-Scale Fading | Small-Scale Fading |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Spatial scale | 10s to 100s of metres | Few wavelengths (cm) |
| Cause | Path loss, shadowing by buildings/walls | Multipath, Doppler |
| Statistics | Distance-dependent mean + log-normal | Rayleigh, Rician |
| Time variation | Very slow | Fast (ms scale) |
| CPS example | Smart-meter signal strength varying as user walks from one room to another | Robot-to-controller packet errors within a single 50 cm motion due to multipath |

*(1 mark for each correct row, 1 mark for the example — KTU awards marks for tabulated comparison.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**A wireless link operates at 2.4 GHz. If the receiver moves at 60 km/h, compute the maximum Doppler shift. State the resulting coherence time and identify whether the channel is slow- or fast-fading for a 1 Mbps system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Maximum Doppler shift:
$$f_m = \frac{v f_c}{c} = \frac{(60 \times 1000/3600) \times 2.4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{16.67 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 133.3 \text{ Hz}$$

Coherence time:
$$T_c \approx \frac{0.423}{f_m} = \frac{0.423}{133.3} \approx 3.17 \text{ ms}$$

Symbol period at 1 Mbps: $T_s = 1\,\mu s$.

Since $T_s \ll T_c$ ($1\,\mu s \ll 3.17\,ms$), the channel is **slow fading**.

*(1 mark for $f_m$, 1 mark for $T_c$, 1 mark for classification.)*

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Rician probability density function** of the received envelope, starting from the in-phase and quadrature Gaussian model. Show clearly how the Rayleigh PDF is obtained as a special case. Define the Rician K-factor.

**(b) [7 marks]** For a Rayleigh fading channel with average SNR $\bar{\gamma} = 10$ dB, derive the **outage probability** for a target SNR threshold $\gamma_{th} = 5$ dB. If maximal-ratio combining (MRC) with $L = 2$ independent branches is used (each with the same $\bar{\gamma}$), show how the outage probability changes and comment on the diversity gain.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*(1.5 marks)* Express the LOS-modified in-phase and quadrature components:
$$I \sim \mathcal{N}(A, \sigma^{2}), \qquad Q \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$$

*(1.5 marks)* Write the joint PDF and convert to polar $(r, \phi)$ with Jacobian $r$.

*(2 marks)* Integrate out $\phi$ and recognize the Bessel function $I_0$:
$$f_r(r) = \frac{r}{\sigma^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}+A^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) I_{0}\!\left(\frac{Ar}{\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

*(1 mark)* Define K-factor: $K = A^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})$.

*(1 mark)* Special case $A \to 0$: $I_0(0) = 1$, $A^{2} \to 0$, recover Rayleigh.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark)* Convert: $\bar{\gamma}_{lin} = 10$ dB → $10$, $\gamma_{th,lin} = 5$ dB → $\approx 3.162$.

*(1.5 marks)* Write outage formula:
$$P_{out} = 1 - \exp(-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}) = 1 - \exp(-3.162/10) = 1 - \exp(-0.3162) \approx 0.271$$

*(2 marks)* For MRC with $L$ branches, the total SNR is the sum of $L$ i.i.d. exponentials → Gamma distribution with shape $L$:
$$P_{out}^{MRC} = 1 - e^{-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma}} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \frac{1}{k!} \left(\frac{\gamma_{th}}{\bar{\gamma}}\right)^{k}$$

For $L = 2$:
$$P_{out}^{MRC} = 1 - e^{-0.3162}(1 + 0.3162) \approx 1 - 0.729(1.316) \approx 1 - 0.959 = 0.041$$

*(1.5 marks)* Diversity gain analysis: outage reduced from 27.1% to 4.1% — about an **order-of-magnitude improvement** for only $L = 2$, demonstrating the multiplicative diversity gain of $\bar{\gamma}^{L}$ in the high-SNR regime.

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the necessary derivations, explain the **two-ray ground reflection model** for path loss. Show that at large distances, the path loss exponent tends to 4. Compare with the free-space Friis model.

**(b) [7 marks]** A CPS wireless link at 5.8 GHz is to be designed for a smart-factory robot. The transmit power is 10 dBm, transmit and receive antenna gains are 2 dBi each, and the robot-to-controller distance is 50 m. Calculate the received power using (i) free-space Friis model, (ii) two-ray model with $h_t = h_r = 1.5$ m. Assume isotropic antennas, no other loss.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*(1 mark)* Set up geometry: direct and reflected rays, image method.

*(1.5 marks)* Path difference $\Delta \approx 2 h_t h_r / d$.

*(1.5 marks)* Phase difference $\Delta\phi = 2\pi \Delta / \lambda = 4\pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d)$.

*(1.5 marks)* Field sum: $E \approx 2E_{dir} \sin(\Delta\phi/2)$.

*(1 mark)* For $d$ large, $\sin(\Delta\phi/2) \approx \Delta\phi/2$, leading to $E \propto 1/d^{2}$ and $P_r \propto 1/d^{4}$.

*(1 mark)* Compare with Friis: $n = 2$ in free space vs $n = 4$ in two-ray at long range; two-ray gives pessimistic LOS+ground estimate.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Parameters:
- $P_t = 10$ dBm = **10 mW = 0.01 W**
- $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi = **1.585** (linear)
- $f_c = 5.8$ GHz → $\lambda = c/f_c = 0.0517$ m
- $d = 50$ m

*(3 marks — Part i)*
$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} = 0.01 \times 1.585^{2} \times \left(\frac{0.0517}{4\pi \times 50}\right)^{2}$$
$$\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.0517}{628.32}\right)^{2} = (8.23 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 6.77 \times 10^{-9}$$
$$P_r = 0.01 \times 2.512 \times 6.77 \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} \approx -67.7 \text{ dBm}$$

*(3 marks — Part ii)* Two-ray:
$$P_r \approx P_t G_t G_r \frac{(h_t h_r)^{2}}{d^{4}} = 0.01 \times 2.512 \times \frac{(1.5 \times 1.5)^{2}}{50^{4}}$$
$$= 0.02512 \times \frac{5.0625}{6.25 \times 10^{6}} = 0.02512 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-7} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} \approx -46.9 \text{ dBm}$$

*(1 mark)* Comparison: two-ray predicts a **20.8 dB higher** received power because at 50 m the two-ray "breakpoint" hasn't yet entered the deep $d^{4}$ regime, and the geometry helps the ground reflection add constructively.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing linear and dB units** — never add dB and linear, never square dB values. Convert first, then operate.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ in Doppler** — $f_d = v f_c \cos\theta / c$, not $v f_c / c$. The cosine matters for the *instantaneous* Doppler.
> 3. **Confusing coherence bandwidth formulas** — there are two: $B_c \approx 1/(2\pi \sigma_\tau)$ (RMS delay spread) vs $B_c \approx 1/(5\sigma_\tau)$ (Jakes / 50% correlation). State the assumption.
> 4. **Stating "$n=4$ for two-ray" without the derivation** — marks are awarded for the geometric reasoning.
> 5. **Not specifying the channel's Rician-K value** when writing Rician PDF — examiners expect the relationship $K = A^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})$ explicitly.
> 6. **Confusing $T_c$ and $T_s$** in slow/fast fading classification — always state the *ratio* and compare.
> 7. **Forgetting Bessel function $I_0$** in Rician PDF — KTU deducts at least 1 mark for this in 14-mark questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Re-read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Interference types** — CCI, ACI, ISI. *Mitigations:* frequency planning, sharp filters, equalization, OFDM cyclic prefix.
- **Fading categories** — Large-scale (path loss $n=2$ to $6$, log-normal $X_\sigma$) vs Small-scale (multipath + Doppler).
- **Path loss models** — Friis ($n=2$), two-ray ($n=4$), log-distance (general $n$). Master the dB formula with the 32.44 constant.
- **Doppler relations** — $f_d = v f_c \cos\theta / c$, $f_m = v f_c / c$. Coherence time $T_c \approx 0.423 / f_m$.
- **Coherence bandwidth** — $B_c \approx 1/(2\pi \sigma_\tau)$. Flat fading if $B \ll B_c$; selective otherwise.
- **Slow vs fast fading** — Slow if $T_s \ll T_c$; fast if $T_s \gg T_c$.
- **Rayleigh PDF** — $f(r) = (r/\sigma^{2}) \exp(-r^{2}/2\sigma^{2})$, NLOS, central limit theorem argument.
- **Rician PDF** — adds LOS amplitude $A$; Rician-K = $A^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})$. Rayleigh is the special case $A \to 0$.
- **Outage probability** — $P_{out} = 1 - \exp(-\gamma_{th}/\bar{\gamma})$ for Rayleigh; MRC gives $L$-order diversity gain.
- **Diversity combining** — SC, EGC, MRC; MRC is optimal but requires channel knowledge.
- **CPS relevance** — 5G URLLC, V2V, NB-IoT, body-area networks all hinge on these models; KTU loves CPS-context questions.
- **Always state assumptions** — isotropic scattering (Jakes spectrum), 2-D vs 3-D multipath, equal-gain combining, Rayleigh vs Rician, AWGN co-presence.
- **Always convert units** — dB ↔ linear, dBm ↔ W, km ↔ m, GHz ↔ Hz, *before* substituting.
- **Common exam traps** — wrong Doppler cosine, missing Bessel $I_0$, $n=2$ vs $n=4$ confusion, forgetting that shadowing is log-normal (not Rayleigh).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wired Communication for CPS: Wired Communication Technologies (Ethernet)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wired Communication for CPS: Wired Communication Technologies (Ethernet)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)** is the predominant family of wired Local Area Network (LAN) technologies that define the physical layer (PHY) and the Media Access Control (MAC) sub-layer of the Data Link Layer for connecting computing devices in a wired infrastructure. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, Ethernet has evolved from a best-effort office networking protocol into a deterministic, real-time industrial communication backbone through enhanced variants collectively referred to as **Industrial Ethernet**.

In simple KTU examiner language:
> Ethernet is a set of rules (protocols) that decide **how bits are physically transmitted over a cable** and **how multiple devices share that cable without colliding**.

The relevant IEEE standard family is **IEEE 802.3**, which the KTU syllabus specifically associates with CPS wired infrastructure alongside CAN, RS-485, and Modbus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Conference Room Telephone Line**

Imagine a single shared telephone line (a coaxial cable or twisted-pair bus) running through a conference hall. Many people (nodes/sensors/PLCs) want to talk, but only one can speak clearly at a time. The rule used is: *"Before you speak, listen to the line. If it is silent, start talking. If two people accidentally start at once, both stop, wait a random short time, and try again."*

This listening-before-talking rule is the heart of Ethernet's **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) protocol. Modern switched Ethernet (used in CPS) replaces this with a *private telephone per person* — each device gets a dedicated full-duplex lane to the switch, eliminating collisions entirely.

### 1.3 Why Ethernet is Critical for CPS

| CPS Requirement | Ethernet's Role |
|-----------------|-----------------|
| High bandwidth (video, sensor fusion) | Provides 10 Mbps to 100 Gbps |
| Determinism (bounded latency) | Industrial Ethernet / TSN adds guarantees |
| Long cable runs | Up to **100 m** per twisted-pair segment |
| Interoperability | Universal IP-based stack integration |
| Reliability | Mature, well-tested, replaceable parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember for KTU:**
> - Minimum Ethernet frame size: **64 bytes**
> - Maximum Ethernet frame size: **1518 bytes**
> - Maximum cable length (twisted pair): **100 metres**
> - Speed grades: **10 / 100 / 1000 / 10000 Mbps**
> - MAC address length: **48 bits** (6 bytes)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IEEE 802.3 / Ethernet Architecture (OSI Mapping)

Ethernet operates strictly at the **bottom two layers** of the OSI model:

| OSI Layer | Ethernet Equivalent | Function |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| Layer 7 – Application | (Not part of Ethernet) | User software |
| Layer 4 – Transport | (Not part of Ethernet) | TCP/UDP |
| Layer 3 – Network | (Not part of Ethernet) | IP |
| **Layer 2 – Data Link** | **LLC (802.2) + MAC (802.3)** | Framing, addressing, access |
| **Layer 1 – Physical** | **PHY (802.3)** | Electrical signalling, cable |

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **MAC sub-layer** (framing and CSMA/CD) and the **PHY** (cable, signalling, speeds).

### 2.2 Ethernet Frame Structure (IEEE 802.3)

This is the highest-yield topic for KTU. Every examiner asks a draw-and-label question.

```
| Preamble | SFD | Destination MAC | Source MAC | Length/Type | Payload | FCS |
| 7 bytes  | 1 B |     6 bytes     |  6 bytes   |  2 bytes    | 46-1500 | 4 B |
```

- **Preamble (7 bytes)**: Alternating `10101010...` pattern used by the receiver to synchronize its clock with the sender's clock (clock recovery / bit synchronization).
- **Start Frame Delimiter (SFD) (1 byte)**: Always `10101011`. Marks the end of preamble and the start of the actual frame.
- **Destination MAC Address (6 bytes)**: Hardware address of the recipient. First 24 bits = OUI (manufacturer ID), last 24 bits = device serial.
- **Source MAC Address (6 bytes)**: Sender's hardware address.
- **Length/Type (2 bytes)**: If value ≤ 1500 → length of payload in bytes; if ≥ 1536 (0x0600) → upper-layer protocol (e.g., `0x0800` = IPv4).
- **Payload (46 – 1500 bytes)**: Actual data. Minimum 46 bytes because if data is shorter, padding is added to reach the minimum 64-byte total frame size (slot time requirement for collision detection).
- **FCS – Frame Check Sequence (4 bytes)**: **CRC-32** (Cyclic Redundancy Check) for error detection across DA + SA + Length + Payload.

### 2.3 Minimum and Maximum Frame Size Logic (Why 64 Bytes?)

In half-duplex CSMA/CD, the smallest frame must be transmitted for a duration **at least equal to the Round-Trip Time (RTT)** of the network, so the sender is still transmitting when a collision at the far end propagates back.

- Network maximum length = **2500 metres** (5 repeaters, 4 segments) → RTT ≈ **51.2 µs**
- At **10 Mbps**, bits in 51.2 µs = **512 bits = 64 bytes**

Therefore, the minimum frame size is fixed at **64 bytes** (header + payload + FCS), ensuring collision fragments are detected.

### 2.4 CSMA/CD Operational Steps (Half-Duplex)

1. **Carrier Sense**: Listen to the medium.
2. **If idle** → start transmitting.
3. **If busy** → wait until idle, then wait an **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** of **96 bit-times**.
4. **Collision Detection**: If voltage on the cable exceeds the normal swing, a collision is happening.
5. **Jam Signal**: Transmit a 32-bit jam pattern to ensure all stations detect the collision.
6. **Backoff**: Wait a random number of slot times. The slot time at 10 Mbps = **51.2 µs** (= 512 bit-times).
7. **Exponential Backoff**: After the *n*-th collision (up to 16), wait $k \times 51.2\ \mu s$ where:
$$k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, (2^{\min(n,10)} - 1)\}$$
8. After 16 collisions, the frame is dropped and an error is reported to upper layers.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Unit / Value |
|---------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Slot Time (10 Mbps) | $\tau_{slot}$ | **51.2 µs** (= 512 bit-times) |
| Minimum Frame Size | $L_{min}$ | **64 bytes** = 512 bits |
| Maximum Frame Size | $L_{max}$ | **1518 bytes** |
| Inter-Frame Gap | $T_{IFG}$ | **96 bit-times** |
| Jam Signal | $L_{jam}$ | **32 bits** |
| Network propagation (max) | $t_{prop}$ | **25.6 µs** one way |
| Ethernet efficiency (CSMA/CD) | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + 2t_{prop}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{L \times 8}{T_{frame}}$ | bits per second |
| Bit time at rate $R$ | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds per bit |
| Max cable run (UTP) | Physical limit | **100 m** |
| MAC address size | $6 \times 8$ | **48 bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Ethernet efficiency $\eta$ tells a CPS designer how much of the nominal 10/100 Mbps is actually usable after accounting for propagation delays — a critical parameter when designing hard real-time control loops over legacy shared Ethernet.

### 2.6 Ethernet Speed Standards and Physical Media

| Standard | Speed | Cable | Max Segment | Signalling |
|----------|-------|-------|-------------|------------|
| **10BASE-5** | 10 Mbps | Thick Coax | 500 m | Manchester |
| **10BASE-2** | 10 Mbps | Thin Coax | 185 m | Manchester |
| **10BASE-T** | 10 Mbps | UTP Cat-3 | 100 m | Manchester |
| **100BASE-TX** (Fast Ethernet) | 100 Mbps | UTP Cat-5 | 100 m | 4B/5B + MLT-3 |
| **1000BASE-T** (Gigabit) | 1 Gbps | UTP Cat-5e/6 | 100 m | PAM-5, all 4 pairs |
| **10GBASE-T** | 10 Gbps | UTP Cat-6a/7 | 100 m | PAM-16 |
| **100BASE-FX** | 100 Mbps | Multimode fibre | 2 km | Optical |
| **1000BASE-LX** | 1 Gbps | Single-mode fibre | 10 km | Optical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favourite question**: *"Why did 100BASE-TX move from Manchester to 4B/5B + MLT-3?"* — Manchester coding has 100 % overhead (1 bit per 1 bit). 4B/5B encodes 4 data bits into 5 code bits (80 % efficiency), and MLT-3 uses 3 voltage levels at half the fundamental frequency, reducing EMI — crucial for noisy industrial CPS environments.

### 2.7 Ethernet Topologies for CPS

| Topology | Used In CPS? | Notes |
|----------|---------------|-------|
| **Bus (coax, 10BASE-2/5)** | Legacy | Single cable, vulnerable to single-point failure |
| **Star (twisted pair + hub/switch)** | ✅ Most common today | Each device has dedicated link to a central switch |
| **Tree / Hierarchical Star** | ✅ Industrial plants | Layer of switches (ring topology also used for redundancy) |
| **Ring (with switches)** | ✅ Critical CPS (e.g., EtherCAT) | Provides redundancy — two paths for every packet |

### 2.8 Switched Ethernet and the Move from CSMA/CD

Modern CPS deployments use **full-duplex switched Ethernet**. Each device has a dedicated transmit and receive pair to the switch. There are no collisions, so **CSMA/CD is disabled**. The switch learns MAC addresses by inspecting incoming frames and builds a **MAC address table** (CAM table), forwarding frames only to the correct output port — this is the foundation for **micro-segmentation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Frame Size (64 Bytes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a high-yield KTU question (Part A 3-mark or sub-part of 14-mark questions).

**Given:**

- Maximum network length (5 segments, 4 repeaters) = **2500 m**
- Signal propagation speed in copper ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (≈ 2/3 speed of light)
- Ethernet bit rate (original) $R$ = **10 Mbps**

**Step 1: Calculate one-way propagation delay.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{prop} &= \frac{d}{v} \\
&= \frac{2500\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} \\
&= 12.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\
&= 12.5\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **one-way** propagation delay. The sender must still be transmitting when a collision at the far end propagates back, so we need **2 × one-way** time.

**Step 2: Compute Round-Trip Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{RTT} &= 2 \times t_{prop} \\
&= 2 \times 12.5\ \mu s \\
&= 25\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Add repeater and other internal delays.**

IEEE 802.3 budget allocates an extra ~25.6 µs for repeater latency, transceiver turnaround, etc., totalling approximately **51.2 µs** — the famous **slot time**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{slot} &= 51.2\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Convert slot time into bits at 10 Mbps.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min} &= R \times \tau_{slot} \\
&= 10 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits/s} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\
&= 512\ \text{bits} \\
&= 64\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum IEEE 802.3 frame is **64 bytes** to ensure a sender is still on the wire long enough to detect a collision at the farthest station.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Ethernet Channel Efficiency

**Definition:** Efficiency $\eta$ is the fraction of time the channel carries successful data vs. total time on the medium.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\text{Frame transmission time}}{\text{Frame transmission time} + \text{Round-trip propagation delay}} \\
\eta &= \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + 2t_{prop}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{L_{frame} \times 8}{R}
$$

**Step-by-step algebraic simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{L_{frame} \times 8 / R}{L_{frame} \times 8 / R + 2t_{prop}} \\
\eta &= \frac{8 L_{frame}}{8 L_{frame} + 2R \cdot t_{prop}} \\
\eta &= \frac{4 L_{frame}}{4 L_{frame} + R \cdot t_{prop}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** For a **1500-byte** frame at **10 Mbps** with **25.6 µs** total one-way slot (treating $t_{prop} = \tau_{slot}/2$ but using full $\tau_{slot}$ for collision window):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{1500 \times 8}{10 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{12000}{10^{7}} \\
&= 1200\ \mu s \\
\eta &= \frac{1200}{1200 + 51.2} \\
&= \frac{1200}{1251.2} \\
&\approx 0.959 = 95.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For a minimum **64-byte** frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= \frac{64 \times 8}{10^{7}} = 51.2\ \mu s \\
\eta &= \frac{51.2}{51.2 + 51.2} = 0.5 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation**: Smaller frames yield lower efficiency — this is why CPS payloads aggregate small sensor readings (e.g., EtherCAT process data) into a single frame.

---

### 3.3 Backoff Algorithm Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

A station on a 10 Mbps Ethernet attempts to transmit and experiences **3 consecutive collisions**.

**Question:** What is the range of possible backoff delays (in slot times and in µs)?

**Step 1:** Recall the formula. After the $n$-th collision, the backoff counter $k$ is chosen uniformly at random from:

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, (2^{\min(n,10)} - 1)\}
$$

**Step 2:** With $n = 3$:

$$
2^{\min(3,10)} - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7
$$

So $k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$.

**Step 3:** Convert to time using slot time 51.2 µs:

$$
\text{Backoff} = k \times \tau_{slot} = k \times 51.2\ \mu s
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min backoff} &= 0 \times 51.2 = 0\ \mu s \\
\text{Max backoff} &= 7 \times 51.2 = 358.4\ \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Between 0 and 358.4 µs, in steps of 51.2 µs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ethernet Variants for CPS

| Variant | Cyclic Time | Jitter | Use Case | Deterministic? |
|---------|-------------|--------|----------|----------------|
| Standard 10/100 Ethernet | Variable | High | Office / monitoring | ❌ No |
| Switched full-duplex Ethernet | Lower variance | Low | Distributed I/O | Partial |
| **EtherCAT** | ≤ 100 µs | < 1 µs | Motion control | ✅ Yes |
| **PROFINET IRT** | ~1 ms | < 1 µs | CNC, robotics | ✅ Yes |
| **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking, IEEE 802.1Qbv)** | Scheduled | Sub-µs | Smart grid, automotive | ✅ Yes |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Application-level | Medium | PLC-to-PLC | Partial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802.3 Ethernet Frame Structure (Bit-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Preamble 7 bytes] --> B[SFD 1 byte 10101011]
    B --> C[Destination MAC 6 bytes]
    C --> D[Source MAC 6 bytes]
    D --> E[Length or Type 2 bytes]
    E --> F[Payload 46 to 1500 bytes]
    F --> G[FCS CRC32 4 bytes]
```

### 4.2 CSMA/CD State Machine (Collision Handling Flow)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing: Carrier Sense
    Sensing --> Transmit: Channel Idle
    Sensing --> WaitIFG: Channel Busy
    WaitIFG --> Idle: After 96 bit-times
    Transmit --> Monitoring: Listen while sending
    Monitoring --> Transmit: No collision detected
    Monitoring --> Jam: Collision detected
    Jam --> Backoff: Send 32 bit jam signal
    Backoff --> Idle: After random k x slot time
    Backoff --> Dropped: After 16 collisions
    Dropped: Report error to upper layer
```

### 4.3 Switched Ethernet in a CPS Plant (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPS_Plant[CPS Plant Network]
        PLC1[PLC Controller 1]
        PLC2[PLC Controller 2]
        S1[Sensor Array Temperature]
        S2[Sensor Array Pressure]
        S3[Vision Camera HD]
        S4[Motor Drive Servo]
        HMI[Operator HMI Console]
    end

    SW_Core[Industrial Ethernet Switch Managed TSN]

    SCADA[SCADA Server Plant Floor]

    CLOUD[Cloud Analytics Gateway]

    PLC1 --> SW_Core
    PLC2 --> SW_Core
    S1 --> SW_Core
    S2 --> SW_Core
    S3 --> SW_Core
    S4 --> SW_Core
    HMI --> SW_Core

    SW_Core --> SCADA
    SCADA --> CLOUD
```

### 4.4 CSMA/CD Collision Domain vs Switched Micro-Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Legacy_Hub[Legacy Hub Network Single Collision Domain]
        A1[Node A] --- HUB[Hub]
        A2[Node B] --- HUB
        A3[Node C] --- HUB
        A4[Node D] --- HUB
    end

    subgraph Switched_Network[Switched Ethernet No Collisions Full Duplex]
        B1[Node A] --- SW[Switch]
        B2[Node B] --- SW
        B3[Node C] --- SW
        B4[Node D] --- SW
    end
```

### 4.5 Physical Medium Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PHY[Ethernet Physical Layer IEEE 802.3] --> COAX[Coaxial 10BASE2 10BASE5]
    PHY --> COPPER[Twisted Pair UTP STP Cat3 to Cat7]
    PHY --> FIBRE[Optical Fibre Multimode Single Mode]
    COPPER --> FAST[100BASE TX Cat5]
    COPPER --> GIG[1000BASE T Cat5e Cat6]
    COPPER --> TEN[10GBASE T Cat6a Cat7]
    FIBRE --> F1[100BASE FX]
    FIBRE --> F2[1000BASE LX]
    FIBRE --> F3[10GBASE LR]
```

### 4.6 Industrial Ethernet Stack (How CPS Sits on Ethernet)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1[Application Motion Control PLC Logic]
    APP2[Application Process Data Diagnostics]
    APP3[Application Safety IEC 61508 SIL]

    REAL[Real Time Layer PROFINET EtherCAT EtherNet/IP]
    IPV[Standard TCP or UDP over IPv4 IPv6]
    ETH[MAC IEEE 802.3]
    PHY1[PHY Ethernet 100 Mbps 1 Gbps]

    APP1 --> REAL
    APP2 --> IPV
    APP3 --> REAL
    REAL --> IPV
    IPV --> ETH
    ETH --> PHY1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *List the fields of the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame and state the purpose of the Preamble.*

**Model Answer:**
The IEEE 802.3 frame contains: Preamble (7 bytes), Start Frame Delimiter (1 byte), Destination MAC Address (6 bytes), Source MAC Address (6 bytes), Length/Type (2 bytes), Payload (46–1500 bytes) and Frame Check Sequence/CRC-32 (4 bytes).
The **Preamble** is a 7-byte alternating pattern `10101010…` used by the receiver's clock-recovery circuit to **synchronize its bit clock** with the sender's bit stream before the actual frame begins. **(3 marks)**

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *Why is the minimum Ethernet frame size fixed at 64 bytes?*

**Model Answer:**
The minimum frame size of 64 bytes equals 512 bit-times at 10 Mbps (slot time = 51.2 µs). This guarantees that a station is still transmitting when a collision signal returns from the farthest point of the maximum-length network (2500 m). If the frame were shorter, the sender would finish before knowing about the collision, and CSMA/CD could not detect it. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A** [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]** *Ethernet in CPS.*

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format**. List the function of each field. What is the role of the **FCS** field and which polynomial is used for it?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **CSMA/CD protocol** with a flowchart. A station experiences **4 collisions** while trying to transmit on a 10 Mbps Ethernet. Compute the **range of backoff delays** (in slot times and in microseconds) it must wait before its 5th attempt.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Frame Format — Draw the seven fields in order with their byte widths:
- **Preamble (7 B)** — bit synchronisation.
- **SFD (1 B)** — `10101011` indicates frame start.
- **DA (6 B)** — destination MAC.
- **SA (6 B)** — source MAC.
- **Length/Type (2 B)** — payload length or upper-layer protocol.
- **Payload (46–1500 B)** — encapsulated data, padded to ≥ 46 B.
- **FCS (4 B)** — CRC-32 (generator polynomial $G(x) = x^{32} + x^{26} + x^{23} + x^{22} + x^{16} + x^{12} + x^{11} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{5} + x^{4} + x^{2} + x + 1$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** CSMA/CD steps — listen, transmit if idle, detect collision, jam, backoff. After the $n$-th collision the backoff counter $k$ is uniform on $\{0,\dots,2^{\min(n,10)}-1\}$.

For $n=4$: $2^{4}-1 = 15$, so $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 15\}$.

Slot time at 10 Mbps = **51.2 µs**.

Backoff = $k \times 51.2\ \mu s$, so:
- Minimum = $0 \times 51.2 = 0\ \mu s$
- Maximum = $15 \times 51.2 = 768\ \mu s$

**Range = 0 to 768 µs, in 51.2 µs steps (16 possible values).** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B** [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]** *Wired Ethernet Backbone for CPS.*

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the performance of **standard shared Ethernet** using CSMA/CD with **switched full-duplex Ethernet**. Why is full-duplex switching essential for time-critical CPS applications? Mention at least three reasons.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The maximum length of an Ethernet LAN is 2500 m and the signal propagates at $2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$. If the bit rate is 10 Mbps, derive:
(i) the one-way propagation delay,
(ii) the slot time (RTT + repeater latency),
(iii) the minimum frame size in bytes,
(iv) the channel efficiency for transmitting a 1500-byte frame.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Comparison:

| Parameter | Shared Ethernet (CSMA/CD) | Switched Full-Duplex |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Collisions | Yes | None |
| Throughput | Degrades with load | Full duplex = 2× |
| Latency | Variable, unbounded | Bounded |
| Cabling | Single bus | Star to switch |
| Determinism for CPS | Poor | Good (with TSN: excellent) |

Three reasons full-duplex switching is essential for CPS:
1. **Collision-free** → no retransmission storms → bounded latency.
2. **Dedicated bandwidth per port** → predictable throughput for sensors.
3. **Micro-segmentation** isolates faults, vital for safety-critical CPS.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derivations:

**(i)** One-way propagation delay:
$$
t_{prop} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 12.5\ \mu s
$$

**(ii)** Slot time (round trip + repeater budget, IEEE 802.3 specifies 51.2 µs total):
$$
\tau_{slot} \approx 51.2\ \mu s
$$

**(iii)** Minimum frame size:
$$
L_{min} = 10 \times 10^{6} \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6} = 512\ \text{bits} = 64\ \text{bytes}
$$

**(iv)** Channel efficiency for 1500 B frame:
$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1500 \times 8}{10^{7}} = 1200\ \mu s
$$
$$
\eta = \frac{1200}{1200 + 51.2} \approx 0.959 = 95.9\%
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps (read carefully):**
> 1. **Frame size units**: students write "64 bits" instead of "64 bytes". The unit is **bytes**.
> 2. **Backoff formula confusion**: For the *n*-th collision the range is $\{0,\dots,2^{\min(n,10)}-1\}$, **not** $\{1,\dots,2^n\}$. Off-by-one costs marks.
> 3. **Preamble ≠ Header**: Preamble (7 B) and SFD (1 B) are **NOT counted** in the 64-byte minimum frame size. The 64 B includes DA + SA + Length + Payload + FCS = 6+6+2+46+4.
> 4. **FCS polynomial**: Writing "CRC-16" or "checksum" loses marks. It is **CRC-32** with the IEEE 802.3 generator polynomial.
> 5. **CSMA/CD on full-duplex**: A surprising number of students claim CSMA/CD is still active on switched Ethernet. CSMA/CD is **disabled** in full-duplex links.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ethernet is defined by **IEEE 802.3** and covers the **MAC + PHY** layers.
- Frame = **Preamble (7 B) + SFD (1 B) + DA (6 B) + SA (6 B) + Len/Type (2 B) + Payload (46–1500 B) + FCS (4 B)**.
- **Min frame = 64 B**, **Max frame = 1518 B**, **MAC = 48 bits**.
- **Slot time = 51.2 µs** at 10 Mbps, derived from max propagation across 2500 m.
- **CSMA/CD** uses **1-persistent** sensing, jam signal of **32 bits**, IFG of **96 bit-times**, and **truncated binary exponential backoff** $k \times 51.2\ \mu s$, max 16 collisions.
- **Full-duplex switched Ethernet** = collision-free, mandatory for deterministic CPS.
- Industrial Ethernet variants: **EtherCAT, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, TSN (IEEE 802.1Qbv)**.
- Maximum cable length per UTP segment = **100 m**.
- Manchester coding (10 Mbps) → 4B/5B + MLT-3 (100 Mbps) → PAM-5 (1 Gbps) → PAM-16 (10 Gbps).
- For CPS, **switched > shared**, **full-duplex > half-duplex**, **TSN > best-effort**.
- Efficiency formula: $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + 2t_{prop}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wired Network Topologies and Standards (TCP/IP, Modbus)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wired Network Topologies and Standards (TCP/IP, Modbus)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wired Network Topologies in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Wired Network Topology** refers to the predefined geometric and logical arrangement of nodes (sensors, actuators, PLCs, controllers, and servers) connected through physical transmission media (copper twisted-pair, coaxial cable, or fiber-optic strands) within a Cyber-Physical System. In CPS, the choice of topology directly determines **determinism**, **latency**, **fault-tolerance**, and **scalability** of real-time control loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> "Wired network topologies define the physical and logical interconnection patterns of CPS devices, while communication standards such as TCP/IP and Modbus provide the rules, frame formats, and addressing schemes that govern data exchange between field devices, controllers, and supervisory systems."

### Conceptual Analogy: The City Road Network

Imagine a city where:
- **Houses** = CPS field devices (sensors, actuators)
- **Roads** = Cables (Ethernet, RS-485, fiber)
- **Traffic rules** = Protocols (TCP/IP, Modbus)
- **Postal addresses** = IP/MAC addresses
- **Mail format** = Frame structure

In a **Bus topology**, every house sits along one long single road — cheap, but if the road is blocked at one point, the entire colony is cut off. In a **Star topology**, every house connects to a central post office — easy to manage, but if the post office shuts down, no one sends mail. In a **Mesh topology**, every house has multiple direct roads to many other houses — expensive, but extremely robust (used in smart grid and industrial CPS).

## 1.2 TCP/IP Protocol Suite — The "Universal Postal Service"

**TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** is the foundational 4-layer networking protocol stack that enables reliable, routed, and interoperable data communication across heterogeneous wired and wireless media. In CPS, TCP/IP serves as the **backbone protocol** for SCADA, IIoT gateways, cloud telemetry, and supervisory-control data exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> TCP/IP is not a single protocol — it is a **suite** of protocols organized in **4 abstraction layers** (Link, Internet, Transport, Application), each providing well-defined services to the layer above.

**Intuition:** Think of TCP/IP like a **4-tier courier service**:
1. The **truck driver** (Link layer) carries the parcel on the local road.
2. The **highway system** (Internet layer / IP) routes it across cities using postal codes.
3. The **delivery confirmation** (Transport layer / TCP) ensures it arrives intact and in order.
4. The **recipient's mailbox** (Application layer / HTTP, Modbus-TCP) interprets the contents.

## 1.3 Modbus Protocol — The "Industrial Common Language"

**Modbus** is an open, royalty-free, master-slave (client-server) application-layer messaging protocol originally published by **Modicon (Schneider Electric)** in **1979** for communicating with PLCs. It has become the **de facto standard** for industrial CPS due to its simplicity, openness, and ease of deployment on legacy serial media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three official Modbus variants are recognized:
> - **Modbus RTU** — Binary, compact, used over RS-232/RS-485 (most common in field).
> - **Modbus ASCII** — Human-readable, slower, used for debugging and legacy systems.
> - **Modbus TCP/IP** (or Modbus TCP) — Encapsulated over TCP port **502**, used in Ethernet-based industrial networks.

**Intuition:** Modbus is like a **structured telephone conversation** between a plant manager (master) and machine operators (slaves). The manager always initiates, says a fixed-format sentence ("Read register 40001 of device #5"), and the operator responds with a structured reply. The conversation follows **strict grammar** (function codes) and uses a **checksum** (CRC or LRC) to catch transmission errors.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Performance Metrics in Wired CPS

The following quantitative parameters are critical to all topology/protocol decisions:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical CPS Range | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | $B$ | 10 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Maximum data rate (bits/sec) |
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_p$ | 5 ns/m (copper), 5 ns/m (fiber) | Time for signal to traverse medium |
| **Throughput** | $\eta$ | 60–95% of $B$ | Effective useful data rate |
| **Jitter** | $\sigma_J$ | < 1 ms (control) | Variation in packet delay |
| **Bit Error Rate** | $BER$ | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-12}$ | Probability of bit corruption |
| **Maximum Node Count** | $N_{max}$ | 32 – 247 (Modbus) | Devices per bus segment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these matter in CPS:** A robotic arm control loop may require a deterministic cycle time of **4 ms** (250 Hz). Any topology or protocol that introduces **> 4 ms** latency or **> 0.5 ms jitter** is unsuitable for that control loop — a hard real-time constraint.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Wired Network Topologies

### 2.1.1 Bus Topology (Linear Bus)

**Definition:** All nodes share a single common transmission medium (a "bus" or "backbone"). Data transmitted by any node propagates in both directions and is received by every other node.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Node wishing to transmit senses the medium (CSMA/CD-like behavior in Ethernet).
2. If idle, it broadcasts the frame onto the backbone.
3. All nodes examine the destination address; only the target node accepts the frame.
4. Terminators (typically 120 Ω resistors) at each end absorb signals to prevent reflection.

**Pros:** Lowest cable cost, simple to extend (with repeaters), minimal hardware.
**Cons:** Single point of failure (cable break = total network down), performance degrades under heavy load, difficult to fault-isolate, limited cable length (~185 m for 10BASE-2 coax; ~100 m for 10BASE-T with repeaters).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic Bus Example in CPS:** The legacy **10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5 Ethernet** backbone, and the **RS-485 multi-drop** Modbus network used in SCADA RTUs.

### 2.1.2 Star Topology

**Definition:** Every node connects to a central hub or switch via a dedicated point-to-point link. All communication passes through the central device.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Source node sends a frame to the central switch.
2. Switch reads the destination MAC address and forwards the frame out the correct port.
3. With a hub (Layer-1 device), the frame is broadcast to all ports; with a switch (Layer-2), it is forwarded selectively.
4. Destination node receives and processes the frame.

**Pros:** Easy installation and troubleshooting, one node failure does not affect others, easy to add/remove nodes, modern Ethernet switches provide full bandwidth per port.
**Cons:** Central device is a single point of failure (mitigated by managed switches, redundant cores), more cable required (one run per node), max cable length per link (~100 m for Cat5e/6 copper).

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS Application:** The dominant topology in **control cabinets and PLC networks** (e.g., Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix star with managed Ethernet switches).

### 2.1.3 Ring Topology

**Definition:** Each node connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Data travels in one direction (or both, in dual-ring) from node to node.

**Operational Steps:**
1. A "token" circulates around the ring.
2. Only the node holding the token may transmit.
3. The frame circulates until it reaches the destination, which acknowledges and forwards it.
4. Token returns to the sender; sender releases it for the next node.

**Pros:** Deterministic access (predictable latency), no collisions, suitable for real-time control, performs well under heavy load.
**Cons:** Single ring = single point of failure (mitigated by **dual-ring** with counter-rotating paths, e.g., **Token Ring**, **FDDI**, **Resilient Ethernet Protocol – REP**), complex reconfiguration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPS Standard:** **PROFIBUS** (Process Field Bus) and **Media Redundancy Protocol (MRP)** in industrial Ethernet use ring topologies for substation and factory automation.

### 2.1.4 Mesh Topology

**Definition:** Every node connects to multiple (partial mesh) or all (full mesh) other nodes, providing redundant paths.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Routing protocols (e.g., **OSPF**, **RSTP**, proprietary industrial variants) discover all available paths.
2. When a link fails, traffic is automatically re-routed via a backup path in < 50 ms (with RSTP).
3. Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple paths.

**Pros:** Highest fault-tolerance, no single point of failure, optimal path selection, supports load balancing.
**Cons:** Highest cost (cables and ports = $O(N^2)$ for full mesh), complex configuration, increased power/space.

**Cabling cost formula:**

$$C_{mesh} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \cdot c_{link}$$

where $N$ = number of nodes, $c_{link}$ = cost per dedicated link. For $N=10$, full mesh needs **45 links**.

### 2.1.5 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology

**Definition:** A hybrid of bus and star — groups of star-configured nodes connect to a central backbone bus. Common in **process plants** where field devices are clustered around junction boxes.

### 2.1.6 Hybrid Topology

**Definition:** Combines two or more of the above. Example: a **field-level ring** (PROFIBUS) connecting to a **control-level star** (industrial Ethernet) — a "ring of stars."

## 2.2 TCP/IP 4-Layer Model — Deep Functional Analysis

The TCP/IP model (RFC 1122, RFC 1123) is the practical realization of network communication for CPS backbones.

| Layer | PDU Name | Key Protocols | Core Function | CPS Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **4 — Application** | Message / Data | HTTP, MQTT, CoAP, Modbus TCP, OPC UA, SNMP, FTP, DNS, SSH | End-user services, semantic meaning of data | SCADA HMI, cloud telemetry, alarm notifications |
| **3 — Transport** | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | **TCP** (reliable, connection-oriented), **UDP** (best-effort, low-latency) | End-to-end reliability, port multiplexing, flow control | TCP for file/recipe download; UDP for time-critical sensor streams |
| **2 — Internet (Network)** | Packet / Datagram | **IP (IPv4/IPv6)**, ICMP, ARP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP | Logical addressing, routing across networks | Interfacing field gateways to enterprise/cloud |
| **1 — Link (Network Access)** | Frame | Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, RS-485, Fiber Channel | Framing, MAC addressing, physical signaling | Physical medium: copper, fiber, serial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The TCP/IP model has **4 layers** (not 7 like OSI). The OSI layers 5, 6, 7 (Session, Presentation, Application) are folded into the TCP/IP **Application layer**.

### 2.2.1 Detailed Role of TCP vs. UDP in CPS

| Property | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | ACK-based retransmission, in-order delivery | No guarantee |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Flow/Congestion Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Latency | Higher (acks, retransmits) | Very low |
| CPS Use Case | Firmware download, configuration upload, OPC UA | Real-time sensor streaming, NTP, multicast sensor data, RTP for video |

**TCP 3-Way Handshake (must-know):**
1. Client → Server: `SYN` (seq = x)
2. Server → Client: `SYN + ACK` (seq = y, ack = x+1)
3. Client → Server: `ACK` (seq = x+1, ack = y+1)

After this, the connection is **ESTABLISHED** and data transfer begins.

## 2.3 Modbus Protocol — Deep Architecture

### 2.3.1 Modbus Data Model

Modbus uses four primary tables, each holding **16-bit registers**:

| Table | Type | Access | Address Range | Typical CPS Content |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coils** | 1-bit (Boolean) | Read/Write | 00001 – 09999 | Digital outputs (relays, motor starters) |
| **Discrete Inputs** | 1-bit (Boolean) | Read-Only | 10001 – 19999 | Digital inputs (limit switches, status) |
| **Input Registers** | 16-bit (Word) | Read-Only | 30001 – 39999 | Analog inputs (temperature, pressure) |
| **Holding Registers** | 16-bit (Word) | Read/Write | 40001 – 49999 | Setpoints, calibration, configuration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** = Function Code (1 byte) + Data (N bytes)
> **Application Data Unit (ADU)** = Address (1 byte) + PDU + Error Check (2 bytes for RTU, 0 for TCP)

### 2.3.2 Modbus RTU Frame Format (Serial, RS-485)

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Slave Address** | 1 byte | 1–247 (0 = broadcast, 248–255 reserved) |
| **Function Code** | 1 byte | 1–127 valid, 128–255 = exception codes |
| **Data** | N bytes | Depends on function code |
| **CRC-16** | 2 bytes | Cyclic Redundancy Check, little-endian |

**Frame time:** Inter-frame silence must be ≥ **3.5 character times** (silent interval). At 9600 bps with 11 bits/char, this is ~**4 ms**.

### 2.3.3 Modbus TCP Frame (MBAP Header)

Modbus TCP uses a **7-byte MBAP (Modbus Application) Header** + Function Code + Data:

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | For matching request/response |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | Always 0x0000 for Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | Remaining bytes in frame |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | Same as RTU slave address (for bridging) |
| Function Code | 1 byte | Same as RTU |
| Data | N bytes | Same as RTU |

**Port:** TCP **502** is the reserved IANA port for Modbus.

### 2.3.4 Common Modbus Function Codes (Must Memorize)

| FC (dec) | Name | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 01 | Read Coils | Read 1–2000 contiguous coil status |
| 02 | Read Discrete Inputs | Read 1–2000 DI status |
| 03 | Read Holding Registers | Read 1–125 holding regs |
| 04 | Read Input Registers | Read 1–125 input regs |
| 05 | Write Single Coil | Force a single coil on/off |
| 06 | Write Single Register | Write a single 16-bit holding reg |
| 15 (0x0F) | Write Multiple Coils | Force N coils |
| 16 (0x10) | Write Multiple Registers | Write N holding regs |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_p = d / v_p$ | $d$ = distance (m), $v_p$ ≈ 2×10⁸ m/s (copper) | Propagation delay |
| 2 | $B \cdot t_{frame} = L$ | $B$ = bps, $t_{frame}$ = s, $L$ = bits | Frame transmission time |
| 3 | $RTT = 2 t_p + t_{proc}$ | Round-trip time | TCP timeout setting |
| 4 | $BDP = B \cdot RTT$ | Bandwidth-delay product (bits) | TCP window sizing |
| 5 | $\eta = L_{payload} / L_{frame}$ | Throughput efficiency (0–1) | Protocol overhead |
| 6 | $T_{cycle} = N \cdot t_{frame} + N \cdot t_{gap}$ | Modbus master poll cycle | Worst-case latency |
| 7 | $C_{mesh} = N(N-1)c/2$ | Mesh link count | Topology cost |
| 8 | $P_{collision} = 1 - (1 - 1/N)^{N-1}$ | Pure ALOHA approx | Bus collision probability |
| 9 | $\sigma_J = \sqrt{E[t^2] - (E[t])^2}$ | Standard deviation of delay | Jitter |
| 10 | $CRC_{poly} = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ (Modbus) | Generator polynomial | Modbus RTU error check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - Ethernet 10BASE-T max segment: **100 m** (copper) / **2 km** (fiber)
> - RS-485 max devices per segment: **32** (with repeaters: up to 247)
> - RS-485 max cable length: **1200 m** at lower baud rates
> - Modbus broadcast address: **0** (no response from slaves)
> - TCP 3-way handshake must complete before any data PDU is sent.

## 2.5 Real-World CPS Utility

- **Smart Grid Substation (IEC 61850):** Uses a **process bus** (ring topology with PRP/HSR redundancy) carrying Sampled Values over Ethernet. The TCP/IP suite is used for engineering access, while GOOSE messages use Layer-2 multicast for sub-cycle tripping (< 4 ms).
- **Process Plant (PROFINET + Modbus TCP):** Field instruments on Profinet (real-time, isochronous) connect to a Modbus TCP gateway exposing registers to a SCADA HMI on the plant Ethernet.
- **Building Automation (BACnet/Modbus):** Chillers, AHUs, and VAVs on RS-485 Modbus RTU; the front-end uses TCP/IP and BACnet/IP to the BAS server.
- **Automotive CPS (CAN/LIN backbone):** Inside a vehicle, wired buses (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet) form a hybrid mesh; externally, the gateway exposes diagnostics over **DoIP** (Diagnostics over IP, UDP port 13400).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modbus RTU CRC-16 Calculation

The **CRC-16** (Cyclic Redundancy Check) in Modbus RTU uses the polynomial:

$$G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$$

which in binary is `1 1000 0000 0000 0101` (`0x8005`). The initial value is `0xFFFF`. The CRC is computed over all bytes **before** the CRC field itself, and the result is transmitted **low byte first, then high byte** (little-endian).

### Worked Example: Compute CRC-16 for the message `0x01 0x03 0x00 0x00 0x00 0x0A`

This is a Modbus request: "Read 10 (0x0A) holding registers starting at address 0x0000, from slave 0x01."

**Step 1 — Initialization**
The CRC register is loaded with the initial value:

$$CRC = 0xFFFF$$

**Step 2 — XOR with First Byte**
XOR the low byte of the CRC with the first message byte (0x01), then keep the result in the CRC register:

$$CRC = 0xFFFF \oplus 0x01 = 0xFFFE$$

**Step 3 — Process 8 Bits of the Byte**
For each of the 8 bits in the byte, perform:

$$\text{If LSB of CRC = 1:} \quad CRC = (CRC \gg 1) \oplus 0xA001$$

$$\text{If LSB of CRC = 0:} \quad CRC = CRC \gg 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The polynomial `0x8005` becomes **`0xA001`** when reflected (LSB-first, which is the way Modbus RTU processes it).

Let us explicitly process the bits of `0x01` (binary `0000 0001`):

| Bit # | CRC before shift | LSB | Operation | CRC after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0xFFFE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x7FFF |
| 2 | 0x7FFF | 1 | (>> 1) ^ 0xA001 | 0x3FFE ^ 0xA001 = 0x9FFF |
| 3 | 0x9FFF | 1 | (>> 1) ^ 0xA001 | 0x4FFF ^ 0xA001 = 0xEFFF |
| 4 | 0xEFFF | 1 | (>> 1) ^ 0xA001 | 0x77FF ^ 0xA001 = 0xD7FE |
| 5 | 0xD7FE | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x6BFF |
| 6 | 0x6BFF | 1 | (>> 1) ^ 0xA001 | 0x357F ^ 0xA001 = 0x957E |
| 7 | 0x957E | 0 | shift >> 1 | 0x4ABF |
| 8 | 0x4ABF | 1 | (>> 1) ^ 0xA001 | 0x255F ^ 0xA001 = 0x855E |

After processing `0x01`: **CRC = 0x855E**

**Step 4 — Process Second Byte 0x03**
XOR 0x855E with 0x03, then process 8 bits. We summarize the iterative computation; for brevity, the result after processing 0x03:

$$CRC_{after\ 0x03} = 0x4161$$

**Step 5 — Process Remaining Bytes**
Continuing similarly for `0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x0A`, the final CRC register value is:

$$CRC_{final} = 0xC5CD$$

**Step 6 — Transmission Order (Little-Endian)**
Modbus RTU transmits the CRC low byte first:

$$\text{Transmitted CRC} = \underbrace{0xCD}_{\text{low byte first}}, \underbrace{0xC5}_{\text{high byte second}}$$

**Final ADU (hexadecimal):**

$$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{01}_{\text{slave}} \; \underbrace{03}_{\text{FC}} \; \underbrace{00 \; 00 \; 00 \; 0A}_{\text{data}} \; \underbrace{CD \; C5}_{\text{CRC lo, hi}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: TCP/IP Encapsulation Overhead

When an application payload of size $L_{app}$ bytes is sent via TCP/IP over Ethernet, the total on-wire size is:

$$L_{wire} = L_{app} + H_{TCP} + H_{IP} + H_{Eth}$$

where the standard header sizes are:

$$H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ bytes (min, no options)}$$
$$H_{IP} = 20 \text{ bytes (min, no options)}$$
$$H_{Eth} = 14 \text{ bytes (Ethernet II) + 4 bytes (VLAN tag, optional)} = 14 \text{ to } 18 \text{ bytes}$$
$$\text{Frame Check Sequence (FCS)} = 4 \text{ bytes (trailer)}$$

For an **un-tagged IPv4 TCP packet** with a 10-byte application payload:

$$L_{wire} = 10 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes}$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{L_{app}}{L_{wire}} = \frac{10}{68} \approx 0.147 = 14.7\%$$

For a **1500-byte** application payload (full-size Ethernet frame):

$$\eta = \frac{1500}{1538} \approx 97.5\%$$

This explains why CPS engineers **batch small sensor readings** into larger packets — to amortize the header overhead.

## 3.3 Derivation: Modbus Master Poll Cycle Time (Worst-Case Latency)

A Modbus master sequentially polls $N$ slaves. The worst-case time for the master to revisit slave #1 is:

$$T_{cycle} = N \cdot (t_{tx} + t_{rx} + t_{gap} + t_{proc}) + t_{silent}$$

The frame transmission time at baud rate $B$ (bps) for an $L$-bit frame:

$$t_{tx} = \frac{L_{frame}}{B}$$

For Modbus RTU at **9600 bps, 11 bits/char, 8-byte request, 7-byte response**:

$$t_{tx,req} = \frac{8 \times 11}{9600} = 9.17 \text{ ms}$$
$$t_{tx,resp} = \frac{7 \times 11}{9600} = 8.02 \text{ ms}$$
$$t_{silent} = 3.5 \times 11 / 9600 = 4.01 \text{ ms}$$
$$t_{gap} \approx 1 \text{ ms (master turnaround)}$$
$$t_{proc} \approx 1 \text{ ms (slave processing)}$$

For **$N = 10$ slaves**:

$$T_{cycle} = 10 \times (9.17 + 8.02 + 4.01 + 1 + 1) = 10 \times 23.20 = 232 \text{ ms}$$

So slave #1 is updated at most every **232 ms** — adequate for **slow processes** (e.g., tank level monitoring) but insufficient for **fast motion control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum slaves per master** is limited not just by RS-485 driver capacity (32) but also by the **cycle time budget** of the control application.

## 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) for CPS TCP Tuning

The BDP gives the number of bits "in flight" on a link:

$$BDP = B \cdot RTT$$

For a plant-wide Ethernet link with $B = 100$ Mbps and $RTT = 10$ ms:

$$BDP = 100 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 10^6 \text{ bits} = 125 \text{ KB}$$

The TCP receive window should be **≥ BDP** for full link utilization:

$$W_{TCP} \geq BDP = 125 \text{ KB}$$

Default Linux TCP window is often 64 KB or 256 KB; for CPS backbones, it must be tuned to **at least the BDP**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Topology vs. CPS Requirement

| Requirement | Bus | Star | Ring | Mesh | Tree |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lowest cost** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |
| **Fault tolerance** | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ (dual ring) | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Determinism** | ★★ (CSMA/CD) | ★★★★ (full-duplex switch) | ★★★★★ (token) | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Scalability** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Real-time control** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Cable simplicity** | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |
| **Diagnostic ease** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Star topology with managed switches is the most common in modern CPS**, because it provides the best balance of determinism, fault isolation, and cost. Ring topologies dominate where determinism is paramount (motion control, substation IEDs). Mesh is reserved for **safety-critical** and **large-scale** (smart grid) CPS.

## 4.3 Modbus Master–Slave Transaction Flow (RTU and TCP)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Modbus Master / Client
    participant S1 as Slave 1 (Addr 0x01)
    participant S2 as Slave 2 (Addr 0x02)
    participant S3 as Slave 3 (Addr 0x03)

    Note over M,S1: Modbus RTU (RS-485 Half-Duplex)
    M->>S1: ADU: 01 03 00 00 00 0A CRC_LO CRC_HI
    activate S1
    S1-->>M: ADU: 01 03 14 [20 bytes data] CRC_LO CRC_HI
    deactivate S1

    Note over M,S2: Master waits 3.5 char times (silent interval)
    M->>S2: ADU: 02 03 00 00 00 0A CRC_LO CRC_HI
    activate S2
    S2-->>M: ADU: 02 03 14 [20 bytes data] CRC_LO CRC_HI
    deactivate S2

    Note over M,S3: Continue polling sequentially
    M->>S3: ADU: 03 03 00 00 00 0A CRC_LO CRC_HI
    activate S3
    S3-->>M: ADU: 03 03 14 [20 bytes data] CRC_LO CRC_HI
    deactivate S3

    Note over M: Cycle repeats: Tcycle = sum of all transactions
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Modbus TCP Client
    participant SW as Ethernet Switch
    participant Sv as Modbus TCP Server / Slave

    Note over C,Sv: Modbus TCP (Full-Duplex, TCP Port 502)
    C->>SW: TCP SYN (src port 50100)
    SW->>Sv: TCP SYN
    Sv-->>SW: TCP SYN+ACK
    SW-->>C: TCP SYN+ACK
    C->>SW: TCP ACK
    SW->>Sv: TCP ACK
    Note over C,Sv: Connection Established (3-way handshake)

    C->>Sv: MBAP + FC03 + Start Addr + Qty
    Sv-->>C: MBAP + FC03 + Byte Count + Data

    Note over C,Sv: Connection kept alive or closed via FIN
    C->>Sv: TCP FIN
    Sv-->>C: TCP FIN+ACK
    C->>Sv: TCP ACK
    Note over C,Sv: Connection Closed
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**Differentiate between Bus, Star, and Ring topologies in a CPS context, with one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bus** | All nodes share a single common backbone terminated at both ends. | Lowest cable cost and easy to extend. | A single cable break brings down the entire network. |
| **Star** | Each node connects via a dedicated link to a central switch/hub. | One node failure does not affect others; easy diagnostics. | Central device is a single point of failure; more cabling. |
| **Ring** | Each node connects to exactly two neighbors forming a closed loop; a token grants transmit rights. | Deterministic access; predictable latency suitable for real-time CPS control loops. | Single ring is fragile; a break disrupts all traffic (mitigated by dual-ring). |

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Explain the function of TCP and UDP in a Cyber-Physical System. When would you prefer UDP over TCP for CPS traffic?**

**Model Answer:**

**TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** is a **connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream** transport-layer protocol that guarantees in-order, error-free delivery using a 3-way handshake, sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmission. It is used in CPS for **non-time-critical but reliability-critical** traffic such as:
- Firmware / recipe download to PLCs.
- SCADA alarm log upload.
- Configuration / parameter write operations.
- OPC UA binary traffic.

**UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** is a **connectionless, best-effort, message-oriented** transport-layer protocol with **no handshake, no ACK, no retransmission**, and an 8-byte header. It offers the **lowest possible latency** and supports multicast/broadcast natively.

**UDP is preferred in CPS when:**
1. **Latency is critical** and a missed packet is acceptable (e.g., real-time sensor streaming at 1 kHz).
2. **Multicast/broadcast** is needed (e.g., NTP time sync, GOOSE-like substation multicast).
3. **Small, periodic messages** are sent (e.g., heartbeat / process image broadcast from a PLC to multiple HMIs).
4. **Application layer provides its own reliability** (e.g., OPC UA Pub/Sub over UDP).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question B — Choice A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **TCP/IP 4-layer model** in detail. List at least **two protocols** operating at each layer and their relevance to a Cyber-Physical System. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Modbus RTU master at **9600 bps, 8N1** (10 bits per character) polls **15 slaves** sequentially. Each request is **8 bytes** and each response is **21 bytes**. The slave processing delay is **2 ms**, the master turnaround is **1 ms**, and the inter-frame silent interval is **3.5 character times**. Calculate:
  (i) the per-slave transaction time,
  (ii) the total master poll cycle time,
  (iii) the maximum control-loop update rate achievable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

The TCP/IP model has **four layers**:

```
+-----------------------------+
| Layer 4: Application        |  HTTP, MQTT, Modbus TCP, OPC UA, FTP, DNS, SNMP
+-----------------------------+
| Layer 3: Transport          |  TCP (reliable), UDP (best-effort)
+-----------------------------+
| Layer 2: Internet / Network |  IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, ARP, OSPF
+-----------------------------+
| Layer 1: Link / Network Acc.|  Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11), RS-485, PPP
+-----------------------------+
```

**Layer 1 – Link:** Provides framing and physical signaling. **Ethernet (802.3)** connects PLCs, switches, and SCADA servers. **RS-485 / RS-232** is the physical medium for Modbus RTU in field networks.

**Layer 2 – Internet:** Provides logical addressing and routing. **IPv4** is widely used; **IPv6** is being adopted in IIoT for its vast address space (2¹²⁸ addresses). **ICMP** is used for diagnostics (ping). **ARP** maps IP to MAC.

**Layer 3 – Transport:** Provides end-to-end delivery. **TCP** offers reliable, in-order delivery with flow control — used for SCADA configuration, firmware upload, OPC UA. **UDP** offers low-latency, connectionless delivery — used for real-time telemetry, NTP time sync, and multicast sensor data.

**Layer 4 – Application:** Provides end-user services. **Modbus TCP** (port 502) is the dominant CPS protocol. **OPC UA** offers secure, semantically rich data exchange. **MQTT** (port 1883/8883) is used for IIoT cloud telemetry. **HTTP/HTTPS** is used for web-based HMIs. **SNMP** is used for network device monitoring.

**CPS-specific note:** The choice of TCP vs UDP at Layer 3 directly impacts **determinism**. Time-critical control loops (e.g., < 10 ms) typically use UDP or raw Layer-2 protocols (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT), while supervisory and configuration traffic uses TCP.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:**
- Baud rate $B = 9600$ bps
- Bits per character = 10 (8N1)
- $N = 15$ slaves
- $L_{req} = 8$ bytes
- $L_{resp} = 21$ bytes
- $t_{proc} = 2$ ms
- $t_{turn} = 1$ ms
- Silent interval = 3.5 char times

**Step 1 — Compute one character time:**

$$t_{char} = \frac{10 \text{ bits}}{9600 \text{ bps}} = 1.0417 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Transmission time for request:**

$$t_{tx,req} = \frac{8 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte}}{9600 \text{ bps}} = \frac{80}{9600} = 8.333 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission time for response:**

$$t_{tx,resp} = \frac{21 \times 10}{9600} = 21.875 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Silent interval:**

$$t_{silent} = 3.5 \times t_{char} = 3.5 \times 1.0417 = 3.646 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 (i) — Per-slave transaction time:**

$$T_{slave} = t_{tx,req} + t_{turn} + t_{proc} + t_{tx,resp} + t_{silent}$$

$$T_{slave} = 8.333 + 1 + 2 + 21.875 + 3.646 = 36.854 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 (ii) — Total poll cycle time:**

$$T_{cycle} = N \times T_{slave} = 15 \times 36.854 = 552.81 \text{ ms} \approx 553 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 7 (iii) — Maximum control-loop update rate:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{0.553} \approx 1.81 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B Choice A):**
> - Do **NOT** confuse 8N1 as 8 bits per character; remember the start, parity, and stop bits — for 8N1 it is **10 bits per character**.
> - Many students forget to **add the inter-frame silent interval** — this is mandatory in Modbus RTU and is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction if missed.
> - Unit conversions: 9600 bps = 9600 bits/s, NOT 9600 bytes/s. Always divide bits by 8 (or 10) to get bytes.
> - Final answer for $T_{cycle}$ must carry **units** (ms or s); an answer without units loses 0.5 mark.

---

### Question B — Choice B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style — Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **Modbus protocol architecture** in detail, covering:
  (i) Modbus data model (Coils, Discrete Inputs, Input Registers, Holding Registers),
  (ii) Modbus RTU frame structure with the function code `0x03` (Read Holding Registers) — show a request and response example,
  (iii) Difference between Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Star and Mesh topologies** as applied to a CPS plant. A plant has **20 PLCs** arranged in a full-mesh topology for a safety-critical line. Calculate:
  (i) the total number of physical links required,
  (ii) the number of Ethernet switch ports needed if the design changes to a star topology with one central switch,
  (iii) which topology offers better fault tolerance and why. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**(i) Modbus Data Model:**
