This small example is for **academic illustration only**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Compression Algorithms for CPS | Algorithm | Type | Best For | Compression Ratio | Speed | CPS Suitability | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Huffman** | Lossless, Entropy | Static probability distributions | Moderate (1.5–3x) | Fast | ★★★★ Firmware updates | | **Arithmetic** | Lossless, Entropy | Skewed distributions, near-optimal | High (2–5x) | Moderate | ★★★★ Sensor telemetry | | **LZW** | Lossless, Dictionary | Repeating patterns | High (2–4x) | Fast | ★★★★★ Structured logs | | **RLE** | Lossless, Run-based | Long runs of identical data | Very high (5–50x) for runs | Very fast | ★★★ Binary sensor maps | | **DEFLATE** | Lossless, Hybrid (LZ77 + Huffman) | General-purpose | High (2.5–4x) | Fast | ★★★★★ Standard ZIP/gzip | ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Encryption Algorithms for CPS | Algorithm | Type | Key Size | Block/Output | Strength | CPS Suitability | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **AES-128** | Symmetric, block cipher | 128 bits | 128-bit blocks | Excellent (NIST approved) | ★★★★★ Real-time control | | **ChaCha20** | Symmetric, stream cipher | 256 bits | Stream | Excellent | ★★★★★ Low-power IoT | | **RSA-2048** | Asymmetric | 2048 bits | Variable | Excellent (factoring-hard) | ★★★★ Bootstrapping, signatures | | **ECC (P-256)** | Asymmetric, elliptic curve | 256 bits | Variable | Excellent (ECDH-hard) | ★★★★★ Constrained devices | | **SHA-256** | Hash (one-way) | N/A | 256-bit digest | Excellent | ★★★★★ Integrity, secure boot | | **MD5** | Hash | N/A | 128-bit digest | **Broken** | ★ Do not use in CPS | | **Ascon-128** | Authenticated encryption | 128 bits | 128-bit | Excellent (NIST LWC 2023) | ★★★★★ Class-0 IoT devices | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Student Errors:** > 1. Confusing **block cipher mode** (ECB, CBC, GCM) with the algorithm itself. > 2. Stating that **MD5 is secure** — it is cryptographically broken since 2004. > 3. Forgetting the **prefix-free property** requirement when describing Huffman codes. > 4. Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in entropy calculations. > 5. Computing RSA decryption with the public key $e$ instead of the private key $d$. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CPS Data Flow with Compression + Encryption (Block Architecture) This block-level functional architecture shows how a CPS sensor node processes data end-to-end before wireless transmission, integrating both compression and encryption pipelines. ```mermaid flowchart LR sensA[Physical Sensor
Temperature Pressure Vibration] --> adcA[ADC
Analog to Digital] adcA --> bufA[Buffer
Sample Queue] bufA --> compA[Compression Engine
Huffman or LZW] compA --> compOut[Compressed Payload
Reduced Bit Stream] compOut --> encA[Encryption Engine
AES-128 GCM] encA --> encOut[Ciphertext
Authenticated] encOut --> macA[MAC Layer
802.15.4 or CAN FD] macA --> phyA[PHY Layer
Wireless or Wired] keyA[Symmetric Key
K derived from ECC] --> encA hashA[SHA-256 of Payload] --> encA phyA --> netA[Receiver CPS Node] netA --> decA[Decryption Engine
AES-128 GCM] decA --> decompA[Decompression Engine
Inverse Huffman or LZW] decompA --> ctlA[Controller / Actuator
Closed Loop Decision] ``` ### 4.2 Huffman Tree for the Worked Example (Visual Tree) This diagram renders the Huffman tree constructed in Section 3.1, showing the codeword assignments for symbols A, B, C, D, and E. ```mermaid flowchart TD rootNode((Root
weight 1.00)) n3Node((N3
weight 0.55)) n2Node((N2
weight 0.45)) leafA([A
0.35]) leafB([B
0.20]) leafC([C
0.15]) leafD([D
0.15]) leafE([E
0.15]) n1Node((N1
weight 0.30)) rootNode -->|0| n2Node rootNode -->|1| n3Node n3Node -->|0| leafB n3Node -->|1| leafA n2Node -->|0| n1Node n2Node -->|1| leafC n1Node -->|0| leafE n1Node -->|1| leafD ``` > [!NOTE] > **Codeword extraction:** Read the branch labels from root to leaf. For example, root → 0 → 0 → 1 gives D = 001 (3 bits), confirming the table in Section 3.1. ### 4.3 RSA Key Generation and Encryption-Decryption Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD pNode[Choose Primes p and q] --> nNode[Compute n = p times q] pNode --> phiNode[Compute phi of n = p minus 1 times q minus 1] phiNode --> eNode[Choose Public Exponent e
with gcd of e and phi n equals 1] eNode --> dNode[Compute Private Exponent d
such that e times d mod phi n equals 1] nNode --> pubKey[Public Key is e and n] dNode --> privKey[Private Key is d and n] pubKey --> encOp[Encryption Operation
C = M to the power e mod n] privKey --> decOp[Decryption Operation
M = C to the power d mod n] encOp --> cipherNode[Ciphertext C] cipherNode --> decOp decOp --> plainNode[Recovered Plaintext M] ``` ### 4.4 CPS Layered Security Architecture This diagram illustrates the defence-in-depth model used in CPS, showing how encryption and compression are placed at different protocol layers. ```mermaid flowchart TD appLayer[Application Layer
MQTT or OPC UA Payload] --> compLayer[Compression Layer
Huffman LZW DEFLATE] compLayer --> tlsLayer[TLS or DTLS Layer
Authenticated Encryption] tlsLayer --> authLayer[Authentication Layer
ECC or RSA Signatures] authLayer --> netLayer[Network Layer
6LoWPAN or RPL] netLayer --> macLayer[MAC Layer
802.15.4 or CAN FD] macLayer --> phyLayer[PHY Layer
Wireless or Wired] phyLayer --> receiverSide((CPS Receiver Node)) ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`** **Differentiate between lossless and lossy data compression. State one example algorithm for each and justify which is mandatory in safety-critical CPS control loops.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Lossless | Lossy | |---|---|---| | Reconstruction | Exact bit-for-bit | Approximate, bounded error | | Information preserved | 100% | Partial (within tolerance) | | Example algorithm | Huffman coding, LZW, RLE | JPEG, MPEG, Wavelet codecs | | CPS suitability | Mandatory for control commands and firmware updates | Acceptable for video surveillance where small errors are tolerable | **Justification (1 mark):** In safety-critical CPS control loops (e.g., industrial robot arm, insulin pump, automotive braking), even a single corrupted bit can cause catastrophic physical failure. Therefore, **lossless compression is mandatory** for control commands, sensor measurements feeding into closed-loop PID controllers, and firmware updates. Lossy compression is permitted only in human-perception loops (e.g., surveillance video, remote telepresence). --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`** **Explain the role of AES-128 in CPS communication. Why is AES preferred over DES and 3DES in modern CPS deployments?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of AES-128 in CPS (1 mark):** AES-128 (Advanced Encryption Standard with 128-bit key) provides **confidentiality** for sensor data, control commands, and firmware transmitted over CPS networks. It operates on 128-bit blocks through 10 rounds of substitution, permutation, mixing, and key addition, providing strong symmetric encryption with low latency — ideal for real-time control loops. **Why AES over DES/3DES (2 marks):** 1. **DES** uses a 56-bit effective key, which is now brute-forceable in hours on commodity hardware. **AES-128** has $2^{128}$ key space — computationally infeasible to break. 2. **3DES** triples the DES key for 112-bit effective security but is **3x slower** than DES and suffers from meet-in-the-middle attacks. **AES-128** is **6x faster** in software and **10x+ faster** in hardware. 3. AES has **no known practical cryptanalytic attacks** and is the **NIST FIPS 197** standard since 2001. 4. AES hardware acceleration (AES-NI instructions) is built into most modern CPS processors (ARM Cortex-M33, ESP32-S3, RPi RP2040), making it energy-efficient. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**, and the student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B within the module. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A CPS vibration monitoring node transmits the following message over a constrained wireless link: **Message:** `S = "ABBACABBA"` with symbol frequencies: $A = 4$, $B = 3$, $C = 1$, total length = 8 symbols. **Required:** 1. Construct the Huffman tree and assign binary codewords for all symbols. 2. Compute the entropy $H(S)$ of the source. 3. Compute the average code length $\bar{L}$ and the coding efficiency $\eta$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **symmetric** and **asymmetric** encryption with suitable CPS examples. Compare RSA and ECC for use in a battery-powered wireless sensor node (WSN) with $\le 32$ KB RAM, justifying your choice with at least three quantitative or qualitative criteria. --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Huffman Coding Construction **Step 1 — Compute symbol probabilities:** $$P(A) = \frac{4}{8} = 0.50, \quad P(B) = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375, \quad P(C) = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$ **Step 2 — Huffman Tree Construction (Iterative Merging):** Iteration 1: Merge $C$ (0.125) and $B$ (0.375) → $N_1$ with weight $0.50$. Queue: $A(0.50), N_1(0.50)$. Iteration 2: Merge $A(0.50)$ and $N_1(0.50)$ → Root with weight $1.00$. ✓ **Step 3 — Assigned Codewords:** | Symbol | Codeword | Length $L_i$ | |---|---|---| | A | 0 | 1 | | B | 10 | 2 | | C | 11 | 2 | **Step 4 — Entropy Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i \\[4pt] &= -\bigl(0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.375 \log_2 0.375 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125\bigr) \\[4pt] &= -\bigl(0.50 \times (-1) + 0.375 \times (-1.4150) + 0.125 \times (-3)\bigr) \\[4pt] &= -\bigl(-0.50 - 0.5306 - 0.375\bigr) \\[4pt] &= 1.4056 \text{ bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Average Code Length:** $$ \begin{aligned} \bar{L} &= (0.50 \times 1) + (0.375 \times 2) + (0.125 \times 2) \\[4pt] &= 0.50 + 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.50 \text{ bits/symbol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Coding Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \times 100\% \\[4pt] &= \frac{1.4056}{1.50} \times 100\% \\[4pt] &= 93.71\% \end{aligned} $$ **Compression Result:** Original message $S$ = 8 symbols × 8 bits (ASCII) = 64 bits. Encoded length = $(4 \times 1) + (3 \times 2) + (1 \times 2) = 12$ bits. **Compression Ratio** $CR = \frac{64}{12} \approx 5.33$, **Saving** $S\% = 81.25\%$. --- #### Part (b) — Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Encryption for WSN **Symmetric Encryption (3.5 marks):** - Uses a **single shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption. - Algorithms: **AES-128, ChaCha20, 3DES** (legacy). - **CPS Example:** A wireless body area network (WBAN) where a heart-rate sensor and a smartphone share a pre-provisioned 128-bit AES key. The sensor encrypts ECG data packets using AES-128-CCM (Counter with CBC-MAC) and transmits over BLE 5.0. - **Advantages:** Very fast (AES achieves > 1 Gbps in hardware), low memory footprint, energy-efficient. - **Disadvantages:** Key distribution problem — how to securely share the key initially among $n$ devices requires $O(n^2)$ keys. **Asymmetric Encryption (3.5 marks):** - Uses a **key pair**: a public key (freely distributed) and a private key (kept secret). - Algorithms: **RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman**. - **CPS Example:** A smart meter in a smart grid uses **ECDSA-P256** to digitally sign its consumption readings before transmission to the data concentrator. The concentrator verifies the signature using the meter's public key stored in its certificate. **Quantitative Comparison: RSA vs. ECC for WSN:** | Criterion | RSA-2048 | ECC P-256 | WSN Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | **Key size for equivalent security** | 2048 bits | 256 bits | ECC is **8x smaller** ✓ | | **Signature size** | 256 bytes | 64 bytes | ECC is **4x smaller** ✓ | | **Key generation time** | ~500 ms | ~5 ms | ECC is **100x faster** ✓ | | **Sign operation time** | ~15 ms | ~2 ms | ECC is **7x faster** ✓ | | **Verify operation time** | ~1 ms | ~5 ms | RSA is faster for verify | | **RAM/flash footprint** | High (large big-int arithmetic) | Low (fits in 32 KB) | **ECC is WSN-friendly** ✓ | | **Battery impact** | High | Low | **ECC saves energy** ✓ | | **Standardisation** | NIST, FIPS 140-3 | NIST, SECG, ANSI | Both are standard | **Conclusion:** **ECC (P-256 or Curve25519) is the clear choice** for battery-powered WSNs because it provides equivalent security to RSA-2048 with **8x smaller keys**, **4x smaller signatures**, and **~10x lower energy consumption per operation** — critical for multi-year battery life targets. RSA is reserved for high-end controllers and backend servers with abundant resources. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **LZW compression algorithm**. Apply LZW to encode the input string `S = "ABABABABA"` step-by-step, showing the dictionary evolution and the emitted output codes. Compute the compression ratio achieved. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **RSA algorithm** with its key generation, encryption, and decryption steps. For $p = 7$, $q = 13$, and $e = 5$, compute the public and private keys, and encrypt the plaintext $M = 10$. Verify by decrypting. --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — LZW Compression of "ABABABABA" **Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** The LZW encoder consists of: - **Input Buffer** — reads source symbols one at a time. - **Dictionary** — initially contains all single ASCII characters (0–255). - **String Matcher** — finds the longest dictionary string matching current input. - **Code Emitter** — outputs the dictionary index of the matched string. - **Dictionary Extender** — adds new string (matched + next input char) to dictionary. **Step-by-Step Encoding of `S = "ABABABABA"` (5 marks):** | Step | Input Read | Longest Match (W) | New Entry Added | Emitted Code | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | A | A | AB (code 256) | 65 (A) | | 2 | B | B | BA (code 257) | 66 (B) | | 3 | A | AB | ABA (code 258) | 256 (AB) | | 4 | B | BA | BAB (code 259) | 257 (BA) | | 5 | A | ABA | ABAB (code 260) | 258 (ABA) | | 6 | B | BAB | BABA (code 261) | 259 (BAB) | | 7 | A | — (incomplete) | — | — | | 8 | end-of-stream | A | — | 65 (A) | **Final Emitted Code Stream:** `65, 66, 256, 258, 259, 65` (6 codes × 9 bits = 54 bits, assuming codes grow to 9 bits from code 256 onwards). **Compression Ratio:** - Original (ASCII): 9 characters × 8 bits = 72 bits. - Compressed: 54 bits. - $CR = \frac{72}{54} = 1.33$, **Saving** $= 25\%$. For a 9-character input, LZW achieves modest compression because the pattern repeats only in small 2-character units. LZW performs better on longer streams with larger repeating patterns. --- #### Part (b) — RSA Key Generation and Encryption **Key Generation (3 marks):** Given: $p = 7$, $q = 13$, $e = 5$. **Step 1 — Compute $n$:** $$n = p \times q = 7 \times 13 = 91$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$:** $$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 6 \times 12 = 72$$ **Step 3 — Verify $e$ is valid:** $\gcd(5, 72) = 1$ ✓ **Step 4 — Compute $d$ such that $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{72}$:** Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm: $$ \begin{aligned} 72 &= 14 \times 5 + 2 \\ 5 &= 2 \times 2 + 1 \\ 2 &= 2 \times 1 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ Back-substitute: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 &= 5 - 2 \times 2 \\ &= 5 - 2 \times (72 - 14 \times 5) \\ &= 29 \times 5 - 2 \times 72 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $d \equiv 29 \pmod{72}$. **Verify:** $5 \times 29 = 145 = 2 \times 72 + 1 = 145$ ✓ **Public Key** = $(e, n) = (5, 91)$. **Private Key** = $(d, n) = (29, 91)$. **Encryption (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= M^{e} \bmod n \\ &= 10^{5} \bmod 91 \end{aligned} $$ Compute via repeated squaring: $$ \begin{aligned} 10^1 \bmod 91 &= 10 \\ 10^2 \bmod 91 &= 100 \bmod 91 = 9 \\ 10^4 \bmod 91 &= 9^2 \bmod 91 = 81 \\ 10^5 \bmod 91 &= 10^4 \times 10^1 \bmod 91 = 81 \times 10 \bmod 91 = 810 \bmod 91 \end{aligned} $$ $810 = 8 \times 91 + 82$ ⟹ $C = 82$. **Ciphertext:** $C = 82$. **Decryption (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} M &= C^{d} \bmod n = 82^{29} \bmod 91 \end{aligned} $$ Using Fermat's Little Theorem shortcut (since $p=7, q=13$ are prime): For verification, we can use $M = C^{d \bmod \phi(n)} \bmod n = 82^{29} \bmod 91$. Compute step-by-step (truncated for brevity, full expansion below): $$ \begin{aligned} 82^1 \bmod 91 &= 82 \\ 82^2 \bmod 91 &= 6724 \bmod 91 = 6724 - 73 \times 91 = 6724 - 6643 = 81 \\ 82^4 \bmod 91 &= 81^2 \bmod 91 = 6561 \bmod 91 = 6561 - 72 \times 91 = 6561 - 6552 = 9 \\ 82^8 \bmod 91 &= 9^2 \bmod 91 = 81 \\ 82^{16} \bmod 91 &= 81^2 \bmod 91 = 9 \end{aligned} $$ Now, $29 = 16 + 8 + 4 + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} 82^{29} \bmod 91 &= 82^{16} \times 82^{8} \times 82^{4} \times 82^{1} \bmod 91 \\ &= 9 \times 81 \times 9 \times 82 \bmod 91 \\ &= (9 \times 9) \times (81 \times 82) \bmod 91 \\ &= 81 \times 6642 \bmod 91 \end{aligned} $$ $81 \bmod 91 = 81$. $6642 \bmod 91 = 6642 - 72 \times 91 = 6642 - 6552 = 90$. So $M = 81 \times 90 \bmod 91 = 7290 \bmod 91$. $7290 \div 91 = 80.109...$ ⟹ $80 \times 91 = 7280$ ⟹ $7290 - 7280 = 10$. **Recovered Plaintext:** $M = 10$ ✓ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Entropy Units:** Always express entropy in **bits per symbol (bps)** using $\log_2$. Using $\ln$ (natural log) gives **nats** and loses 1 mark if the unit is wrong. > 2. **Code Length Verification:** After constructing Huffman codes, students often forget to verify the **prefix-free property** (no code is a prefix of another). Examiners explicitly check this — losing 1–2 marks. > 3. **RSA Decryption Mistake:** A common error is computing $C^{d} \bmod p$ and $C^{d} \bmod q$ separately and forgetting the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** recombination. For small examples without CRT, you can compute $C^{d} \bmod n$ directly using repeated squaring — but show all steps. > 4. **Confusing Encryption Direction:** Students sometimes write $M = C^{e} \bmod n$ for decryption. Memorise: **Encrypt with $e$, Decrypt with $d$.** > 5. **LZW Dictionary Initialisation:** The dictionary must start with **all 256 ASCII codes (0–255)**. New entries begin at code 256. Missing this loses 1 mark. > 6. **Compression Ratio Direction:** Higher $CR$ means **better** compression. $CR = 1$ means no compression. Never state "$CR$ should be less than 1." --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:** - ✅ **Data compression** in CPS serves two masters: **bandwidth efficiency** and **latency reduction** in real-time control loops. - ✅ **Lossless compression** is mandatory for control commands, firmware, and medical/financial CPS data; **lossy compression** is acceptable for human-perception streams. - ✅ **Huffman coding** is optimal among prefix-free codes; entropy $H(S) \le \bar{L} < H(S) + 1$. - ✅ **LZW** builds a dynamic dictionary starting from 256 single-character entries; emits dictionary indices, not literal symbols. - ✅ **RLE** is best for long runs; CPS use case is sparse binary sensor data and threshold bitmaps. - ✅ **AES-128** has a 128-bit block size and 10 rounds; it is the **de facto standard** for symmetric encryption in CPS. - ✅ **RSA security** rests on the difficulty of factoring $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are large primes; minimum key size is **2048 bits** (NIST 2024). - ✅ **ECC** offers equivalent security to RSA with **~10x smaller key sizes** — ideal for resource-constrained CPS nodes. - ✅ **SHA-256** produces a 256-bit (32-byte, 64-hex-character) digest; **MD5 is broken** — never use it in new CPS designs. - ✅ **Lightweight cryptography** (e.g., Ascon, NIST LWC winner 2023) is the future for Class-0 IoT/CPS devices with extreme resource constraints. - ✅ **Compression before encryption** is the standard CPS pipeline — never reverse the order (random-looking ciphertext is incompressible). - ✅ **CIA Triad** governs CPS security: **Confidentiality** (encryption), **Integrity** (hash/MAC), **Authentication** (digital signatures). - ✅ **Key distribution** is the hardest problem in symmetric cryptography; public-key crypto solves it but is slower. - ✅ **Quantum threat:** Both RSA and ECC are vulnerable to **Shor's algorithm** on a sufficiently powerful quantum computer; CPS systems with 10+ year lifetimes should plan for **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** migration per NIST FIPS 203/204/205 (2024). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For Part A (3 marks), expect a definition or comparison question on compression or encryption. For Part B (14 marks), the most likely combinations are: **(i) Huffman numerical + Symmetric/Asymmetric comparison**, **(ii) LZW numerical + RSA numerical**, or **(iii) AES architecture + ECC vs RSA in WSN**. Practise all three combinations to be fully prepared.

topics.title: Communication Protocols for CPS Applications: IoT Communication Protocols (MQTT, CoAP, LWM2M)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols for CPS Applications: IoT Communication Protocols (MQTT, CoAP, LWM2M)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to IoT Communication Protocols for CPS

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** integrates computation, networking, and physical processes. The glue that ties the physical sensors/actuators to the cyber computation layer is the **communication protocol**. In IoT-enabled CPS, these protocols are lightweight, bandwidth-efficient, and designed to operate over constrained devices and lossy networks (LLNs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> IoT communication protocols are *application-layer* protocols optimized for low power, low bandwidth, and intermittent connectivity — unlike traditional TCP/HTTP used in the wider Internet.

### 1.2 What is MQTT?

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish/subscribe (pub/sub) messaging protocol designed by IBM (1999) and standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922** and **OASIS MQTT v5.0**. It runs over TCP/IP, uses a central **broker**, and is ideal for unreliable networks with constrained devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *MQTT is an asynchronous, broker-mediated pub/sub messaging transport that decouples publishers and subscribers using a topic-based addressing scheme, providing three Quality of Service (QoS) levels and a Last Will and Testament (LWT) mechanism for fault detection.*

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Kiosk
Imagine a **newspaper kiosk** in a city:
- Readers **subscribe** to topics like "Sports" or "Politics" (these are the **MQTT Topics**).
- Publishers (reporters) drop newspapers into the kiosk (the **Broker**).
- Readers do not know who wrote the article; they only care about the topic.
- The kiosk is the single point of delivery.

Similarly, in MQTT, a **device publishes** data to a topic (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`), the **broker** holds and forwards it, and **subscribers** receive only what they care about. **No direct device-to-device connection exists.**

### 1.3 What is CoAP?

**CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** is a specialized web transfer protocol defined in **RFC 7252** for use with constrained nodes and constrained networks. It mimics HTTP (RESTful: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) but runs over **UDP** to save bandwidth. CoAP supports asynchronous confirmable (CON) and non-confirmable (NON) message exchanges and an **Observe** pattern (RFC 7641) for push-based notifications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *CoAP is a RESTful transfer protocol that maps HTTP semantics onto UDP using a compact 4-byte binary header, supports resource discovery, and provides reliable transmission via confirmable messages with retransmission and exponential back-off.*

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Vending Machine
A **vending machine** accepts specific commands (`GET /item/Soda`, `POST /payment`). You press a button, it returns a packet. CoAP is essentially the same idea — a small "machine" (sensor/actuator) with resources that you can read or write using tiny HTTP-like requests, but without the heavy TCP overhead.

### 1.4 What is LWM2M?

**LWM2M (Lightweight Machine-to-Machine)** is a device management protocol defined by the **Open Mobile Alliance (OMA SpecWorks)** in **OMA-LWM2M v1.2**. It runs on top of **CoAP** and is specifically built for **device management and service enablement** in IoT. It uses an object/resource model (similar to a structured database), supports firmware updates, connectivity monitoring, and security (DTLS, PSK, RPK, certificates).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *LWM2M is an application-layer device management protocol that leverages CoAP for transport, employs an object/resource data model where each resource is identified by a numeric URI, and provides standardized interfaces for bootstrapping, registration, device management, and information reporting.*

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Smart-Phone's MDM (Mobile Device Management)
A corporate **IT department** managing thousands of company smartphones can remotely lock, wipe, install apps, and check battery/temperature. LWM2M is the IoT equivalent — a **LWM2M Server** manages fleets of **LWM2M Clients** (sensors, smart meters, wearables), reading their resources and writing configuration updates over CoAP.

### 1.5 Why These Three Protocols? The CPS Trifecta

| CPS Requirement | Best Suited Protocol | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Telemetry streaming from many sensors | **MQTT** | Pub/sub scales well for fan-out |
| Resource-oriented control (read/write sensor) | **CoAP** | RESTful + UDP = ideal for actuators |
| Lifecycle management of devices | **LWM2M** | Built-in boot/register/update/report |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Big Picture:**
> In a CPS, MQTT carries *event data* (telemetry), CoAP carries *control commands* (actuation), and LWM2M carries *lifecycle/management traffic* (provisioning, OTA, diagnostics). They are **complementary, not competing**.

### 1.6 Common Terminology (Standard Metrics)

- **QoS (Quality of Service)** — Reliability level: **0** (at most once), **1** (at least once), **2** (exactly once).
- **Topic** — UTF-8 string for pub/sub routing (MQTT).
- **URI** — Uniform Resource Identifier (CoAP: `coap://host:port/path`).
- **Object/Resource/Instance** — LWM2M data model hierarchy.
- **Broker** — Central MQTT server.
- **DTLS** — Datagram TLS (used by CoAP/LWM2M for security).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MQTT — Deep Architectural Analysis

MQTT uses a **star topology** with a **Broker** at the center. The protocol has three main components:

1. **Publisher** — produces data on a topic.
2. **Subscriber** — receives data from topics it has subscribed to.
3. **Broker** — receives all messages, filters by topic, and distributes to subscribers.

#### 2.1.1 MQTT Control Packet Structure

Every MQTT control packet has a **Fixed Header (2 bytes minimum)**, an optional **Variable Header**, and a **Payload** up to **256 MB**.

The **Fixed Header** layout is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 1} &= [\text{Control Packet Type (4 bits)} \,\vert\, \text{Flags (4 bits)}] \\
\text{Byte 2+} &= \text{Remaining Length (1–4 bytes, variable-length encoding)}
\end{aligned}$$

#### 2.1.2 MQTT Control Packet Types (Kerala University standard list)

| Type (Hex) | Name | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x10 | CONNECT | C → B | Client requests connection |
| 0x20 | CONNACK | B → C | Connection acknowledgement |
| 0x30 | PUBLISH | C ↔ B | Publish a message |
| 0x40 | PUBACK | C ↔ B | QoS 1 acknowledgement |
| 0x50 | PUBREC | C ↔ B | QoS 2 — received |
| 0x60 | PUBREL | C ↔ B | QoS 2 — release |
| 0x70 | PUBCOMP | C ↔ B | QoS 2 — complete |
| 0x80 | SUBSCRIBE | C → B | Subscribe to topic(s) |
| 0x90 | SUBACK | B → C | Subscription ack |
| 0xC0 | PINGREQ | C → B | Keep-alive ping |
| 0xD0 | PINGRESP | B → C | Ping response |
| 0xE0 | DISCONNECT | C → B | Clean disconnect |

> [!NOTE]
> **C → B** = Client to Broker; **B → C** = Broker to Client.

#### 2.1.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Levels — Step-by-Step Logic

- **QoS 0 — At most once:** Fire-and-forget. No acknowledgement. Lowest overhead.
- **QoS 1 — At least once:** PUBLISH → PUBACK. Possible duplicates but guaranteed delivery.
- **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** Four-step handshake (PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP). Highest overhead, no duplicates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The QoS handshake is **between publisher and broker** AND **between broker and subscriber** — end-to-end QoS can therefore be different at each leg.

#### 2.1.4 Last Will and Testament (LWT) and Retained Messages

- **LWT** — A message the broker stores for a client; if the client disconnects ungracefully, the broker publishes the LWT on the client's behalf.
- **Retained Message** — The broker stores the last message on a topic and immediately delivers it to new subscribers.

#### 2.1.5 Real-world Engineering Utility
- **Smart Home** (AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT) — telemetry from thousands of sensors.
- **Automotive** (BMW Car-2-Cloud) — vehicle telemetry.
- **Industrial CPS** (PTC ThingWorx, Siemens MindSphere) — factory floor sensor data.

---

### 2.2 CoAP — Deep Architectural Analysis

CoAP is defined in **RFC 7252** and has these design goals:
- Fulfil RESTful requirements (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
- Low header overhead (4 bytes minimum).
- Operate over UDP (default port **5683**); secure variant **CoAPS** runs over DTLS on port **5684**.
- Support multicast and asynchronous confirmations.

#### 2.2.1 CoAP Message Format

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CoAP Message} &= [\text{Ver (2b)} \,\vert\, \text{T (2b)} \,\vert\, \text{TKL (4b)} \,\vert\, \text{Code (8b)} \,\vert\, \text{Message ID (16b)}] \\
&\quad + \text{Token (0–8 bytes)} + \text{Options} + \text{Payload}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- **Ver** = Version (must be 1).
- **T** = Type — `CON` (Confirmable), `NON` (Non-confirmable), `ACK`, `RST`.
- **TKL** = Token Length.
- **Code** = Class (3 bits) + Detail (5 bits), e.g., `0.01` = GET, `0.02` = POST, `0.04` = DELETE.
- **Message ID** = used for duplicate detection and CON/ACK pairing.

#### 2.2.2 The Four Message Types

| Type | Meaning | Reliability |
|---|---|---|
| CON | Confirmable — must be ACKnowledged | Reliable |
| NON | Non-confirmable — fire & forget | Unreliable |
| ACK | Acknowledgement for CON | — |
| RST | Reset — indicates context loss | — |

#### 2.2.3 Reliability Layer

For CON messages, the client uses **exponential back-off** retransmission:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{retransmit}}(i) &= T_{\text{initial}} \times 2^{i-1} + \text{random jitter} \\
\text{where } i &= 1, 2, 3, \ldots, N_{\text{max retransmits}}
\end{aligned}$$

After $N_{\text{max retransmits}}$ (default **4**), the message is considered failed.

#### 2.2.4 CoAP Observe (RFC 7641) — Push Model

A client can issue a `GET` with the `Observe` option. The server then sends **notifications** whenever the resource state changes. The client can later send another `GET` with `Observe: 1` to **cancel** the subscription.

#### 2.2.5 Real-world Engineering Utility
- **Smart streetlights** — controller queries lamp status, switches ON/OFF.
- **Industrial CPS actuators** — read valve position, write setpoint.
- **Healthcare wearables** — Observe heart rate continuously.

---

### 2.3 LWM2M — Deep Architectural Analysis

LWM2M is built on **CoAP** as transport and is structured around four logical interfaces:

| Interface | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Bootstrap** | Server → Client | Provision credentials & config |
| **Registration** | Client → Server | Register client with its endpoints |
| **Device Management & Service Enablement** | Bidirectional | Read/Write/Execute/Observe on resources |
| **Information Reporting** | Client → Server | Send notifications/observations |

#### 2.3.1 LWM2M Data Model

The data model is hierarchical:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LWM2M Server} &\rightarrow \text{LWM2M Client (Endpoint)} \\
\text{Client} &\rightarrow \text{Objects} \\
\text{Object} &\rightarrow \text{Instances} \\
\text{Instance} &\rightarrow \text{Resources} \\
\text{Resource} &\rightarrow \text{Resource Instances (optional)}
\end{aligned}$$

Each element is identified numerically, e.g., `/3/0/9` = Object 3 (Device), Instance 0, Resource 9 (Battery Level).

#### 2.3.2 Standard LWM2M Objects (OMA Registry)

| Object ID | Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | LWM2M Security | Security mode & keys |
| 1 | LWM2M Server | Server parameters |
| 3 | Device | Manufacturer, model, battery |
| 4 | Connectivity Monitoring | Radio signal, IP address |
| 5 | Firmware Update | OTA update management |
| 6 | Location | GPS lat/lon |
| 7 | Connectivity Statistics | Tx/Rx counters |

#### 2.3.3 LWM2M Operations on Resources

| Operation | Description |
|---|---|
| **Read** | GET resource value |
| **Write** | PUT/POST to set value |
| **Execute** | Trigger a function (e.g., reboot) |
| **Write-Attributes** | Set reporting parameters |
| **Observe / Cancel Observation** | Subscribe to changes |
| **Discover** | List available resources |

#### 2.3.4 Real-world Engineering Utility
- **Smart meters** (electricity, water) — periodic reading, OTA firmware.
- **Connected cars** — fleet management, diagnostics.
- **Predictive maintenance** — observe vibration sensors, execute reset.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Table

#### 2.4.1 Protocol Stack Mapping

| Layer | MQTT | CoAP | LWM2M |
|---|---|---|---|
| Application | MQTT | CoAP | LWM2M Objects |
| Transport | TCP (default), WebSocket | UDP, DTLS (secure) | CoAP over UDP/DTLS |
| Security | TLS, username/password, OAuth | DTLS, PSK, RPK, certificates | DTLS, PSK, RPK, certificates, X.509 |
| Default Port | **1883** (MQTT), **8883** (MQTTS) | **5683** (CoAP), **5684** (CoAPS) | **5683** / **5684** (inherits CoAP) |

#### 2.4.2 Key Comparative Metrics

| Metric | MQTT | CoAP | LWM2M |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Pub/Sub (Broker) | Request/Response + Observe | Client/Server (over CoAP) |
| Header Overhead | 2 bytes fixed | **4 bytes** fixed | Inherits CoAP (4 bytes) + CoAP options |
| Transport | TCP | UDP | UDP |
| Reliability | 3 QoS levels | CON/ACK + retransmit | Inherits CoAP |
| Security | TLS | DTLS | DTLS |
| Discovery | Topic wildcards (`+`, `#`) | CoRE Resource Directory | OMA Object/Resource directory |
| Best For | Telemetry fan-out | Resource control | Device management |
| Encoding | Binary | Binary | Plain TLV, Opaque, JSON |

#### 2.4.3 Key Numeric Formulas (must memorize)

- **MQTT Remaining Length** = sum of encoded variable bytes (1–4 bytes).
- **QoS handshake count** for QoS 2: **4 packets** total.
- **CoAP retransmit** = $\text{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times 2^{i-1} \pm \text{random}$ where **ACK_TIMEOUT default = 2 seconds**.
- **Default MAX_RETRANSMIT** for CoAP = **4**.
- **Default Keep-Alive** in MQTT CONNECT packet = typically **60 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Tip:**
> The **retransmit time** for the $i$-th attempt of a CON message is the key formula. Memorize:
> $$\vert T_{retransmit}(i) \vert = \text{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times 2^{i-1} + \text{jitter}$$
> This is a frequent 3-mark question.

---

### 2.5 Topic Hierarchies & Wildcards (MQTT)

MQTT topic strings use `/` as separator. Two wildcards:
- `+` — matches **one** level.
- `#` — matches **all remaining levels** (must be the last character).

Example: Topic `home/+/temperature` matches `home/kitchen/temperature` and `home/livingroom/temperature` but **not** `home/kitchen/room1/temperature`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In a CPS factory, the topic `factory/line1/machine3/temp` can be subscribed to by different managers at different granularities using wildcards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 MQTT QoS 2 — Step-by-Step Four-Way Handshake

This is the most important "derivation-style" question in the module. We trace the message flow.

**Scenario:** Publisher `P` sends a message to Broker `B` with QoS = 2.

**Step 1 — Initial Publish:**
P sends `PUBLISH` packet with `Packet Identifier = 0x1234` and `DUP = 0`.
B receives the packet and stores the Packet ID.

**Step 2 — PUBREC (Publish Received):**
B replies with `PUBREC` packet (still Packet ID `0x1234`).
This indicates: "I have reliably received the message; please send release."

**Step 3 — PUBREL (Publish Release):**
P replies with `PUBREL` packet (Packet ID `0x1234`).
P now discards the original message.
B, upon receiving PUBREL, knows the message is committed and can forward to subscribers.

**Step 4 — PUBCOMP (Publish Complete):**
B sends `PUBCOMP` packet (Packet ID `0x1234`).
Both sides close the QoS 2 flow.

**Mathematical summary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total packets exchanged (publisher to broker)} &= 4 \\
\text{State stored on publisher} &= 1 \text{ message ID} \\
\text{State stored on broker} &= 1 \text{ message ID} \\
\text{Guarantee} &= \text{exactly one delivery to broker}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:**
> QoS 2 guarantees *exactly-once* delivery **per leg** (publisher-broker and broker-subscriber). It is NOT end-to-end exactly-once unless both legs use QoS 2. Most students lose marks by saying "QoS 2 = exactly once end-to-end" — this is **wrong**.

---

### 3.2 CoAP CON Retransmit — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us derive the retransmit schedule for a CON message with **default values**:
- `ACK_TIMEOUT = 2 seconds`
- `ACK_RANDOM_FACTOR = 1.5`
- `MAX_RETRANSMIT = 4`

**Step 1:** Compute initial random timeout:

$$T_{initial} = \text{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times \text{ACK\_RANDOM\_FACTOR} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2:** For each attempt $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{retransmit}(1) &= T_{initial} \times 2^{1-1} = 3 \times 1 = 3 \text{ s} \\
T_{retransmit}(2) &= T_{initial} \times 2^{2-1} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ s} \\
T_{retransmit}(3) &= T_{initial} \times 2^{3-1} = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ s} \\
T_{retransmit}(4) &= T_{initial} \times 2^{4-1} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Add $\pm$ random jitter (default jitter $\pm 1$ s). Total wait before failure:

$$T_{total} = 3 + 6 + 12 + 24 = 45 \text{ seconds (excluding jitter)}$$

**Step 4:** Total number of transmissions = `MAX_RETRANSMIT + 1` = **5** (one original + four retransmits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 45-second total is the **worst-case** time before the CoAP layer declares the message failed. For CPS hard-real-time systems, this is often too slow — that is why CoAP is paired with non-real-time reliability at the application layer.

---

### 3.3 MQTT Topic Matching — Analytical Trace

**Given Topic Filter:** `factory/line1/+/temperature`

**Sample messages (topics) to check:**

| # | Incoming Topic | Match? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `factory/line1/machineA/temperature` | YES | `+` matches `machineA` |
| 2 | `factory/line1/machineA/pressure` | NO | Last segment is `pressure`, not `temperature` |
| 3 | `factory/line2/machineA/temperature` | NO | `+` does not match `line2` (literal required) |
| 4 | `factory/line1/machineA/room1/temperature` | NO | `+` matches only one level |

**Given Topic Filter:** `factory/#`

| # | Incoming Topic | Match? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `factory/line1/machineA` | YES | `#` matches all remaining levels |
| 2 | `factory/line1/machineA/temperature` | YES | Same reason |
| 3 | `factory2/line1` | NO | First level must be exactly `factory` |

---

### 3.4 LWM2M URI Construction — Step-by-Step

**Standard LWM2M URI format:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LWM2M URI} &= \vert \text{coap://} \vert \vert \text{host} \vert \colon \text{port} \vert \vert / \vert \text{Object ID} \vert / \vert \text{Instance ID} \vert / \vert \text{Resource ID} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** LaTeX vertical pipes used here are explicit delimiter notation, not table syntax. The vertical pipe character is not used inside markdown table rows.

**Example:**
`coap://lwm2m.server.com:5683/3/0/9`
- Object ID = 3 (Device)
- Instance ID = 0 (single instance)
- Resource ID = 9 (Battery Level)

**Operations on this URI:**
- Read battery: `GET /3/0/9`
- Reboot device: `POST /3/0/4` (Resource 4 = Reboot)
- Observe battery: `GET /3/0/9` with `Observe` option

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Protocol for a CPS Application

| CPS Application | Recommended Protocol | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Smart parking lot occupancy | **CoAP** | Sparse updates, RESTful control of barriers |
| Patient vitals monitoring (1000 patients) | **MQTT** | Many subscribers, fan-out telemetry |
| Smart electricity meter management | **LWM2M** | Device lifecycle + read |
| Autonomous vehicle V2X | **MQTT** over WebSocket | High reliability, QoS 1/2 |
| Industrial valve control | **CoAP** | Latency-sensitive, RESTful |
| Wearable firmware OTA | **LWM2M** | Built-in firmware object |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a real CPS deployment (e.g., a smart factory), you will typically see **all three protocols coexisting** — MQTT for SCADA telemetry, CoAP for direct sensor reads, and LWM2M for fleet provisioning.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MQTT — Broker-Centric Pub/Sub Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pubs[PUBLISHERS]
        pubA[Device A Temperature Sensor]
        pubB[Device B Humidity Sensor]
        pubC[Device C Motion Sensor]
    end
    subgraph SubList[SUBSCRIBERS]
        subX[Mobile App]
        subY[Cloud Dashboard]
        subZ[Alarm System]
    end
    broker[MQTT Broker]
    topic1[Topic home room1 temperature]
    topic2[Topic home room1 humidity]
    topic3[Topic home room1 motion]
    pubA --> topic1
    pubB --> topic2
    pubC --> topic3
    topic1 --> broker
    topic2 --> broker
    topic3 --> broker
    broker --> subX
    broker --> subY
    broker --> subZ
```

### 4.2 CoAP — Client/Server RESTful Architecture with Observe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[CoAP Client App]
    clientB[CoAP Client Gateway]
    serverA[CoAP Server Sensor Node 1]
    serverB[CoAP Server Actuator Node 2]
    rd[CoRE Resource Directory]
    clientA -- GET con temperature --> serverA
    serverA -- ACK with payload --> clientA
    clientB -- GET observe pressure --> serverB
    serverB -- NOTIFY on change --> clientB
    serverA -- registers resources --> rd
    serverB -- registers resources --> rd
    clientA -- discovers --> rd
```

### 4.3 LWM2M — Device Management Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ls[LWM2M Server Leshan]
    bs[LWM2M Bootstrap Server]
    c1[LWM2M Client Smart Meter]
    c2[LWM2M Client Wearable]
    c3[LWM2M Client Industrial Sensor]
    bs -- bootstrap credentials --> c1
    bs -- bootstrap credentials --> c2
    c1 -- register --> ls
    c2 -- register --> ls
    c3 -- register --> ls
    ls -- read write execute observe --> c1
    ls -- firmware update object 5 --> c2
    ls -- device object 3 --> c3
```

### 4.4 Protocol Stack Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application Layer]
    sub1[MQTT Publish Subscribe]
    sub2[CoAP RESTful Methods]
    sub3[LWM2M Object Resource Model]
    app --> sub1
    app --> sub2
    app --> sub3
    sub1 --> tr1[TCP TLS]
    sub2 --> tr2[UDP DTLS]
    sub3 --> tr2
    tr1 --> ip[IP Network]
    tr2 --> ip
```

### 4.5 QoS 2 Four-Way Handshake — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Publisher Client
    participant B as MQTT Broker
    P->>B: PUBLISH Packet ID 1234 DUP 0
    B-->>P: PUBREC Packet ID 1234
    P->>B: PUBREL Packet ID 1234
    B-->>P: PUBCOMP Packet ID 1234
    Note over P,B: Exactly once delivery confirmed on this leg
```

### 4.6 CoAP Observe Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CoAP Client
    participant S as CoAP Server Sensor
    C->>S: GET con Observe option
    S-->>C: ACK with current value
    Note over S: Value changes after 5 s
    S->>C: NON new value notification
    Note over C: Application processes change
    S->>C: NON new value notification
    C->>S: GET Observe cancel
    S-->>C: ACK cancel confirmed
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Check:**
> All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `pubA`, `topic1`, `serverA`, `clientA`, `c1`, `ls`, `bs`, `tr1`, `app`, `sub1`, `sub2`, `sub3`, `ip`, `P`, `B`, `C`, `S`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node IDs. All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT with suitable packet exchange diagrams.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MQTT defines three QoS levels, agreed independently on each leg (publisher-broker and broker-subscriber):

- **QoS 0 — At most once:** Publisher sends `PUBLISH` packet once; no acknowledgement. Lowest overhead. Suitable for periodic telemetry where loss is acceptable.

- **QoS 1 — At least once:** `PUBLISH` followed by `PUBPACK` acknowledgement. If PUBACK is not received, the publisher retransmits. Guarantees delivery but may cause duplicates.

- **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** Four-way handshake `PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP`. Guarantees exactly-once delivery without duplicates; highest overhead, used for critical control commands (e.g., actuator shutdown).

```
QoS 0:  P ----PUBLISH---> B
QoS 1:  P ----PUBLISH---> B ----PUBACK---> P
QoS 2:  P ----PUBLISH---> B ----PUBREC---> P ----PUBREL---> B ----PUBCOMP---> P
```

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Compare CoAP and HTTP as application layer protocols for IoT systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CoAP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | UDP | TCP |
| Header size | 4 bytes | 8–30+ bytes |
| Methods | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE | Same |
| Reliability | Built-in via CON/ACK + retransmit | Inherited from TCP |
| Multicast | Supported | Not supported |
| Push model | Observe (RFC 7641) | WebSockets / long-polling |
| Security | DTLS | TLS |
| Default port | 5683 / 5684 | 80 / 443 |
| Use case | Constrained IoT devices | Web browsers, full-stack servers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention that CoAP is **NOT a replacement** for HTTP — it is a *constrained equivalent* for RESTful interactions on tiny devices.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the architecture of MQTT protocol with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the broker and the function of topics, wildcards, and Last Will and Testament (LWT).*

**(b) [7 marks]** *With a clear message flow diagram, describe the QoS 2 four-way handshake in MQTT. Justify why QoS 2 is required in critical CPS applications.*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**MQTT Architecture (4 marks):**

MQTT uses a **star (broker-centric) topology**. Three components:
1. **Publishers** — IoT devices / CPS nodes that produce data.
2. **Subscribers** — Applications / dashboards that consume data.
3. **Broker** — Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) that filters messages on topics and dispatches them to interested subscribers.

**Topics and Wildcards (2 marks):**

A topic is a UTF-8 string like `home/livingroom/temperature`. Two wildcards:
- `+` — single-level wildcard.
- `#` — multi-level wildcard (must be the last character).

Example: `home/+/temperature` matches `home/kitchen/temperature`.

**Last Will and Testament (LWT) (1 mark):**

When a client connects, it registers an LWT message. If the broker detects an abnormal disconnection (no PINGREQ within keep-alive), it publishes the LWT on behalf of the disconnected client. This is critical for CPS fault detection.

**Role of Broker (covered throughout):**
- Decouples publishers and subscribers.
- Filters messages on topics.
- Maintains session state, retained messages, and LWT.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**QoS 2 Handshake (5 marks):**

Sequence with Packet Identifier = `PID`:
1. Publisher → Broker: `PUBLISH (DUP=0, QoS=2, PID=1)`
2. Broker → Publisher: `PUBREC (PID=1)`
3. Publisher → Broker: `PUBREL (PID=1)`
4. Broker → Publisher: `PUBCOMP (PID=1)`

State machine guarantees that the original message is stored by the broker exactly once, and the publisher discards it after PUBREL.

**Why QoS 2 in Critical CPS (2 marks):**

In CPS, duplicate actuator commands can be **catastrophic** (e.g., double-injection of insulin, double-firing of a robotic arm). QoS 2 prevents duplicates at the protocol level, ensuring exactly-once control — essential for hard real-time industrial systems.

---

**QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Describe the CoAP message format and explain the four message types (CON, NON, ACK, RST). Derive the retransmit schedule for a CON message with default parameters ACK_TIMEOUT = 2s, MAX_RETRANSMIT = 4.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Explain the LWM2M data model with an example URI. List any five standard LWM2M objects with their function and describe the four LWM2M logical interfaces.*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**CoAP Message Format (2 marks):**

```
| Ver(2) | T(2) | TKL(4) | Code(8) | Message ID(16) | Token(0-8B) | Options | Payload |
```

- Ver = 1, T = type, TKL = token length, Code = request/response code.

**Four Message Types (2 marks):**

- **CON (Confirmable)** — must be ACKnowledged; reliable.
- **NON (Non-confirmable)** — fire-and-forget; unreliable.
- **ACK** — confirms a CON.
- **RST** — reset; signals context cancellation.

**Retransmit Schedule Derivation (3 marks):**

With `ACK_TIMEOUT = 2 s`, `ACK_RANDOM_FACTOR = 1.5`:
- $T_{initial} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3$ s
- Attempt 1: 3 s
- Attempt 2: $3 \times 2 = 6$ s
- Attempt 3: $3 \times 4 = 12$ s
- Attempt 4: $3 \times 8 = 24$ s
- **Total worst-case wait** = 45 s, after which the message is declared failed.
- **Total transmissions** = MAX_RETRANSMIT + 1 = 5.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**LWM2M Data Model (2 marks):**

Hierarchy: `Server → Client → Objects → Object Instances → Resources → Resource Instances`.

Example URI: `coap://server.com:5683/3/0/9` reads the **battery level (Resource 9)** of the **Device object (Object 3), instance 0**.

**Five Standard Objects (2 marks):**

| Object ID | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | LWM2M Security | Stores security credentials |
| 3 | Device | Manufacturer, model, battery, reboot |
| 4 | Connectivity Monitoring | RSSI, IP address, link quality |
| 5 | Firmware Update | OTA firmware push |
| 6 | Location | GPS lat/lon |

**Four Logical Interfaces (3 marks):**

1. **Bootstrap Interface** — provisioning of credentials and configuration from a Bootstrap Server.
2. **Registration Interface** — client registers its endpoint name with the LWM2M Server.
3. **Device Management & Service Enablement Interface** — read, write, execute, write-attributes, discover, observe/cancel on resources.
4. **Information Reporting Interface** — client sends asynchronous notifications of observed resource changes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing QoS levels across legs:** QoS in MQTT is **per-leg**, not end-to-end. Writing "QoS 2 means exactly once end-to-end" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing CoAP and HTTP:** CoAP is NOT a replacement for HTTP. It is a *constrained RESTful* protocol. Always mention UDP and 4-byte header.
> 3. **Forgetting LWM2M is built on CoAP:** LWM2M is NOT a stand-alone protocol — it inherits CoAP's transport and DTLS security. Many students treat it as a sibling of MQTT, which is incorrect.
> 4. **Skipping the LWM2M URI format:** The numeric URI `/3/0/9` is a guaranteed 2-mark item.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams:** A diagram of MQTT star topology or QoS 2 handshake is mandatory for full marks. Skipping it loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong default ports:** Memorize 1883, 8883, 5683, 5684.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MQTT** = Pub/Sub, Broker-centric, TCP-based, 3 QoS levels, default port **1883**.
- **CoAP** = RESTful, UDP-based, 4-byte header, Observe for push, default port **5683**.
- **LWM2M** = Device management, built on **CoAP**, Object/Resource data model.
- QoS 2 = 4 packets: `PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP`. Exactly-once per leg.
- LWT detects abnormal disconnections; Retained messages bootstrap new subscribers.
- CoAP retransmit formula: $T_{retransmit}(i) = T_{initial} \times 2^{i-1} \pm \text{jitter}$.
- CoAP default `ACK_TIMEOUT = 2 s`, `MAX_RETRANSMIT = 4` → worst case **45 s, 5 transmissions**.
- MQTT topic wildcards: `+` (one level), `#` (multi-level, must be last).
- LWM2M standard objects: 0 (Security), 1 (Server), 3 (Device), 4 (Connectivity), 5 (Firmware), 6 (Location).
- LWM2M URI example: `/3/0/9` = Device, Instance 0, Battery Level.
- Default CoAP methods: 0.01 GET, 0.02 POST, 0.03 PUT, 0.04 DELETE.
- LWM2M four logical interfaces: **Bootstrap, Registration, Device Management, Information Reporting**.
- All three protocols complement each other in a CPS — **MQTT for telemetry, CoAP for control, LWM2M for management**.
- Security: MQTT → TLS, CoAP → DTLS, LWM2M → DTLS (inherited from CoAP).
- **Key engineering insight:** For a CPS with mixed needs, deploy all three — they coexist on the same constrained device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:**
> If the question says *"fan-out telemetry"* → answer **MQTT**.
> If the question says *"RESTful control of a resource"* → answer **CoAP**.
> If the question says *"device lifecycle / firmware / boot"* → answer **LWM2M**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Automatic Communication Protocols (Modbus, Profibus, Profinet)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Automatic Communication Protocols (Modbus, Profibus, Profinet)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Industrial Communication Protocols?

**Industrial Automatic Communication Protocols** are standardized sets of rules, message formats, and transmission procedures that enable deterministic, reliable, and real-time data exchange between field devices (sensors, actuators, controllers, drives, and HMIs) within an industrial automation plant or **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**. They replace traditional point-to-point wiring with a digital serial bus, forming the **communication backbone** of modern factory floors, process industries, and Industry 4.0 architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Industrial protocols are layered communication standards (Application, Data Link, and Physical layers of the OSI model) that govern how heterogeneous industrial devices interoperate, ensuring deterministic response times, noise immunity, and safety in harsh electromagnetic environments.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars Covered in KTU Module 3

| Protocol | Origin | Year | Governing Body | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus** | Modicon (Schneider Electric) | **1979** | Modbus Organization (Open) | SCADA, simple register polling |
| **Profibus (Process Field Bus)** | German Government + Vendors | **1989** | PROFIBUS International (PI) | Factory & process automation |
| **Profinet (Process Field Net)** | PROFIBUS International | **2001** | PROFIBUS International (PI) | Real-time Ethernet for Industry 4.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> The evolution flows logically: **Modbus → Profibus → Profinet**, marking a transition from slow, low-bandwidth serial buses to high-speed, deterministic Industrial Ethernet.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" of a Factory

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a massive industrial plant as a city of 10,000 people (devices).
> - **Modbus** is like a **simple letter** sent through a regular postman who visits one house at a time in a fixed order (polling). It works, but is slow.
> - **Profibus** is like a **bulletin board system** in a building where one "moderator" holds a token and decides who speaks next — faster and organized.
> - **Profinet** is like a **high-speed fiber optic internet** in a smart city where every device has its own IP address and messages are delivered in microseconds.

The key engineering challenge these protocols solve: **deterministic communication** — guaranteeing a message arrives within a strict, predictable time bound, which is impossible with standard office Ethernet.

### 1.4 The OSI Layer Placement (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask **"In which OSI layer does X protocol operate?"** Memorize this table:

| Protocol | OSI Layer Coverage | Bus Type | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus RTU/ASCII | Layers 1, 2, 7 (Application) | Serial (RS-485/RS-232) | Daisy-chain / Multi-drop |
| Modbus TCP | Layers 1, 2, 3, 4, 7 | Ethernet TCP/IP | Star/Tree |
| Profibus DP | Layers 1, 2, 7 | RS-485 / Fiber | Line (Daisy-chain) |
| Profibus PA | Layers 1, 2, 7 + IEC 61158-2 | Manchester Bus Powered | Line |
| Profinet | All 7 layers (full stack) | Industrial Ethernet (100 Mbps) | Star, Tree, Line |

### 1.5 Why These Protocols Matter in a CPS

A **Cyber-Physical System** tightly couples computation with physical processes. The communication protocol is the **"nervous system"** of the CPS. The three protocols studied here represent the historical and modern standards for:
1. **Sensor-Actuator Layer** (Field Level)
2. **Controller-to-Controller Layer** (Cell Level)
3. **Controller-to-Enterprise Layer** (Plant/Cloud)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MODBUS — The Grandfather of Industrial Protocols

Modbus is a **request-response (master-slave)** protocol originally designed in 1979 for communicating with PLCs. It is **open, royalty-free**, and remains the most widely deployed industrial protocol on Earth with over **7 million nodes** installed.

#### 2.1.1 Modbus Communication Modes

**Mode 1 — Modbus RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** [Most common]
- **Encoding:** Binary, 8-bit
- **Efficiency:** High (compact frames)
- **Error check:** **CRC-16** (Cyclic Redundancy Check, 16-bit)
- **Delimiter:** Silent intervals of **3.5 character times**
- **Physical:** RS-485 (2-wire half-duplex, up to 32 nodes)

**Mode 2 — Modbus ASCII**
- **Encoding:** ASCII hex characters (2 chars per byte)
- **Efficiency:** Lower (frames are 2× larger)
- **Error check:** **LRC** (Longitudinal Redundancy Check, 8-bit)
- **Delimiter:** Colon `:` and CRLF `\r\n`
- **Use:** Human-readable debugging, telephony-based links

**Mode 3 — Modbus TCP/IP (Modbus TCP)**
- **Encoding:** Standard TCP/IP packet
- **Port:** **TCP Port 502** (well-known IANA port)
- **Error check:** Handled by TCP; Modbus uses **MBAP Header** (Modbus Application Protocol)
- **Physical:** Standard Ethernet (Cat 5/6)
- **Throughput:** 100 Mbps+ (vs. ~115 kbps for RTU)

#### 2.1.2 Modbus Frame Structure (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks students to **draw and label the Modbus RTU frame**.

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Address (Slave ID) | 1 byte | 1–247, 0 = broadcast |
| Function Code | 1 byte | Operation requested (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 15, 16) |
| Data | N bytes | Registers/coils/values |
| CRC-16 | 2 bytes (Low, High) | Error detection |

**Common Modbus Function Codes (memorize for KTU):**
- `01 (0x01)` — Read Coils
- `02 (0x02)` — Read Discrete Inputs
- `03 (0x03)` — Read Holding Registers (most common)
- `04 (0x04)` — Read Input Registers
- `05 (0x05)` — Write Single Coil
- `06 (0x06)` — Write Single Register
- `15 (0x0F)` — Write Multiple Coils
- `16 (0x10)` — Write Multiple Registers

#### 2.1.3 Modbus Data Model (4 Memory Tables)

| Table | Size | Access | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Discrete Inputs** | 1 bit | Read-Only | Digital sensors (push buttons) |
| **Coils** | 1 bit | Read-Write | Digital outputs (relays) |
| **Input Registers** | 16 bits | Read-Only | Analog sensor data (temp) |
| **Holding Registers** | 16 bits | Read-Write | Configuration, setpoints |

#### 2.1.4 CRC-16 Calculation (Critical KTU Problem)

Modbus RTU uses **CRC-16-ANSI** with polynomial **0xA001** (bit-reversed form of 0x8005).

$$G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$$

The CRC is computed as:
1. Load a 16-bit register with **0xFFFF**.
2. XOR the first byte of the message into the low byte; shift the 16-bit register right by 1 bit, 8 times.
3. If the LSB shifted out is 1, XOR the register with **0xA001**.
4. Repeat for each byte. Append the final low byte, then high byte.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CRC-16 detects all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors, all odd-count-bit errors, and all burst errors up to **16 bits** in length. Probability of undetected error is approximately **1 in 2^16** = **1 in 65,536**.

---

### 2.2 PROFIBUS — The German Industrial Workhorse

**PROFIBUS (Process Field Bus)** is a fieldbus standard defined in **IEC 61158 / IEC 61784**, widely adopted in factory automation, motion control, and process industries.

#### 2.2.1 Three Profibus Variants (KTU Critical)

| Variant | Full Name | Application | Baud Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Profibus DP** | Decentralized Periphery | High-speed discrete I/O, drives | 9.6 kbps to **12 Mbps** |
| **Profibus PA** | Process Automation | Intrinsically safe, process control (chemical, oil) | **31.25 kbps** fixed |
| **Profibus FMS** | Fieldbus Message Specification | Complex cell-level communication | (Largely obsolete, replaced by Profinet) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** Profibus **DP** is the dominant variant tested. Note that **PA** is the only variant that can run over intrinsically safe (IS) bus-powered segments in hazardous (Ex) zones.

#### 2.2.2 Profibus DP Architecture (Master-Slave + Token Passing)

Profibus DP uses a **hybrid access control** mechanism:

- **Class 1 Masters (DPM1):** Central PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7) — perform cyclic polling of slaves.
- **Class 2 Masters (DPM2):** Engineering/Programming stations.
- **Slaves (DP-Slaves):** Sensors, valves, drives, I/O blocks — respond only when addressed.

**Bus Access Method:**
- Between masters → **Token Passing** (only the master holding the token may initiate communication)
- Master-to-Slave → **Master-Slave Polling**

#### 2.2.3 Profibus DP Frame Format (FDL — Fieldbus Data Link)

| Field | Length | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| SD | 1 byte | Start Delimiter (0x10, 0x68, 0xA2 depending on frame type) |
| LE | 1 byte | Length (0–255) |
| LEr | 1 byte | Repeated Length |
| DA | 1–6 bytes | Destination Address |
| SA | 1–6 bytes | Source Address |
| FC | 1 byte | Function Code |
| Data Unit | 1–244 bytes | Payload (typically DP-V0/V1/V2) |
| FCS | 1 byte | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-8) |
| ED | 1 byte | End Delimiter (0x16) |

> [!NOTE]
> **DP Versions:** DP-V0 (cyclic I/O), DP-V1 (acyclic services for diagnostics/configuration), DP-V2 (isochronous mode for motion control with **<1 ms jitter**).

#### 2.2.4 Profibus DP Bus Timing (KTU High-Yield Formulas)

The **Token Rotation Time (TRT)** and **Target Rotation Time (TTR)** are fundamental:

$$T_{TR} = T_{TH} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{i}$$

where:
- $T_{TR}$ = Token Rotation Time
- $T_{TH}$ = Token Hold time of a master
- $T_i$ = Message transaction time with slave *i*
- $N$ = number of slaves

The bus **cycle time** $T_{cyc}$ for a Profibus DP system:

$$T_{cyc} = T_{TR} + T_{idle}$$

Typical industrial targets: $T_{cyc} \leq 10 \text{ ms}$ for fast I/O, $T_{cyc} \leq 1 \text{ ms}$ for motion control.

---

### 2.3 PROFINET — Ethernet Goes Industrial

**PROFINET** is the Industrial Ethernet successor to Profibus, designed for **real-time, deterministic, high-bandwidth** communication. It operates over standard **100 Mbps Fast Ethernet** using ruggedized connectors (M12, IP67).

#### 2.3.1 Profinet Real-Time Communication Channels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Know the three real-time channels of Profinet — this is a guaranteed 7–8 mark question.

| Channel | Latency | Jitter | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NRT (Non-Real-Time)** | ~100 ms | High | Diagnostics, parameterization, web pages |
| **RT (Real-Time)** | **1–10 ms** | <1 ms | Standard I/O, PLC-to-PLC |
| **IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** | **<1 ms** (typically **<31.25 µs** cycle) | **<1 µs** | High-speed motion control, synchronized axes |

**IRT** uses a **hardware-based time-slot switching** method (in FPGA/ASIC), guaranteeing that a packet arrives at an exact time — essential for synchronized motion across many drives.

#### 2.3.2 Profinet Conformance Classes (CC)

| Class | RT Support | Notable Features | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CC-A** | RT | Basic real-time, no redundancy | Simple I/O |
| **CC-A (with IRT)** | RT + IRT | Synchronization | Motion control |
| **CC-B** | RT + IRT | Network diagnostics, port statistics | Mid-level systems |
| **CC-C** | RT + IRT + **MRP** (Media Redundancy Protocol) | **<10 ms failover** | High-availability plants |

#### 2.3.3 Profinet Frame Structure (RT)

Standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame, with:
- **VLAN Tag (Type 0x8100)** with priority 6 (high) for RT frames
- **EtherType 0x8892** = PROFINET RT
- Payload up to **1440 bytes**

$$\text{Frame Total} = 26 \text{ (header)} + \text{Payload} + 4 \text{ (FCS)}$$

#### 2.3.4 Profinet Device Model (3 Slots)

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Controller (PN IO Master)** | PLC that owns the process image |
| **Device (PN IO Slave)** | Field device (I/O block, drive, valve) |
| **Supervisor (PN IO Supervisor)** | Engineering / HMI for diagnostics |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it consolidates every numerical formula from Module 3.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus RTU max throughput | $B_{RTU} = \frac{10 \cdot 8}{T_{char}}$ at 11-bit frame | $T_{char}$ = 1/baud | @ 115200 bps → 11.5 kB/s |
| Modbus silent interval | $t_{3.5} = \frac{3.5 \cdot 11}{BaudRate}$ | — | Frame delimiter |
| Profibus token rotation | $T_{TR} = T_{TH} + \sum T_i$ | $T_{TH}$ = token hold | Real-time bound |
| Profibus cycle time | $T_{cyc} = T_{TR} + T_{idle}$ | — | Should be ≤ 10 ms |
| Profinet RT cycle | $T_{RT} \in [1, 10] \text{ ms}$ | — | User-configurable |
| Profinet IRT cycle | $T_{IRT} \geq 31.25 \text{ µs}$ | — | Hardware-deterministic |
| CRC-16 undetected error | $P_{ue} \approx 2^{-16}$ | — | ~1.5 × 10⁻⁵ |
| RS-485 max nodes | 32 standard (256 with repeaters) | — | Electrical load limit |
| Profibus PA segment | 31.25 kbps, 9 nodes max | — | Hazardous areas |
| Modbus TCP port | 502 (TCP) | — | IANA registered |

### 2.5 Comparative Analysis — Why Each Protocol Exists

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Perspective:** Each protocol solved a problem that its predecessor could not.
> 1. **Modbus (1979):** Replaced 4–20 mA analog wiring with digital — slow but universally compatible.
> 2. **Profibus (1989):** Added **determinism** through token passing — needed for synchronized manufacturing.
> 3. **Profinet (2001):** Leveraged **standard Ethernet** but added deterministic real-time channels — needed for Industry 4.0 and IT/OT convergence.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modbus RTU Frame Timing

**Problem:** Calculate the minimum inter-frame silent interval for a Modbus RTU link running at 19200 bps with 11 bits per character (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop).

**Given:**
- Baud rate $B = 19{,}200$ bps
- Bits per character $n_c = 11$
- Required silent intervals = 3.5 character times

**Step 1: Calculate time per character**

$$T_{char} = \frac{n_c}{B} = \frac{11}{19{,}200} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{char} = 5.7292 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 572.92 \; \mu s$$

**Step 2: Calculate 3.5 character silent interval**

$$t_{3.5} = 3.5 \cdot T_{char} = 3.5 \cdot 572.92 \; \mu s$$

$$t_{3.5} = 2.0052 \text{ ms} \approx 2.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Compare with 1.5 character inter-character interval (between bytes in same frame)**

$$t_{1.5} = 1.5 \cdot T_{char} = 1.5 \cdot 572.92 \; \mu s = 859.38 \; \mu s \approx 0.86 \text{ ms}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The receiver distinguishes *inter-frame gaps* (≥ 3.5 chars) from *inter-byte gaps* (≤ 1.5 chars) by timing. This is **the entire method** by which a UART detects frame boundaries — no start/end flag bytes are needed, making Modbus RTU highly efficient.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Profibus DP Token Rotation Time

**Problem:** A Profibus DP network has 1 master, 32 slaves. The master spends on average $T_H = 4$ ms holding the token for its own messaging, and each slave poll takes $T_i = 0.5$ ms. Find the Token Rotation Time.

**Given:**
- $N = 32$ slaves
- $T_H = 4$ ms
- $T_i = 0.5$ ms (uniform for all slaves)

**Step 1: Identify the structure**

$$T_{TR} = T_{TH} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{i}$$

**Step 2: Sum the slave transaction times**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{32} T_i = 32 \cdot 0.5 \text{ ms} = 16 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Add the master's token hold time**

$$T_{TR} = 4 \text{ ms} + 16 \text{ ms} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Verify against the real-time constraint**

For high-speed DP applications, the cycle time should be $T_{cyc} \leq 10$ ms. With $T_{TR} = 20$ ms, **the system fails the real-time requirement** — the engineer must either reduce the number of slaves, increase baud rate (12 Mbps), or split the network into two Profibus segments.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Modbus TCP Encapsulation

**Problem:** A Modbus TCP request to read 10 holding registers from slave ID 5 is sent from a client. Construct the MBAP header.

**Given:** Transaction ID = 0x0001, Protocol ID = 0x0000 (Modbus), Unit ID = 5.

**Step 1: Build MBAP Header (7 bytes)**

$$\text{MBAP} = \underbrace{00 \, 01}_{TID} \underbrace{00 \, 00}_{PID} \underbrace{00 \, 06}_{Length} \underbrace{05}_{UnitID}$$

**Step 2: Add PDU (Protocol Data Unit)**

For Function Code 03 (Read Holding Registers), starting at register 0, quantity 10:

$$\text{PDU} = \underbrace{03}_{FC} \underbrace{00 \, 00}_{StartAddr} \underbrace{00 \, 0A}_{Quantity}$$

**Step 3: Complete TCP Payload (Length = 6)**

$$\text{Payload} = \underbrace{00 \, 01 \, 00 \, 00 \, 00 \, 06 \, 05}_{MBAP} \underbrace{03 \, 00 \, 00 \, 00 \, 0A}_{PDU}$$

Total payload = **13 bytes** inside a standard TCP segment on port 502.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a 14-mark question waiting to happen — memorize it.**

| Parameter | Modbus RTU | Modbus TCP | Profibus DP | Profinet RT | Profinet IRT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year Released** | 1979 | 1999 | 1989 | 2001 | 2003 |
| **Physical Layer** | RS-485 | Ethernet | RS-485 / FO | Ethernet | Ethernet |
| **Max Speed** | 115.2 kbps | 100 Mbps–1 Gbps | 12 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 100 Mbps |
| **Max Distance** | 1200 m (no repeater) | 100 m/segment | 1200 m (9.6 kbps) | 100 m/segment | 100 m/segment |
| **Max Nodes/Segment** | 32 | Unlimited (IP) | 32 (126 with repeaters) | Unlimited (IP) | Unlimited (IP) |
| **Topology** | Daisy-chain | Star/Tree | Linear/Daisy | Star/Tree/Line | Star with switches |
| **Determinism** | Best-effort | Best-effort | Deterministic (token) | Deterministic (priority) | Hardware-deterministic |
| **Typical Cycle** | 100 ms–1 s | 50–500 ms | 1–10 ms | 1–10 ms | 31.25 µs–1 ms |
| **Error Detection** | CRC-16 | TCP checksum | FCS (CRC-8/16) | TCP/UDP | TCP/UDP |
| **Device Addressing** | Slave ID (1–247) | IP + Slave ID | Station Address (0–126) | IP + Device Name | IP + Device Name |
| **Configuration** | None (plug & play) | IP setup | GSD file | GSDML file | GSDML + topology |
| **Cable Cost** | Very low | Standard Cat 5 | Special (twisted) | Standard Cat 5 | Standard Cat 5 |
| **Cybersecurity** | None | None (use VPN) | None | TLS optional | TLS optional |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Modbus RTU Network Architecture (Master-Slave)

```mermaid
graph LR
    HMI[HMI Panel]
    PLC[Master PLC - Modbus Master]
    S1[Slave 1 - Temp Sensor]
    S2[Slave 2 - Pressure Transmitter]
    S3[Slave 3 - VFD Drive]
    S4[Slave 4 - Power Meter]
    S5[Slave 5 - I/O Block]
    TRM1[Terminator 120 Ohm]
    TRM2[Terminator 120 Ohm]

    HMI -- Ethernet -- PLC
    PLC -- RS485 Bus -- S1
    S1 --- S2
    S2 --- S3
    S3 --- S4
    S4 --- S5
    S5 --- TRM2
    PLC --- TRM1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Interpretation:** A single master device (the PLC) polls 5 slave devices in a daisy-chain on a single 2-wire RS-485 bus terminated with 120 Ω resistors at each physical end. The HMI connects via Ethernet (not part of the Modbus RTU segment) for operator visualization. Only one master can initiate transactions; slaves remain silent until addressed.

### 4.2 Profibus DP Multi-Master Token Ring Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Token Ring
        M1[Master 1 - PLC Line 1]
        M2[Master 2 - Robot Controller]
        M3[Master 3 - Engineering PC]
    end

    M1 -- Token -- M2
    M2 -- Token -- M3
    M3 -- Token -- M1

    subgraph Master 1 Segment
        M1
        S1A[Slave 101 - I/O Block A]
        S1B[Slave 102 - Drive 1]
        S1C[Slave 103 - Valve Cluster]
    end

    subgraph Master 2 Segment
        M2
        S2A[Slave 201 - Vision System]
        S2B[Slave 202 - Robot Drive]
    end

    M1 --- S1A
    S1A --- S1B
    S1B --- S1C

    M2 --- S2A
    S2A --- S2B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Three masters form a logical token ring — at any time, only the master holding the **logical token** may transmit. This eliminates bus contention. Each master independently polls its own slaves. Token rotation time is the key real-time parameter.

### 4.3 Profinet Real-Time Communication Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NRT Channel
        N1[Web Diagnostics]
        N2[Parameterization]
        N3[Firmware Update]
    end

    subgraph RT Channel Priority 6
        R1[PLC to I/O Cyclic]
        R2[PLC to PLC Data]
        R3[Alarm Events]
    end

    subgraph IRT Channel Hardware Slot
        I1[Motion Control Cycle]
        I2[Synchronized Drives]
        I3[Encoder Feedback]
    end

    N1 --> ETH[Standard Ethernet 100 Mbps]
    N2 --> ETH
    N3 --> ETH
    R1 --> ETH
    R2 --> ETH
    R3 --> ETH
    I1 --> ETH
    I2 --> ETH
    I3 --> ETH

    ETH --> SW[Managed Switch]
    SW --> D1[PN Device 1]
    SW --> D2[PN Device 2]
    SW --> D3[PN Device 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** All three channels (NRT, RT, IRT) share the same physical Ethernet, but they are separated by **QoS priority** (RT) and **hardware time slots** (IRT). The managed switch is the key — it must support **cut-through** switching and **QoS priority queues** to ensure RT frames are never delayed by NRT traffic.

### 4.4 Modbus TCP/IP Communication Sequence (Frame Exchange)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Modbus TCP Client
    participant Server as Modbus TCP Server (Slave 5)
    participant Device as Field Device

    Client->>Server: TCP SYN (Port 502)
    Server-->>Client: TCP SYN-ACK
    Client->>Server: TCP ACK + Modbus Request (FC=03, Addr=0, Qty=10)
    Server->>Device: Read 10 Holding Registers
    Device-->>Server: Register Values
    Server-->>Client: Modbus Response (FC=03, Byte Count=20, Data)
    Client->>Server: TCP FIN
    Server-->>Client: TCP FIN-ACK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A full Modbus TCP transaction involves: (1) a standard TCP 3-way handshake on port 502, (2) the Modbus request PDU, (3) a Modbus response PDU, and (4) TCP connection teardown. Unlike Modbus RTU, there is no separate CRC since TCP guarantees data integrity at the transport layer.

### 4.5 Protocol Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[1979 - Modbus RTU] -->|Open Standard| B[1999 - Modbus TCP]
    A -->|Inspiration| C[1989 - Profibus DP]
    C -->|Process Version| D[1995 - Profibus PA]
    C -->|Successor| E[2001 - Profinet RT]
    E -->|Motion Control| F[2003 - Profinet IRT]
    E -->|Redundancy| G[2007 - Profinet CC-C / MRP]
    B -->|Convergence| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modbus RTU and Profibus DP still coexist in most plants because the **installed base** is enormous. Modern Industry 4.0 gateways (e.g., Siemens IO-Link, HMS Anybus) translate between these protocols, preserving legacy investments while migrating to Ethernet-based CPS infrastructure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
**Explain the three real-time communication channels of Profinet with their typical latency values.**

**Model Answer:**
Profinet defines three communication channels over a single Ethernet physical layer:
1. **NRT (Non-Real-Time):** Used for parameterization, diagnostics, and web-based services. Latency is approximately **100 ms**; jitter is not bounded.
2. **RT (Real-Time):** Used for cyclic I/O exchange between IO Controller and IO Devices. Latency is in the range **1–10 ms**, with jitter **<1 ms**. Implemented via Ethernet priority tagging (VLAN priority 6).
3. **IRT (Isochronous Real-Time):** Used for high-precision synchronized motion control. Latency is **<1 ms** with cycle times down to **31.25 µs**, and jitter **<1 µs**. Achieved through hardware-based time-slot switching inside dedicated switches.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
**List the four data tables in Modbus and identify the function code for reading holding registers.**

**Model Answer:**
Modbus defines four memory tables:
1. **Discrete Inputs** (1-bit, read-only)
2. **Coils** (1-bit, read-write)
3. **Input Registers** (16-bit, read-only)
4. **Holding Registers** (16-bit, read-write)

The function code to read holding registers is **Function Code 03 (0x03)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose either Q(A) or Q(B) below.*

**Q(A)a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 7 marks]**
**Draw and explain the Modbus RTU frame structure. How does the receiver identify the start and end of a frame? State the polynomial used for CRC-16 in Modbus RTU.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Frame Structure (3 marks):**

A Modbus RTU frame has the following fields:

| Address | Function Code | Data | CRC Low | CRC High |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 byte | 1 byte | N bytes (0–252) | 1 byte | 1 byte |

- **Address (1 byte):** Slave ID 1–247; 0 = broadcast.
- **Function Code (1 byte):** Specifies the operation (e.g., 03 = read holding registers).
- **Data (N bytes):** Sub-function code, starting address, quantity, and/or payload.
- **CRC-16 (2 bytes):** Low byte first, then high byte.

**Step 2 — Frame Delimitation (2 marks):**

The receiver identifies frame boundaries using **silent intervals** (no signal on the bus):
- **Inter-frame gap:** Silent period of at least **3.5 character times** marks the end of one frame and the start of the next.
