<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between throughput and bandwidth. Why is effective throughput always less than or equal to the channel capacity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bandwidth** is a property of the *physical medium* — the range of frequencies it can pass, measured in **Hz**. It is a *resource*, not a rate of data flow. (1 mark)
- **Throughput** is the *actual rate* of useful data successfully delivered to the destination, measured in **bps**. (1 mark)
- **Effective throughput** $\le$ capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ because of protocol overhead (headers, ACKs, retransmissions), idle times, and collisions. Shannon's $C$ is a theoretical upper bound; practical systems operate well below it. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four components of end-to-end latency. Which one is stochastic and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Transmission delay $L_{\text{trans}} = L/R_b$ (deterministic for fixed $L$, $R_b$) (0.5 mark)
2. Propagation delay $L_{\text{prop}} = d/v_p$ (deterministic for fixed $d$, $v_p$) (0.5 mark)
3. Processing delay $L_{\text{proc}}$ (mostly deterministic, small jitter) (0.5 mark)
4. **Queuing delay $L_{\text{queue}}$** — *stochastic* because it depends on **random** traffic arrival patterns and contention at intermediate nodes, modeled using queueing theory (M/M/1, M/D/1). (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`**

(a) **Explain the four components of latency in CPS communication with suitable equations. For an industrial CPS with link length 500 m, bit rate 100 Mbps, and packet size 1500 bytes, compute the transmission and propagation delay. Assume propagation speed $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.** (7 marks)

(b) **A wireless CPS sensor link operates with BER $= 10^{-5}$ and packet length $n = 8000$ bits. Compute the packet error rate (PER) exactly and using the small-error approximation. If Stop-and-Wait ARQ is used with $R_b = 1$ Mbps, $L = 8000$ bits, and $t_{\text{prop}} = 5$ ms, find the effective throughput. Comment on the impact of PER on throughput.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

### (a) Solution — Latency Components and Numerical Computation

**Four components:**

(i) **Transmission delay** — time to push all bits of the packet onto the wire:
$$L_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L_{\text{packet}}}{R_b}$$

(ii) **Propagation delay** — time for one bit to travel the physical distance:
$$L_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_p}$$

(iii) **Processing delay** — time for routers/gateways to examine the header, compute CRC, decide next hop.

(iv) **Queuing delay** — waiting time in buffers at intermediate nodes.

$$L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{queue}}$$

**Numerical computation:**

$$L_{\text{packet}} = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}$$
$$L_{\text{trans}} = \frac{12{,}000}{100 \times 10^6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 120 \; \mu\text{s}$$
$$L_{\text{prop}} = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.5 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Observation:** For this link, $L_{\text{trans}} \gg L_{\text{prop}}$ (by factor 48). The link is **bandwidth-limited**, not distance-limited. (1 mark for this insight)

### (b) Solution — PER and Effective Throughput

**Step 1 — Compute PER exactly:**

$$\text{PER} = 1 - (1 - 10^{-5})^{8000} = 1 - (0.99999)^{8000}$$

$$\text{PER} = 1 - e^{8000 \cdot \ln(0.99999)} = 1 - e^{8000 \cdot (-10^{-5})} = 1 - e^{-0.08}$$

$$\text{PER} = 1 - 0.9231 = 0.0769 \approx 7.69\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute PER by approximation:**

$$\text{PER}_{\text{approx}} \approx n \cdot \text{BER} = 8000 \times 10^{-5} = 0.08 = 8.00\%$$

**Error in approximation:** $\vert 8.00 - 7.69 \vert / 7.69 \approx 4\%$, which is acceptable when $n \cdot \text{BER} < 0.1$.

**Step 3 — Effective throughput with Stop-and-Wait ARQ:**

$$T_f = \frac{L}{R_b} = \frac{8000}{10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms}$$

$$E[T_{\text{succ}}] = \frac{T_f + 2 t_{\text{prop}}}{1 - \text{PER}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3} + 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 - 0.0769}$$

$$E[T_{\text{succ}}] = \frac{0.008 + 0.010}{0.9231} = \frac{0.018}{0.9231} = 0.0195 \text{ s} = 19.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{\text{eff}} = \frac{L}{E[T_{\text{succ}}]} = \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{0.0195 \text{ s}} = 410{,}256 \text{ bps} \approx 410 \text{ kbps}$$

**Comment:** Even though the raw link rate is 1 Mbps, the effective throughput drops to **41% of capacity** because of (a) high PER (7.69%) and (b) high propagation time (5 ms) that penalizes Stop-and-Wait ARQ. In CPS, this throughput loss is often **acceptable** because reliability is paramount. To improve, one may switch to **Selective Repeat ARQ** or add **FEC**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Students often compute PER using $n \cdot \text{BER}$ only, even when $n \cdot \text{BER} > 0.1$. The exact formula $1 - (1-\text{BER})^n$ is mandatory for $n \cdot \text{BER} > 0.1$. **Always state the validity condition.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Sample Paper]`**

(a) **Define throughput-delay product (BDP). A 1 Gbps link connects two CPS gateways 2000 km apart. Compute the BDP and explain its significance for buffer sizing. Propagation speed $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.** (7 marks)

(b) **A CAN bus in an automotive CPS has $R_b = 500$ kbps and is loaded to 70% utilization. The maximum packet (frame) size is 8 bytes, and the bus length is 20 m. Estimate the worst-case end-to-end latency for a 64-bit control message, and discuss how it satisfies the typical 1 ms deadline for chassis control. Use $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

### (a) Solution — Bandwidth-Delay Product

**Definition:** BDP is the number of bits that "fill the pipe" — i.e., the maximum number of bits that can be in transit on the link at any instant without overwhelming the receiver.

$$\text{BDP} = R_b \cdot t_{\text{prop}} = R_b \cdot \frac{d}{v_p}$$

**Computation:**

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

$$\text{BDP} = 10^9 \text{ bps} \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10^7 \text{ bits} = 10 \text{ Mbits} \approx 1.25 \text{ MB}$$

**Significance for buffer sizing:**

The receiver (and intermediate routers) must have a buffer of **at least BDP bits** to avoid dropping in-flight data during peak bursts. If a CPS gateway has a buffer smaller than 1.25 MB, the protocol's **flow-control window** must be correspondingly reduced; otherwise, packets are dropped, harming reliability. This is critical in CPS gateways connecting, e.g., a remote sensor to a central controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical interpretation:** BDP = 1.25 MB means at any moment, **1.25 million bits** are simultaneously "on the wire" between the two gateways. This is why long-fat-pipes (LFPs) require **window scaling** in TCP and **large TX/RX FIFOs** in CPS hardware.

### (b) Solution — CAN Bus Latency Estimation

**Step 1 — Transmission delay for 64 bits:**

$$L_{\text{trans}} = \frac{64}{500 \times 10^3} = 1.28 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 128 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation delay:**

$$L_{\text{prop}} = \frac{20}{2 \times 10^8} = 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.1 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Effective bit time on the bus.** In CAN, the *nominal bit time* is $1/500\text{k} = 2 \; \mu\text{s/bit}$. A 64-bit frame (after bit-stuffing, ~80 bits) takes $\approx 160 \; \mu\text{s}$ to transmit.

**Step 4 — Queuing delay under 70% utilization.** For an M/M/1-like model, the average waiting time is approximately:

$$L_{\text{queue}} \approx \frac{\rho \cdot L_{\text{trans}}}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.7 \times 128 \; \mu\text{s}}{0.3} \approx 299 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Worst-case total latency** (under 70% load, max-bit-stuffing, one retry):

$$L_{\text{total, max}} \approx L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{queue}} + L_{\text{proc}}$$

$$\approx 128 + 0.1 + 299 + 50 \text{ (processing)} \approx 477 \; \mu\text{s} < 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** The CAN bus at 500 kbps, 70% load, 20 m length, comfortably meets the 1 ms deadline for chassis control. (1 mark for this conclusion)

**However**, at higher loads ($\rho \to 1$), the queuing term diverges, and **deadlines will be missed**. This is why CAN is engineered for **bounded load** (typically $\rho \le 0.7$) in safety-critical CPS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 2:** Students frequently *ignore queuing delay* and answer only with $L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{prop}}$. This is **incomplete** for CPS analysis, where queuing dominates under load. The examiner deducts up to 3 marks for this omission. **Always include all four components.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up units:** bandwidth (Hz) vs. throughput (bps). Examiners deduct 1 mark per error.
> 2. **Ignoring queuing delay** in latency calculations — up to **3 marks** lost.
> 3. **Wrong PER formula:** using $n \cdot \text{BER}$ when $n \cdot \text{BER} > 0.1$ — up to **2 marks** lost.
> 4. **Confusing reliability with availability.** Use precise statistical definitions.
> 5. **Forgetting the BDP / buffer-sizing implication** in throughput-delay questions.
> 6. **Omitting the trade-off discussion.** When proposing ARQ or FEC, *always* state the cost to latency or throughput.
> 7. **Not simplifying the Q-function or erfc** to a numerical value when asked for "compute."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Throughput** $T$ = *useful* data per unit time, **not** raw bit rate. Bound by Shannon capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- ⚡ **Latency** has **four** components: $L = L_{\text{trans}} + L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{proc}} + L_{\text{queue}}$. *Queuing is the only random one*.
- ⚡ **Transmission delay** depends on **packet size and bit rate**; **propagation delay** depends on **distance and medium**.
- ⚡ **Reliability** is quantified by **BER**, **PER**, **availability**, and **MTBF/MTTR**. Never just "trust."
- ⚡ **BDP** = $R_b \cdot t_{\text{prop}}$; dictates **buffer size** and **flow-control window**.
- ⚡ **PER** = $1 - (1 - \text{BER})^n$; approximation $n \cdot \text{BER}$ only valid when $n \cdot \text{BER} \ll 1$.
- ⚡ **Stop-and-Wait ARQ throughput** $= L(1-p)/(L/R_b + 2t_{\text{prop}})$.
- ⚡ **Trade-off triangle:** ↑throughput ⇔ ↓latency ⇔ ↑reliability cannot all be optimized together.
- ⚡ **CPS critical deadlines** (industrial / automotive): 1–10 ms latency, BER $< 10^{-9}$, availability > 99.999%.
- ⚡ **Jitter** is the *variance* of latency; bounded jitter is more important than low mean latency in real-time CPS.
- ⚡ For exam: always **state assumptions** (independent errors, M/M/1 queue, AWGN channel) before computing.
- ⚡ Memorize the **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet** (Section 2.4) — it covers > 80% of numerical questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** When a question says "explain with example," use a **CPS-specific** example (CAN, PROFINET, smart grid, autonomous vehicle) — generic Internet examples earn only partial credit. **Tie every metric to a real CPS application in your answer.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation Tools (Simulation, Testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation Tools (Simulation, Testing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Performance Evaluation in CPS?

**Performance Evaluation** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refers to the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative assessment of a CPS communication infrastructure using **simulation-based virtual prototyping** and **real-world testbed-driven testing** methodologies. The objective is to measure critical communication parameters such as **latency**, **throughput**, **jitter**, **packet delivery ratio (PDR)**, **energy consumption**, and **reliability** under controlled and reproducible conditions before field deployment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, performance evaluation is the **bridge between theoretical design and physical deployment** of CPS communication networks (WSN, IoT, Industrial IoT, V2X, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Performance evaluation of CPS communication systems involves modelling the network and physical behaviour using simulation frameworks (NS-3, OMNeT++, MATLAB/Simulink, Cooja, TrueTime) and validating the same using testbeds and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crash Test Dummy" Approach 🚗

Imagine you are designing a **new car** for Indian roads:
- You cannot release it directly onto the highway and "see what happens" — that is dangerous and irreversible.
- Instead, you build a **computer model** of the car, run **crash simulations** thousands of times in software (this is **Simulation**), and then you crash **real prototype cars** on a controlled test track with dummies (this is **Testing**).
- Finally, both results are compared before mass production.

Similarly, in CPS:
- **Simulation** = A virtual CPS where the communication protocol runs in software against a synthetic environment (channel models, mobility models, traffic patterns).
- **Testing** = A physical CPS testbed where real nodes (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers) communicate using the actual radio (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G) under real interference, fading, and noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need BOTH?**
> - Simulation = **scalable, repeatable, cheap**, but approximate.
> - Testing = **realistic, accurate, costly**, but harder to scale.
> - Together they form the **V-Model of CPS Validation**.

## 1.3 The CPS Communication Stack Under Evaluation

The CPS communication subsystem consists of:

| Layer | Component | Performance Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | Radio (Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE, 5G) | Signal strength, BER, range |
| Data Link | MAC (CSMA, TDMA, TSCH) | Collision, channel access delay |
| Network | Routing (RPL, AODV) | Path delay, energy cost |
| Transport | TCP/UDP, CoAP | Reliability, congestion |
| Application | MQTT, DDS, OPC-UA | End-to-end latency, jitter |

**Standard Performance Metrics (must be memorised for KTU):**
- **End-to-End Latency (ms)**
- **Throughput (kbps / Mbps)**
- **Jitter (ms)**
- **Packet Delivery Ratio / PDR (%)**
- **Energy per Bit (J/bit)**
- **Network Lifetime (days)**
- **Reliability / Availability (%)**

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU — "L-T-J-P-E-R-A"** → **L**atency, **T**hroughput, **J**itter, **P**DR, **E**nergy, **R**eliability, **A**vailability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Performance Evaluation

Performance evaluation methodologies are broadly classified into three families:

### A. Analytical Modelling (Pure Math)
- Uses **Queueing Theory** (M/M/1, M/G/1), **Markov Chains**, and **Network Calculus**.
- Pros: Closed-form, fast.
- Cons: Oversimplified, ignores real-world artefacts.

### B. Simulation (Virtual Prototyping) 🖥️
- **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)**: NS-3, OMNeT++, OPNET.
- **Continuous Simulation**: MATLAB/Simulink, Modelica.
- **Hybrid / Co-simulation**: Ptolemy II, mosaik, Simu5G.

### C. Emulation & Testbed (Real Hardware) 🔧
- **Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)**: Real software on virtual hardware.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)**: Real hardware with simulated plant.
- **Testbed**: Real deployment (e.g., IoT-Lab, FIT IoT-Lab, GENI, ORBIT).

## 2.2 Popular Simulation Tools — KTU Must-Know Table

| Tool | Type | Best For | Language | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NS-3** | Discrete Event | IP networks, 5G, Wi-Fi, LTE | C++ / Python | Realistic PHY/MAC |
| **OMNeT++** | Discrete Event | WSN, VANET, IoT | C++ / NED | Modular INET/INETMANET |
| **MATLAB/Simulink** | Continuous / Hybrid | Control + Comms co-design | MATLAB | TrueTime toolbox |
| **Cooja (Contiki)** | Emulator | Contiki OS WSN | Java / C | Real firmware in sim |
| **TrueTime** | Co-simulation | Real-time control networks | MATLAB | Kernel + network sim |
| **Mininet** | Emulator | SDN, OpenFlow | Python | Lightweight VM-based |
| **ns-2 (legacy)** | Discrete Event | Legacy WSN research | C++ / OTcl | Historical use |
| **OPNET** | Commercial DES | Enterprise networks | C / Proto-C | GUI-driven |
| **GNS3** | Emulator | Cisco / router networks | GUI | Real network images |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Trio:** NS-3, OMNeT++, and MATLAB/Simulink. Questions frequently appear on these.

## 2.3 Testing Methodologies — The V-Model

The **V-Model** is the industry-standard framework for CPS verification:

```
Model-in-the-Loop (MIL)
        ↕
Software-in-the-Loop (SIL)
        ↕
Processor-in-the-Loop (PIL)
        ↕
Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)
```

- **MIL**: Plant model + controller model, both in simulation.
- **SIL**: Generated code from controller model runs on PC.
- **PIL**: Generated code runs on target processor (e.g., ARM Cortex).
- **HIL**: Real embedded controller + simulated physical plant (or vice versa).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📊

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU-defining and must be memorised verbatim.**

### A. End-to-End Latency

$$L_{E2E} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$$

Where:
- $L_{proc}$ = processing delay at the node
- $L_{queue}$ = queuing delay at the MAC layer
- $L_{trans}$ = transmission delay = $\frac{S}{R}$ (S = packet size, R = data rate)
- $L_{prop}$ = propagation delay = $\frac{d}{v}$ (d = distance, v = signal speed)

### B. Throughput

$$T = \frac{N_{rx} \cdot S_{pkt}}{T_{obs}} \quad [\text{bits/sec}]$$

### C. Jitter (Standard Deviation of Delay)

$$J = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(D_i - \bar{D})^2} \quad [\text{ms}]$$

### D. Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)

$$PDR = \frac{N_{rx}}{N_{tx}} \times 100\%$$

### E. Energy Consumption (Linear Model)

$$E_{total} = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

For multi-state radios (TX / RX / Idle / Sleep):

$$E_{total} = V \cdot \left( I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + I_{rx} \cdot t_{rx} + I_{idle} \cdot t_{idle} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} \right)$$

### F. Network Lifetime (First Node Dies)

$$T_{lifetime} = \min_{i=1..n} \left( \frac{E_{initial,i}}{P_{avg,i}} \right)$$

### G. Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR)

$$SINR = \frac{P_{rx}}{P_{noise} + \sum_{j \neq i} P_{interference,j}}$$

### H. Bit Error Rate (BER) for BPSK in AWGN

$$BER_{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot SINR}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{SINR}\right)$$

### I. Channel Capacity (Shannon's Theorem)

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad [\text{bits/sec}]$$

### J. M/M/1 Queueing Model (for MAC analysis)

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}, \quad W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}, \quad W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = packet arrival rate
- $\mu$ = service rate
- $\rho$ = utilisation factor (must be less than 1 for stability)

## 2.5 Formula Summary Table (Exam-Ready) 📋

| Metric | Formula | Unit | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | $L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$ | ms | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Throughput | $N_{rx} \cdot S \vert T_{obs}$ | bps | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Jitter | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum(D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | ms | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| PDR | $N_{rx} \vert N_{tx} \times 100$ | % | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Energy | $V \cdot I \cdot t$ | J | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| SINR | $P_{rx} \vert (N + I)$ | dB | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Capacity | $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Lifetime | $E_{init} \vert P_{avg}$ | s | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These metrics directly feed into the design of **autonomous vehicles (latency-critical V2X)**, **smart grids (reliability-critical SCADA)**, **remote surgery (jitter-critical 5G URLLC)**, and **precision agriculture (energy-critical LoRaWAN)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key CPS Performance Equations

### 🧮 Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency from First Principles

We start by modelling a CPS packet traversing a multi-hop network.

**Step 1 — Define the four fundamental delay components:**

A packet experiences delay at every hop. The total delay is the sum of the delays at every layer and every hop.

**Step 2 — Transmission Delay derivation:**

Transmission delay is the time taken to push all bits of a packet onto the physical medium. If a packet has $S$ bits and the link has a data rate of $R$ bits/second:

$$L_{trans} = \frac{\text{Number of bits}}{\text{Rate}} = \frac{S}{R} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Step 3 — Propagation Delay derivation:**

Propagation delay is the time taken for the electromagnetic wave to travel the physical distance between transmitter and receiver.

$$L_{prop} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Signal Speed}} = \frac{d}{v} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

For wireless, $v \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. For copper, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 4 — Queueing Delay (M/M/1 result):**

For a Poisson arrival process with rate $\lambda$ and exponential service time with mean $1/\mu$, the average queueing delay is:

$$L_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Step 5 — Processing Delay (constant model):**

$$L_{proc} = T_{CPU} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Step 6 — Summing up for a single hop:**

$$L_{hop} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + \frac{S}{R} + \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 7 — Extending to $H$ hops (typical multi-hop WSN):**

$$L_{E2E} = \sum_{h=1}^{H} \left( L_{proc,h} + L_{queue,h} + \frac{S}{R_h} + \frac{d_h}{v} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question gives "10 m distance, 250 kbps, 128-byte packet, M/M/1 with λ=50 pkt/s, μ=200 pkt/s", plug directly into the above.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Throughput in a Multi-Hop CPS Network

**Step 1 — Define observed throughput:**

Throughput is the useful data successfully delivered per unit time, NOT the raw channel rate.

$$T = \frac{\text{Total payload bits delivered}}{\text{Observation time}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of packet count:**

If $N_{rx}$ packets each of size $S$ bits are received correctly in time $T_{obs}$:

$$T = \frac{N_{rx} \cdot S}{T_{obs}} \quad [\text{bps}]$$

**Step 3 — Relate to PDR:**

Since $N_{rx} = PDR \cdot N_{tx}$:

$$T = \frac{PDR \cdot N_{tx} \cdot S}{T_{obs}}$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput considering MAC overhead:**

Let $S_{total}$ include MAC header, PHY preamble, ACK, and inter-frame spacing.

$$T_{eff} = \frac{N_{rx} \cdot S_{payload}}{N_{tx} \cdot T_{frame}} = \frac{S_{payload}}{S_{total}} \cdot R_{raw} \cdot PDR$$

This explains why 802.15.4 with 250 kbps raw rate only delivers ~100 kbps in practice.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Energy Consumption for a CPS Sensor Node

**Step 1 — Radio state model:**

A typical CPS node (e.g., CC2650) has four states: TX, RX, Idle, Sleep.

**Step 2 — Energy in each state:**

$$E_k = V \cdot I_k \cdot t_k \quad \text{for } k \in \{TX, RX, Idle, Sleep\}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy over duty cycle $D$:**

$$E_{total} = V \cdot \left( I_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + I_{rx} \cdot t_{rx} + I_{idle} \cdot (1-D)\cdot T_{cycle} + I_{sleep} \cdot D \cdot T_{cycle} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Energy per useful bit:**

$$E_{bit} = \frac{E_{total}}{N_{bits\_delivered}} \quad [\text{J/bit}]$$

**Step 5 — Network lifetime:**

$$T_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{battery}}{\bar{P}_{total}}$$

This is the central equation for energy-aware CPS design.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 4: Shannon Capacity Limit for CPS Wireless Links

**Step 1 — Starting point: Nyquist noise-free capacity:**

$$C = 2B \log_2(M) \quad [\text{bps}]$$

**Step 2 — Add noise (Shannon's 1948 theorem):**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad [\text{bps}]$$

**Step 3 — Express in dB:**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}\right) \quad [\text{bps}]$$

**Step 4 — High-SNR approximation:**

When $S/N \gg 1$:

$$C \approx B \cdot \frac{SNR_{dB}}{3} \quad [\text{bps}]$$

This is the famous "3 dB rule": every 3 dB doubles the capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in CPS:** Industrial Wireless (e.g., WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a) targets capacity *just below* Shannon's limit for maximum spectral efficiency in 2.4 GHz.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Strategy

| Question Type | Approach | Key Formula(s) |
|---|---|---|
| "Find E2E delay for H hops" | Sum hop delays | $L_{E2E} = \sum L_{hop}$ |
| "Find throughput from PDR" | Multiply by packet size | $T = PDR \cdot N_{tx} \cdot S / T_{obs}$ |
| "Find jitter" | Use delay variance | $J = \sigma_D$ |
| "Find network lifetime" | Energy ÷ Avg power | $T_life = E_0 \vert \bar{P}$ |
| "Find queue delay" | Use M/M/1 | $W_q = \rho \vert (\mu - \lambda)$ |
| "Find max data rate" | Use Shannon | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ |
| "Find SINR / BER" | Use BPSK formula | $BER = Q(\sqrt{2 \cdot SINR})$ |
| "Compare tools" | Tabulate features | Use Section 2.2 table |

---

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Performance Evaluation Workflow in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPS Communication Design] --> B[Define Performance Metrics]
    B --> C1[Analytical Modeling]
    B --> C2[Simulation]
    B --> C3[Testbed Emulation]
    C1 --> D1[Queueing Theory / Network Calculus]
    C2 --> D2[NS-3 / OMNeT++ / MATLAB]
    C3 --> D3[IoT-Lab / FIT / GENI]
    D1 --> E[Collect Metrics: Latency, Throughput, Jitter, PDR, Energy]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    F -- No --> G[Refine Protocol]
    G --> A
    F -- Yes --> H[Field Deployment]
    H --> I[Continuous Monitoring]
    I --> A
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end
    subgraph Verification_Phase [VERIFICATION PHASE]

    end
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    A4 --> B4
    B1 --> C1[Controller on PC + Plant in Sim]
    B2 --> C2[Controller Code on PC + Plant in Sim]
    B3 --> C3[Controller Code on Target CPU + Plant in Sim]

    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**List any three key performance metrics used for evaluating CPS communication systems. Briefly explain the significance of latency and packet delivery ratio (PDR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three key performance metrics are:
1. **End-to-End Latency** (3 marks partial) — Defined as the total time taken by a data packet to travel from the source sensor to the destination actuator. It is critical in **time-sensitive CPS** such as autonomous vehicles and industrial control, where latencies above 10 ms can cause instability.
2. **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** — Defined as $\frac{N_{rx}}{N_{tx}} \times 100\%$. It quantifies **communication reliability**, vital for safety-critical CPS like medical devices and smart grids.
3. **Throughput** / **Jitter** / **Energy per bit** — any one (1 mark).

*(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for significance, 1 mark for example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between Software-in-the-Loop (SIL) and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing in CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SIL | HIL |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware | No real controller hardware | Real embedded controller |
| Plant | Simulated | Real or simulated |
| Fidelity | Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Use case | Early validation | Final-stage validation |

*(1 mark per correct difference point. 3 distinct points = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **V-Model of CPS verification**, clearly describing MIL, SIL, PIL, and HIL stages. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A. (b)** A CPS wireless sensor node transmits packets of size **128 bytes** over a **250 kbps** IEEE 802.15.4 link. The MAC queue follows an M/M/1 model with arrival rate **λ = 40 pkt/s** and service rate **μ = 100 pkt/s**. The distance is **30 m** and the speed of signal is $3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute the **end-to-end latency** for a single hop. *(7 marks)*

### **OR**

### **Question B [14 Marks]**

**Q.B. (a)** Compare **NS-3, OMNeT++, and MATLAB/Simulink** as simulation tools for CPS communication. Highlight the suitability of each for WSN, IoT, and control-co-design scenarios. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B. (b)** Derive the **Shannon capacity** of a CPS wireless link with bandwidth **B = 1 MHz** and SNR = **20 dB**. If the link is observed for 10 seconds and delivers 5000 packets of 256 bytes each with PDR = 95%, compute the **effective throughput**. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 📋 Complete Model Solutions

### ✅ Solution to Q.A. (a) — V-Model (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the V-Model as a symmetric validation framework where each design phase has a corresponding verification phase.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe each stage:
- **MIL (Model-in-the-Loop):** Both the plant (e.g., motor, drone) and the controller are mathematical models. Used in the earliest design stage using Simulink/Modelica.
- **SIL (Software-in-the-Loop):** The auto-generated C code from the controller model runs on a PC against the plant model. Validates algorithmic correctness.
- **PIL (Processor-in-the-Loop):** The generated code is cross-compiled and runs on the **target processor** (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4). Detects compiler and architecture issues.
- **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop):** The **real embedded board** communicates with a **real-time simulated plant** (e.g., via dSPACE, OPAL-RT, NI VeriStand). Final pre-deployment validation.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Block Diagram (redraw Mermaid 4.2 in exam):

```
Plant Model      → MIL  → Validate
Controller Model → MIL  → Validate
    ↓
Code Generation  → SIL  → Validate
    ↓
Target Processor → PIL  → Validate
    ↓
Embedded Hardware → HIL → Validate
```

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Conclude with benefits: early bug detection, ISO 26262 / IEC 61508 compliance, reduced field failures.

---

### ✅ Solution to Q.A. (b) — Latency Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $S = 128 \text{ bytes} = 128 \times 8 = 1024 \text{ bits}$
- $R = 250 \text{ kbps} = 2.5 \times 10^5 \text{ bps}$
- $\lambda = 40 \text{ pkt/s}$, $\mu = 100 \text{ pkt/s}$
- $d = 30$ m, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Assume $L_{proc} = 1$ ms (typical for CC2650)

**Step 1 — Transmission delay (1 mark):**

$$L_{trans} = \frac{S}{R} = \frac{1024}{2.5 \times 10^5} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation delay (1 mark):**

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{30}{3 \times 10^8} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.0001 \text{ ms}$$

(Negligible at short range — but write it for full marks!)

**Step 3 — Queueing delay using M/M/1 (2 marks):**

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4$$

$$L_{queue} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{0.4}{100 - 40} = \frac{0.4}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.667 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Processing delay (1 mark):**

$$L_{proc} = 1 \text{ ms} \text{ (given)}$$

**Step 5 — Total E2E latency for single hop (2 marks):**

$$L_{E2E} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$$

$$L_{E2E} = 1 + 6.667 + 4.096 + 0.0001 \approx 11.763 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $L_{E2E} \approx 11.76$ ms (within the 10 ms–15 ms range typical for IEEE 802.15.4).

---

### ✅ Solution to Q.B. (a) — Simulation Tool Comparison (7 marks)

**(1 mark) Introduction:** Performance evaluation of CPS communication requires a mix of network-level and control-level simulation.

**(2 marks) NS-3:**
- Discrete-event network simulator written in C++ with Python bindings.
- Best for **IP-based CPS**: Wi-Fi, LTE, 5G, TCP/UDP performance.
- Limitation: Poor support for control-loop co-simulation.

**(2 marks) OMNeT++:**
- Modular DES framework with **INET** and **INETMANET** frameworks.
- Best for **WSN and MANET** CPS: IEEE 802.15.4, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RPL.
- Highly modular; community-driven MiXiM framework for wireless channel.

**(2 marks) MATLAB/Simulink:**
- Best for **control + communication co-design** using the **TrueTime** toolbox.
- Suitable for **real-time CPS**: robotics, autonomous systems, networked control systems (NCS).
- Limitation: Expensive licence; slow for large-scale networks.

| Tool | Best for | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| NS-3 | 5G, LTE, Wi-Fi | Realistic PHY | No control sim |
| OMNeT++ | WSN, IoT | Modular | Steep learning |
| MATLAB | NCS, robotics | Control + Comms | Cost, scalability |

---

### ✅ Solution to Q.B. (b) — Shannon Capacity + Throughput (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale (1 mark):**

$$SNR_{linear} = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Shannon Capacity (2 marks):**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + SNR) = 10^6 \cdot \log_2(101) = 10^6 \cdot 6.658 = 6.658 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Total bits delivered in observation window (1 mark):**

$$N_{rx} = 5000 \text{ packets}, \quad S = 256 \times 8 = 2048 \text{ bits}$$

$$N_{rx} \cdot S = 5000 \times 2048 = 1.024 \times 10^7 \text{ bits} = 10.24 \text{ Mbits}$$

**Step 4 — Effective throughput (2 marks):**

$$T_{eff} = \frac{N_{rx} \cdot S}{T_{obs}} = \frac{1.024 \times 10^7}{10} = 1.024 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 1.024 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 — Adjust for PDR (1 mark):**

Theoretical max delivery if PDR = 100%:

$$N_{tx} = \frac{N_{rx}}{PDR} = \frac{5000}{0.95} = 5263 \text{ packets}$$

The effective throughput already accounts for the actual delivered packets, so:

$$\boxed{T_{eff} = 1.024 \text{ Mbps}, \quad C = 6.658 \text{ Mbps}}$$

Note: $T_{eff} \ll C$ because (a) the link isn't saturated, (b) MAC overhead exists.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners penalise heavily:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write `ms`, `bps`, `J`. A numerical answer without units gets **zero credit** for the step.
> 2. **Conflating bits and bytes** — Packet size of "128 bytes" means 1024 **bits** for transmission calculations. Wrong by 8x is a classic KTU failure.
> 3. **Using throughput formula incorrectly** — Use $N_{rx} \cdot S$ not $N_{tx} \cdot S$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Ignoring propagation delay** — Even at short range, you MUST compute and add it. Examiners check for the four-term latency sum.
> 5. **Confusing M/M/1 with M/D/1** — M/M/1 has exponential service; if the question says "deterministic", the formula changes.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — In 7-mark questions, a block diagram is **mandatory**. Skip it = lose 1-2 marks.
> 7. **Writing "NS-3 is better than OMNeT++"** — Examiners want a **comparative table**, not a subjective verdict.
> 8. **No V-Model diagram in MIL/SIL/HIL questions** — Always draw the V-shape (left arm = design, right arm = verification).

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorise These 12 Points:**
> 
> 1. **Performance Evaluation = Simulation + Testing (testbed)** — both are essential, never choose one.
> 2. **Three families:** Analytical (math), Simulation (NS-3, OMNeT++, MATLAB), Emulation/Testbed (Cooja, Mininet, IoT-Lab).
> 3. **Key Metrics (in order of importance for KTU):** Latency, Throughput, Jitter, PDR, Energy, Reliability.
> 4. **Latency Formula:** $L = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$ — must show all four terms.
> 5. **Throughput Formula:** $T = N_{rx} \cdot S \vert T_{obs}$ — use received count, not transmitted.
> 6. **Jitter Formula:** $J = \sigma_D$ = standard deviation of delay.
> 7. **PDR Formula:** $\frac{N_{rx}}{N_{tx}} \times 100$.
> 8. **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the absolute upper bound.
> 9. **M/M/1 Queueing:** $W_q = \rho \vert (\mu - \lambda)$ — valid only when $\rho < 1$.
> 10. **V-Model Stages (in order):** MIL → SIL → PIL → HIL → Field Deployment.
> 11. **Energy Model:** $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ for each state (TX, RX, Idle, Sleep).
> 12. **Always convert:** bytes ↔ bits (×8), kbps ↔ bps (×1000), dB ↔ linear ($10^{dB/10}$).
> 
> **Bonus Mnemonic:** **"S-S-M-H"** for testing stages: **S**imulate → **S**oftware-in-loop → **H**ardware-in-loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Techniques for CPS Communication (Buffer Sizing, Packet Scheduling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimization Techniques for CPS Communication (Buffer Sizing, Packet Scheduling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Optimization in CPS Communication?

In a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, the communication network is the *nervous system* connecting sensors, controllers, and actuators. Unlike general-purpose computer networks (where the goal is best-effort throughput), CPS communication must satisfy **deterministic timing, bounded latency, and zero (or near-zero) jitter**. Two of the most critical optimization levers in this domain are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buffer Sizing** and **Packet Scheduling** are the two principal optimization techniques that jointly determine the **Quality of Control (QoC)** in a CPS. Together they govern *delay*, *jitter*, *packet loss*, and *memory footprint* of the network nodes.

---

### 1.2 Buffer Sizing — Formal Definition

**Buffer Sizing** is the process of determining the *minimum* required memory allocation at a network node (switch, router, or bus interface) such that:
- No packet is dropped due to overflow under worst-case traffic arrival.
- No excessive queuing delay is introduced that would violate the *control loop deadline*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Buffer sizing in CPS refers to the analytical and heuristic determination of queue capacity at a network node, expressed in bits or packets, so as to guarantee bounded end-to-end delay and zero-loss performance for real-time control flows under worst-case arrival assumptions.*

---

### 1.3 Packet Scheduling — Formal Definition

**Packet Scheduling** is the policy (algorithm) that decides the *order* in which buffered packets are transmitted onto the outgoing link. In CPS, scheduling is not just about fairness — it is about **meeting deadlines** of real-time tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Packet scheduling in CPS is the set of deterministic algorithms (e.g., FIFO, Priority Queuing, EDF, WFQ, TSN Time-Aware Shaper) that arbitrate access to the shared communication medium, ensuring that time-critical flows are transmitted before their hard deadlines expire.*

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

#### Analogy 1: The Factory Conveyor Belt (Buffer Sizing)
Imagine a **bottling plant**:
- Bottles arrive on Belt A at an uneven rate (analogous to sensor data arriving at a network switch).
- They are processed and placed on Belt B for filling.
- The **storage rack between A and B** is the *buffer*.
- If the rack is **too small** → bottles fall off (packet loss).
- If the rack is **too large** → bottles sit too long waiting (added latency) and the *control loop* (filling station feedback) becomes unstable.
- **Optimal buffer size** = just enough rack space to absorb the worst-case burst without adding unnecessary delay.

#### Analogy 2: The Hospital Triage (Packet Scheduling)
Imagine an **Emergency Room**:
- Patients (packets) arrive constantly — some with mild fever, some with heart attacks.
- The **triage nurse** is the *scheduler*.
- A **First-Come-First-Served** nurse (FIFO) would treat a sprained ankle before a cardiac arrest — disastrous.
- An **Earliest-Deadline-First (EDF)** or **Priority-based** nurse recognizes that a heart-attack patient has a *hard 5-minute deadline* and must be treated first, even if they arrived later.
- This is exactly what **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** and **EDF scheduling** do in CPS.

---

### 1.5 Key Metrics in CPS Communication Optimization

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| End-to-End Delay | $D_{e2e}$ | $\le 1\text{ ms}$ (hard real-time) |
| Jitter | $J$ | $\le 100\ \mu s$ |
| Packet Loss Probability | $P_{loss}$ | $\approx 0$ |
| Buffer Occupancy | $B$ | Minimized subject to $P_{loss} \approx 0$ |
| Schedulability Ratio | $U$ | $\le 1$ (Liu & Layland bound) |

---

### 1.6 Why Optimization is Non-Trivial in CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CPS, communication is **closed-loop with physical dynamics**. A delay of a few milliseconds in an automotive brake-by-wire system or a robotic arm controller can cause **physical instability, oscillation, or even catastrophic failure**. Hence, optimization is not about maximizing throughput — it is about **minimizing worst-case delay** while **guaranteeing zero loss**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Buffer Sizing — Theoretical Foundations

#### 2.1.1 The Buffer Sizing Problem

At a network node with link capacity $C$ (bits/sec) and arrival process $A(t)$ (cumulative bits arrived by time $t$), the queue length $Q(t)$ evolves as:

$$Q(t) = \max\left\{0,\ \sup_{0 \le s \le t}\left[A(s) - S(s,t)\right]\right\}$$

where $S(s,t)$ is the cumulative service offered to the flow in the interval $(s, t]$.

The **minimum buffer** to guarantee zero loss is:

$$B_{min} = \max_{t \ge 0}\left\{A(t) - S(t)\right\}$$

In steady state, this reduces to the famous **bandwidth-delay product**:

$$B_{min} = C \times RTT \quad \text{(bits)}$$

---

#### 2.1.2 Kleinrock's Approximation (Independent Traffic Model)

For $N$ long-lived TCP flows sharing a bottleneck link, Kleinrock showed that the average queue length and hence the required buffer is:

$$\boxed{B_{Kleinrock} = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT}{2\sqrt{N}}}$$

where:
- $C$ = bottleneck link capacity (bits/s)
- $RTT$ = round-trip time (seconds)
- $N$ = number of long-lived flows

> [!NOTE]
> The **classical rule of thumb** of $B = C \cdot RTT$ (the "bandwidth-delay product") is an **upper bound** for *single-flow* worst-case analysis. For $N$ desynchronized flows, the buffer can be reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$.

---

#### 2.1.3 Network Calculus Approach (Worst-Case Bounding)

Network calculus uses **arrival curves** $\alpha(t)$ and **service curves** $\beta(t)$ to derive *deterministic* bounds.

Given an arrival curve $\alpha(t) = b + r \cdot t$ (leaky-bucket) and a service curve $\beta(t) = R \cdot (t - T)^+$, the **maximum backlog** is:

$$B_{max} = \alpha \otimes \beta(0) = b + r \cdot T$$

where $b$ = burst tolerance, $r$ = average rate, $R$ = service rate, $T$ = latency of the service curve.

The **worst-case delay** $D_{max}$ is the **horizontal deviation** between $\alpha(t)$ and $\beta(t)$:

$$D_{max} = \dfrac{b}{R} + T$$

---

#### 2.1.4 CPS-Specific Buffer Sizing: The Stability Constraint

For a CPS control loop with sampling period $h$ and physical plant constant $\tau$, the **maximum tolerable one-way delay** $D_{max}$ must satisfy:

$$D_{max} < h - \tau \quad \text{(Schwager et al., 2014, generalized)}$$

The buffer is then sized to ensure the induced queuing delay $W_q$ satisfies:

$$W_q \le D_{max} - d_{prop} - d_{proc}$$

where $d_{prop}$ = propagation delay, $d_{proc}$ = processing delay.

---

### 2.2 Packet Scheduling — Algorithmic Theory

#### 2.2.1 Classification of Schedulers

Schedulers are classified along three axes:

1. **Work-Conserving vs Non-Work-Conserving**: Does the server idle when the queue is non-empty?
2. **Priority-based vs Fair-sharing**: Does it differentiate among flows?
3. **Offline (Time-Triggered) vs Online (Event-Triggered)**: Is the schedule pre-computed?

#### 2.2.2 Major Scheduling Algorithms

**A. FIFO (First-In-First-Out)**
- Non-prioritized; packets leave in arrival order.
- Pros: simple, fair among identical flows.
- Cons: head-of-line blocking, no deadline guarantees.

**B. Strict Priority Queuing (PQ)**
- Higher priority queues are always served first.
- Pros: low latency for high-priority traffic.
- Cons: starvation of low-priority flows; needs **traffic policing** to prevent hogging.

**C. Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ)**
- Each flow $i$ gets a guaranteed share $w_i / \sum w_j$ of the bandwidth.
- Pros: fairness, bounded delay per flow.
- Cons: complex implementation; delay bound is $D_i \le B_i / R_i$ where $B_i$ is backlog.

**D. Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**
- Packets are tagged with absolute deadlines; the one with the earliest deadline is sent first.
- **Liu & Layland (1973) Schedulability Test:**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1 \quad \text{(on a single processor/link)}$$

where $C_i$ = transmission time of flow $i$, $T_i$ = period (deadline interval) of flow $i$.

- Pros: optimal for uniprocessor scheduling.
- Cons: requires accurate time synchronization; not natively supported in classical Ethernet.

**E. Time-Aware Shaper (TAS) — IEEE 802.1Qbv (TSN)**
- Time-triggered: gates open/close according to a pre-computed schedule table.
- Deterministic to the microsecond level; used in automotive (AVB/TSN) and industrial control.
- Requires **time synchronization** (IEEE 802.1AS, gPTP).

**F. Deficit Round Robin (DRR)**
- Approximates fair queuing with $O(1)$ complexity; widely used in practical switches.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $B = C \cdot RTT$ | Minimum buffer for a single flow (worst case) |
| 2 | Kleinrock's Buffer | $B = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT}{2\sqrt{N}}$ | Buffer for $N$ desynchronized flows |
| 3 | Network Calculus Backlog | $B_{max} = b + rT$ | With leaky-bucket arrival, latency-$T$ service |
| 4 | Network Calculus Delay | $D_{max} = \dfrac{b}{R} + T$ | Horizontal deviation of curves |
| 5 | Little's Law | $N = \lambda \cdot W$ | Avg. number in system = arrival rate × avg. wait |
| 6 | EDF Schedulability | $\sum_i \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$ | Feasibility on a single link |
| 7 | Rate Monotonic Priority | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Utilization upper bound; for $n=3$, $U \approx 0.78$ |
| 8 | WFQ Delay Bound | $D_i \le \dfrac{B_{max,i}}{R}$ | Per-flow delay under WFQ |
| 9 | CPS Stability Delay | $D_{max} < h - \tau$ | Hard real-time control constraint |
| 10 | Effective Bandwidth (Guerroui's) | $E_b = \dfrac{1}{\theta}\log\mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X}]$ | For stochastic arrivals, $\theta > 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for students:** In the KTU exam, the *most frequently asked* formula is the **Kleinrock buffer** (Q2) and the **Network Calculus backlog** (Q3). Always state the *assumptions* explicitly when using them.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR, CAN, Automotive Ethernet/TSN):** TAS + EDF combined to schedule brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, and infotainment on shared Ethernet.
- **Industrial Automation (PROFINET IRT, EtherCAT):** Time-triggered scheduling with sub-microsecond jitter.
- **Smart Grid IEC 61850:** GOOSE messages use strict-priority to bypass normal traffic.
- **Aerospace (AFDX):** Bounded latency via policing + WFQ.
- **Robotics (ROS 2, EtherCAT):** Deterministic scheduling for joint control loops.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Kleinrock's Buffer Size Formula

**Starting Assumption:** Consider $N$ persistent (greedy) TCP sources sharing a single bottleneck link of capacity $C$ bits/sec. Each source has a round-trip time $RTT_i$. The flows are *desynchronized* (do not all back off simultaneously).

**Step 1 — Fluid Model of TCP:**

The aggregate window of all flows is $W(t) = \sum_i W_i(t)$. The aggregate throughput approximates the link capacity:

$$W(t) \approx \dfrac{C \cdot RTT_{avg}}{1} = C \cdot RTT_{avg}$$

(Each flow keeps its window such that the link is fully utilized.)

**Step 2 — Stochastic Independence:**

If $N$ flows have i.i.d. RTTs with mean $\mu_{RTT}$ and variance $\sigma_{RTT}^2$, the variance of the aggregate window is, by the central limit theorem:

$$Var(W) = N \cdot \sigma_{RTT}^2$$

**Step 3 — Queue Length via Kingman's Formula:**

Kingman's $M/M/1$ approximation gives the average queue length as:

$$\mathbb{E}[Q] = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}\cdot \dfrac{C_a^2 + C_s^2}{2}$$

For high utilization ($\rho \to 1$) and deterministic service ($C_s = 0$), this reduces to:

$$\mathbb{E}[Q] = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C_a^2}{2(1-\rho)}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the Coefficient of Variation:**

The coefficient of variation of the *aggregate* window is:

$$C_a^2 = \dfrac{Var(W)}{(\mathbb{E}[W])^2} = \dfrac{N \cdot \sigma_{RTT}^2}{(C \cdot \mu_{RTT})^2}$$

**Step 5 — Assembling Kleinrock's Result:**

Substituting and simplifying (Kleinrock 1979, also Enachescu et al. 2005):

$$\mathbb{E}[Q] = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT_{avg}}{2\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{C \cdot \mu_{RTT}}{2\sqrt{N}}$$

Thus, the recommended buffer size is:

$$\boxed{B_{Kleinrock} = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT_{avg}}{2\sqrt{N}} \quad \text{(bits)}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Illustration:**

Let $C = 1\ \text{Gbps} = 10^9\ \text{bits/s}$, $RTT = 100\ \text{ms} = 0.1\ \text{s}$, $N = 100$.

$$B = \dfrac{10^9 \cdot 0.1}{2\sqrt{100}} = \dfrac{10^8}{20} = 5 \times 10^6\ \text{bits} = 625\ \text{KB}$$

Compare to the classical rule of thumb: $B_{BDP} = 10^9 \cdot 0.1 = 10^8\ \text{bits} = 12.5\ \text{MB}$ — a 20× reduction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Network Calculus Worst-Case Delay and Backlog

**Setup:** A flow is shaped by a **leaky-bucket** regulator:
- Average rate $r$ (bits/s)
- Burst tolerance $b$ (bits)

The arrival curve is:

$$\alpha(t) = b + r \cdot t \quad \text{for } t \ge 0$$

The node offers a **rate-latency service curve**:

$$\beta(t) = R \cdot (t - T)^+ = \begin{cases} R(t-T), & t \ge T \\ 0, & t < T \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Max-Plus Convolution for Backlog:**

The maximum backlog is the **vertical deviation** between $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

$$B_{max} = \sup_{t \ge 0}\{\alpha(t) - \beta(t)\} = \sup_{t \ge 0}\{b + rt - R(t-T)^+\}$$

For $t \ge T$:

$$B_{max} = \sup_{t \ge T}\{b + rt - R(t-T)\} = b + rT + (r-R)\cdot t\big|_{t=T}$$

To maximize, take derivative w.r.t. $t$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}[b + rt - R(t-T)] = r - R$$

If $R > r$ (link is faster than source's average), the supremum is at $t = T$:

$$B_{max} = b + rT$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Max-Plus Deconvolution for Delay:**

The maximum delay is the **horizontal deviation** — the smallest $d$ such that $\alpha(t) \le \beta(t+d)$ for all $t \ge 0$:

$$b + r(t) \le R(t + d - T) \quad \text{for all } t \ge T$$

For $t = T$ (boundary), we need:

$$b + rT \le R \cdot d \implies d \ge \dfrac{b + rT}{R}$$

For $t \gg T$, the $rT$ term is dominated. The infimum is:

$$\boxed{D_{max} = \dfrac{b}{R} + T}$$

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation:**
- $T$ is the **worst-case processing latency** at the node (a property of the hardware).
- $b/R$ is the **residual delay** due to burst absorption.
- These two add linearly — a key feature of the **pay-bursts-only-once (PBOO)** property of network calculus.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: EDF Schedulability on a Shared Link

**Setup:** $n$ real-time flows share a link of capacity $C$. Flow $i$ has:
- Period $T_i$ (also = relative deadline)
- Packet size $L_i$ bits
- Hence transmission time $C_i = L_i / C$.

**Condition for EDF feasibility (Liu & Layland 1973):**

A set of independent, preemptable flows is schedulable under EDF on a single link if and only if:

$$\boxed{U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{C \cdot T_i} \le 1}$$

**Proof sketch:**

1. Suppose $U \le 1$. By exchange argument, EDF is optimal — no other work-conserving scheduler can meet all deadlines if EDF cannot.
2. If $U > 1$, the link is overloaded; some deadline must be missed (by counting argument over a busy interval).

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Consider 3 flows on a 100 Mbps link:

| Flow | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Payload $L_i$ (bytes) | $C_i$ ($\mu$s) | $C_i/T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | 1 | 64 | 5.12 | 0.00512 |
| F2 | 2 | 128 | 10.24 | 0.00512 |
| F3 | 5 | 256 | 20.48 | 0.00410 |

Total utilization:

$$U = 0.00512 + 0.00512 + 0.00410 = 0.01434 \ll 1$$

The flow set is **feasible** under EDF with huge margin. The system is far from saturation; optimization here focuses on **minimizing jitter** rather than meeting deadlines.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Technique | Delay Guarantee | Jitter | Implementation Cost | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FIFO | None | High | Very Low | Best-effort, non-critical CPS traffic |
| Strict Priority | Bounded for high-prio only | Low (high) / High (low) | Low | Mixed criticality with policing |
| WFQ | Per-flow bound $\propto B/R$ | Medium | Medium | Fair multi-flow sharing |
| EDF | Optimal (Liu-Layland) | Low (with sync) | Medium-High | Soft real-time on shared Ethernet |
| TAS (802.1Qbv) | Deterministic, $\mu s$ | Very Low | High (needs time sync) | Hard real-time, automotive, industrial |
| DRR | Approximate fairness | Medium | Low | Practical switches with many flows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks for *which scheduler to choose*, justify with: (i) timing criticality, (ii) flow count, (iii) synchronization availability, and (iv) hardware constraints.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: CPS Communication Optimization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sensors[Sensors\nPhysical World] --> A1[Sample\nA-D Conversion]
    A1 --> NetA[Network Ingress]
    NetA --> Buf[Buffer at Switch]
    Buf --> Sched[Scheduler]
    Sched --> Link[Output Link]
    Link --> Ctrl[Controller]
    Ctrl --> Plant[Actuator Plant]
    Plant -->|Feedback| Sensors

    Opt[Optimization Engine] -.->|Size| Buf
    Opt -.->|Policy| Sched

    classDef io fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    class Sensors,Plant,Ctrl,Link,A1 io
    class NetA,Buf,Sched core
    class Opt opt
```

**Explanation of the flow:**
- The **physical plant** is sampled by sensors → ingress into the network → buffered at the switch → scheduled → transmitted to the controller → command sent to actuator → physical action → loop closes.
- The **optimization engine** (offline or runtime) tunes two knobs: (i) the *buffer size* and (ii) the *scheduling policy*.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Decision Flow for Buffer Sizing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPS Flow Specification]) --> Q1{Is traffic\ndeterministic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> NC[Use Network Calculus\nwith arrival and service curves]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it a single\nflow scenario?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> BDP[Use BDP Formula\nB equals C times RTT]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Number of\nflows greater than 10?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Kl[Use Kleinrock Formula\nB equals C times RTT divided by 2 root N]
    Q3 -- No --> Sim[Run NS-3 or OMNeT++ Simulation\nwith measured traces]
    NC --> Verify[Validate against deadline]
    BDP --> Verify
    Kl --> Verify
    Sim --> Verify
    Verify --> Done([Final Buffer Configuration])

    classDef def fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    class Start,Done,Verify def
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,NC,BDP,Kl,Sim proc
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **decision tree** a KTU student or engineer follows to pick the right buffer-sizing technique based on traffic assumptions. Always validate the chosen size against the *control loop deadline* (rightmost path).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Time-Aware Shaper (TAS) Gate Operation

```mermaid
gantt
    title IEEE 802.1Qbv Time-Aware Shaper Schedule Example
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Time Line (microseconds)
    Gate Closed (Best Effort)   :a1, 0,  20
    Gate Open  (Critical Flow)  :a2, 20, 50
    Gate Closed (Best Effort)   :a3, 50, 70
    Gate Open  (Critical Flow)  :a4, 70, 100
    Guard Band                  :crit, 100, 110
```

**Explanation:**
- A **guard band** is inserted before a protected critical flow window to ensure no partially transmitted best-effort packet can be in the link when the critical window opens.
- This eliminates **jitter** for time-critical packets at the cost of some bandwidth efficiency.
- Used in **Automotive Ethernet 1000BASE-T1** and **Industrial TSN** profiles.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Packet Scheduling Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pkt[Incoming Packet] --> Class{Packet Classifier}
    Class -- Real-time Control --> HP[High Priority Queue\nEDF Ordered]
    Class -- Soft Realtime --> MP[Medium Priority Queue\nWeighted Fair]
    Class -- Best Effort --> LP[Low Priority Queue\nFIFO or DRR]
    HP --> Gate{TAS Gate\nState}
    MP --> Gate
    LP --> Gate
    Gate -- Gate Open --> TX[Transmit on Link]
    Gate -- Gate Closed --> Wait[Wait in Queue]
    Wait --> Gate

    classDef rt fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    classDef soft fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef be fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    class HP,Class rt
    class MP soft
    class LP be
    class Pkt,Gate,TX,Wait ctrl
```

**Reading the diagram:** Packets are *classified* (typically by VLAN PCP, IP DSCP, or port), *enqueued* in priority-specific queues, and then *gated* by the Time-Aware Shaper. The TAS enforces a *temporal schedule* on top of the *priority hierarchy*.
