## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> Define **buffer sizing** in the context of CPS communication. Why is the classical bandwidth-delay product rule $B = C \cdot RTT$ often an *over-estimation* in multi-flow scenarios?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Buffer sizing is the determination of the memory allocation at a network node to absorb traffic bursts without packet loss while bounding queuing delay to satisfy CPS deadlines. In a multi-flow scenario, the flows are typically *desynchronized*, meaning their bursts do not align in phase. By Kleinrock's approximation, the aggregate arrival variance scales as $\sqrt{N}$ rather than $N$, allowing the buffer to be reduced by a factor of $2\sqrt{N}$ compared to the BDP rule. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for the limitation of BDP, 1 for the $\sqrt{N}$ factor justification.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

> Compare **FIFO** and **EDF** packet scheduling in terms of (i) deadline guarantee, (ii) jitter, and (iii) computational complexity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | FIFO | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline guarantee | None (best-effort) | Optimal (Liu-Layland) |
| Jitter | High and unpredictable | Low (with accurate time sync) |
| Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(\log n)$ per enqueue/dequeue |

FIFO transmits in arrival order, ignoring deadlines, hence unsuitable for hard real-time CPS. EDF always serves the packet with the earliest absolute deadline, achieving full link utilization up to $U \le 1$. (3 marks — 1 mark per property.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus on Buffer Sizing**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q11]**

> **(a)** Derive **Kleinrock's approximation** for the buffer size at a bottleneck link shared by $N$ persistent TCP flows. List the assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1 Gbps bottleneck link is shared by 400 desynchronized long-lived flows, each with a mean RTT of 80 ms. Compute (i) the classical BDP-based buffer, (ii) Kleinrock's buffer, and (iii) the percentage reduction in memory. Comment on whether this buffer is acceptable for a CPS control loop with sampling period $h = 2$ ms and propagation delay $d_{prop} = 0.2$ ms. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. $N$ persistent (always backlogged) flows.
2. Each flow has a stochastic, independent RTT.
3. The bottleneck link is the only contention point.
4. Service at the link is deterministic, FIFO scheduling.
5. No cross-traffic interference at other nodes.

**Derivation:**

The aggregate window $W = \sum_i W_i$ has by the central limit theorem:
- Mean: $\mathbb{E}[W] = N \cdot \mu_W$ where $\mu_W = C \cdot \mu_{RTT}/N \cdot N = C \cdot \mu_{RTT}$ (each flow keeps a window proportional to its RTT).
- Variance: $Var(W) = N \cdot \sigma_W^2$ (by independence).

The queue at the bottleneck (by Kingman's heavy-traffic approximation):

$$\mathbb{E}[Q] = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C_a^2}{2(1-\rho)}$$

with $C_a^2 = Var(W)/(\mathbb{E}[W])^2 = N\sigma_W^2/(C\mu_{RTT})^2$.

For $\rho \to 1$ and $\sigma_W \approx \mu_{RTT}/2$ (Poisson-like):

$$\mathbb{E}[Q] \approx \dfrac{C \cdot \mu_{RTT}}{2\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT}{2\sqrt{N}}$$

Hence, $\boxed{B_{Kleinrock} = \dfrac{C \cdot RTT}{2\sqrt{N}}\ \text{bits}}$. **(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 4 for derivation, 1 for the boxed result.)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $C = 10^9$ bits/s, $N = 400$, $RTT = 0.08$ s, $h = 2$ ms = 0.002 s, $d_{prop} = 0.2$ ms.

**(i) Classical BDP:**

$$B_{BDP} = C \cdot RTT = 10^9 \times 0.08 = 8 \times 10^7\ \text{bits} = 10\ \text{MB}$$

**(ii) Kleinrock's buffer:**

$$B_{Kleinrock} = \dfrac{10^9 \times 0.08}{2\sqrt{400}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^7}{40} = 2 \times 10^6\ \text{bits} = 250\ \text{KB}$$

**(iii) Reduction:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{10 - 0.25}{10} \times 100 = 97.5\%$$

**Comment on CPS suitability:**

Available time budget for queuing:

$$W_q^{max} = h - d_{prop} = 0.002 - 0.0002 = 0.0018\ \text{s} = 1.8\ \text{ms}$$

Queueing delay at 1 Gbps for $B = 2 \times 10^6$ bits:

$$W_q = \dfrac{B}{C} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{10^9} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 2\ \text{ms}$$

Since $W_q = 2\ \text{ms} > W_q^{max} = 1.8\ \text{ms}$, the **buffer is too large** for this CPS loop. The queueing delay must be brought below 1.8 ms by either (a) reducing the buffer, (b) using priority scheduling to bypass non-critical traffic, or (c) using a faster link. **(7 marks — 2 each for (i), (ii), (iii); 1 for the comment.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus on Packet Scheduling**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Q12]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** packet scheduling algorithm. State and prove the **Liu & Layland schedulability test** for $n$ flows sharing a link of capacity $C$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Three control flows in a CPS share a 10 Mbps Ethernet link:
> - Flow F1: period $T_1 = 5$ ms, payload $L_1 = 250$ bytes
> - Flow F2: period $T_2 = 10$ ms, payload $L_2 = 500$ bytes
> - Flow F3: period $T_3 = 20$ ms, payload $L_3 = 1000$ bytes
>
> Check EDF schedulability. If a new flow F4 is added with $T_4 = 8$ ms, $L_4 = 400$ bytes, can the system still meet all deadlines under EDF? If not, what is the minimum link capacity required to make the flow set schedulable? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**EDF Algorithm:** At every scheduling decision point, transmit the buffered packet with the **earliest absolute deadline** $d_i = a_i + T_i$, where $a_i$ is the arrival time and $T_i$ is the relative deadline (= period for periodic flows). If multiple packets share the earliest deadline, break ties arbitrarily.

**Liu & Layland Test (1973):**

A set of $n$ independent, preemptable periodic flows with computation/transmission times $C_i$ and periods $T_i$ is schedulable under EDF on a single processor (or link) if and only if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$$

**Proof sketch (necessity):**
If $U > 1$, then in any interval of length $T = \text{lcm}(T_1, ..., T_n)$, the total demanded work is $\sum C_i \cdot T/T_i = U \cdot T > T$, exceeding the available service. Hence, at least one deadline is missed.

**Proof sketch (sufficiency):**
Assume for contradiction that EDF misses a deadline at time $t^*$ for some flow $i$. The work done by EDF in $[0, t^*]$ equals $t^*$ (work-conserving). The demand is $\sum C_i \cdot \lfloor t^*/T_i \rfloor \le \sum C_i \cdot t^*/T_i = U \cdot t^* \le t^*$. Contradiction. Hence no deadline is missed. **(7 marks — 2 for explanation, 3 for necessity, 2 for sufficiency.)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute transmission times on 10 Mbps link:**

$$C = \dfrac{10^7\ \text{bits/s}}{8} = 1.25 \times 10^6\ \text{bytes/s}$$

$$C_1 = \dfrac{250}{1.25 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.2\ \text{ms}$$

$$C_2 = \dfrac{500}{1.25 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.4\ \text{ms}$$

$$C_3 = \dfrac{1000}{1.25 \times 10^6} = 8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.8\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \dfrac{0.2}{5} = 0.04$$

$$U_2 = \dfrac{0.4}{10} = 0.04$$

$$U_3 = \dfrac{0.8}{20} = 0.04$$

$$U = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.04 = 0.12$$

Since $U = 0.12 \le 1$, **the original 3-flow set is schedulable** with huge margin.

**Step 3 — Add Flow F4:**

$$C_4 = \dfrac{400}{1.25 \times 10^6} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.32\ \text{ms}$$

$$U_4 = \dfrac{0.32}{8} = 0.04$$

$$U_{new} = 0.12 + 0.04 = 0.16$$

Since $0.16 \le 1$, **the system is still schedulable** under EDF. The link has 84% idle capacity for future expansion.

**Step 4 — Minimum link capacity for the new flow set:**

For schedulability at full saturation, we need $C$ such that:

$$U = \dfrac{L_1}{C \cdot T_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{C \cdot T_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{C \cdot T_3} + \dfrac{L_4}{C \cdot T_4} = 1$$

In terms of bytes (multiplying numerator and denominator by 8):

$$C = \dfrac{8 L_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{8 L_2}{T_2} + \dfrac{8 L_3}{T_3} + \dfrac{8 L_4}{T_4}$$

$$C = \dfrac{8 \cdot 250}{0.005} + \dfrac{8 \cdot 500}{0.010} + \dfrac{8 \cdot 1000}{0.020} + \dfrac{8 \cdot 400}{0.008}$$

$$C = 400{,}000 + 400{,}000 + 400{,}000 + 400{,}000 = 1{,}600{,}000\ \text{bytes/s} = 12.8\ \text{Mbps}$$

**Result:** The minimum link capacity is **12.8 Mbps**. A 10 Mbps link is too slow at full saturation but is fine for the given traffic mix with 84% headroom. **(7 marks — 2 for $C_i$, 2 for $U$, 1 for verdict, 2 for minimum capacity calculation.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting assumptions in Kleinrock's derivation** — Examiners specifically test whether you can state "persistent flows", "desynchronization", and "FIFO at bottleneck". Missing 2 of these = -2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up units in buffer calculations** — Buffer in *bits* (formula) vs. *bytes* (practical). Always convert: $1\ \text{Mbps} \cdot 1\ \text{s} = 10^6\ \text{bits} = 125\ \text{KB}$.
> 3. **Confusing Liu-Layland for fixed-priority vs EDF** — The bound $U \le 1$ is for **EDF only**; for Rate Monotonic, the bound is $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the wrong algorithm is named.
> 4. **Not validating against CPS deadline** — In a CPS question, you must always check $W_q \le h - d_{prop}$. A correct buffer calculation without this check loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the leaky-bucket burst term** — In network calculus, writing only $D_{max} = T$ (forgetting $b/R$) is a classic 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam:**

- **Buffer Sizing**
  - Classical BDP: $B = C \cdot RTT$ (single-flow worst case).
  - Kleinrock: $B = C \cdot RTT / (2\sqrt{N})$ (multi-flow, desynchronized).
  - Network Calculus backlog: $B_{max} = b + rT$ (leaky-bucket arrival, latency-$T$ service).
  - Network Calculus delay: $D_{max} = b/R + T$ — always two terms!
  - CPS constraint: $W_q \le h - d_{prop} - d_{proc}$.

- **Packet Scheduling**
  - FIFO: simple, no deadline guarantee.
  - Priority Queuing: low latency for high priority, starvation risk.
  - WFQ: per-flow fairness with bounded delay $D_i \le B_i / R_i$.
  - EDF: optimal on a single link, test $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le 1$.
  - Rate Monotonic: priority inversely proportional to period; bound $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$.
  - TAS (802.1Qbv): time-triggered, deterministic, requires time sync (gPTP).
  - DRR: practical $O(1)$ fair queuing for many flows.

- **CPS-Specific Highlights**
  - Optimization target is *determinism* and *bounded worst-case delay*, not throughput.
  - Buffer is a *delay-introducing element*; smaller is better if deadlines allow.
  - TSN combines TAS + FRER + Qbu for ultra-reliable CPS communication.
  - In KTU exam, always justify the *choice* of algorithm with reference to the *control loop deadline*.

- **Common Numerical Values to Remember**
  - $1\ \text{Gbps} = 10^9\ \text{bits/s} = 125\ \text{MB/s}$.
  - $1\ \text{ms}\ \text{at}\ 1\ \text{Gbps} = 10^6\ \text{bits} \approx 122\ \text{KB}$.
  - For $n = 3$ Rate Monotonic bound: $U_{max} \approx 0.7798$.
  - For $n \to \infty$ Rate Monotonic bound: $U_{max} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

- **Frequently-Asked Exam Keywords**
  - "Bounded delay", "schedulability", "leaky-bucket", "TAS guard band", "worst-case backlog", "deterministic Ethernet", "quality of control".

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Optimization Techniques for CPS Communication**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security and Privacy in CPS Communication: Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security and Privacy in CPS Communication: Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that seamlessly integrate sensing, computation, control, and networking into physical processes. The communication substrate in CPS forms the critical bridge between the cyber (computation/communication) and physical (sensing/actuation) domains, creating an inherently **attack surface** that differs fundamentally from traditional IT systems.

**Security in CPS Communication** refers to the disciplined set of protocols, cryptographic primitives, hardware-rooted trust mechanisms, and policy frameworks designed to preserve **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authenticity, and Non-repudiation (CIAAN)** across the multi-layered communication fabric connecting sensors, edge controllers, fog nodes, and cloud orchestrators.

**Privacy in CPS Communication** is the controlled management of personally identifiable information (PII), operational telemetry, behavioral fingerprints, and physical context metadata that can be inferred from the bidirectional data flows of CPS, ensuring compliance with principles such as **data minimization, purpose limitation, and consent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The course outcome (CO) for this module mandates that students must be able to *identify, classify, and articulate the threat landscape and vulnerability taxonomy unique to CPS communication channels, including but not limited to industrial control protocols, vehicular networks, smart-grid SCADA, IoMT, and UAV swarms.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital ICU Analogy

Imagine a modern hospital ICU. The **nurses, ventilators, infusion pumps, and patient monitors** are the CPS components. The **communication cables and wireless telemetry** that carry heart rate, oxygen saturation, and infusion rate data are the CPS communication channels. Now imagine:

- A **hacker injecting false heart rate readings** (spoofed sensor data) → could trigger a fatal adrenaline overdose.
- An **eavesdropper listening to wireless telemetry** → patient privacy breach.
- A **denial-of-service jamming attack on the medical wireless network** → ventilator fails to receive control commands.
- A **replay attack where yesterday's heart rate is resent** → doctor misdiagnoses a cardiac arrest.

This is precisely the threat landscape in CPS: a compromised communication channel does not just leak data — it can cause **physical harm, environmental damage, or loss of human life**. The stakes are categorically different from leaking credit card numbers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from IT Security**
> In IT, security failure → financial/data loss. In CPS, security failure → *physical world consequences*. This is why CPS security is sometimes called **"safety-security coexistence."**

### 1.3 Key Terminology — The KTU Glossary

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential event or actor capable of exploiting a vulnerability to cause harm to the CPS |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness, flaw, or deficiency in the design, implementation, or operation of the CPS that can be exploited |
| **Attack Vector** | The specific path or methodology used by a threat actor to deliver an exploit |
| **Attack Surface** | The sum total of all points (interfaces, protocols, firmware, APIs) where an unauthorized actor can attempt to enter or extract data |
| **Adversary / Threat Actor** | An entity (insider, nation-state, hacktivist, script kiddie) with motive, capability, and opportunity |
| **Risk** | A function of Threat × Vulnerability × Impact, typically expressed qualitatively or via CVSS scoring |
| **Zero-Day** | A previously unknown vulnerability for which no patch exists |
| **Side-Channel** | Information leakage through unintended physical observables (power, EM emanation, timing, acoustic) |
| **Firmware** | Low-level software embedded in CPS hardware that controls device behavior at the hardware-software boundary |
| **Trust Boundary** | A logical or physical demarcation where data, control, or authority changes trust level |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CPS Communication Threat Landscape — Structured Breakdown

The threats against CPS communication can be stratified across **four orthogonal axes**: the **OSI layer** attacked, the **attacker model**, the **protocol category**, and the **physical consequence**. We break each down:

#### 2.1.1 By OSI Layer Targeted

- **Physical Layer (L1)**: Jamming, electromagnetic interference (EMI), physical tampering, cable cuts, hardware implants.
- **Data Link Layer (L2)**: MAC address spoofing, ARP poisoning (in TCP/IP bridged CPS), frame injection, collision flooding.
- **Network Layer (L3)**: Routing protocol attacks (RPL in IoT, BGP in industrial WAN), IP spoofing, sinkhole attacks in WSN.
- **Transport Layer (L4)**: TCP/UDP flooding, SYN flood against MODBUS/TCP gateways, session hijacking.
- **Application Layer (L7)**: Protocol-specific exploits — malformed MODBUS packets, malicious DNP3 commands, MQTT broker takeover, OPC-UA authentication bypass.

#### 2.1.2 By Attacker Model (Dolev-Yao and Beyond)

- **Passive Attacker**: Only observes communication (eavesdropping, traffic analysis, side-channel). Hardest to detect.
- **Active Attacker**: Can inject, modify, replay, or drop messages. Most common in CPS threat models.
- **Insider Attacker**: Legitimate credential holder acting maliciously (engineer with plant access). Highest impact.
- **Compromised Node**: A physically captured sensor/actuator now controlled by adversary (node capture attack in WSN).
- **Byzantine Adversary**: Sends conflicting information to different parts of the CPS to desynchronize state.

#### 2.1.3 By Protocol Family

| Protocol Family | Typical CPS Domain | Inherent Vulnerabilities |
|---|---|---|
| **MODBUS / MODBUS-TCP** | SCADA, Industrial PLCs | No authentication, no encryption, broadcast friendly |
| **DNP3** | Power grid SCADA | Weak authentication (pre-IEEE 1815), cleartext by default |
| **IEC 61850 / GOOSE** | Substation automation | Multicast cleartext, no native encryption (relies on network segregation) |
| **OPC-UA** | Industrial M2M | Complex security model; misconfiguration is common pitfall |
| **Zigbee / Z-Wave** | Smart home, building automation | Key management weaknesses, replay vulnerabilities |
| **MQTT / CoAP** | IoT, edge-cloud | Broker centralization = single point of failure |
| **CAN Bus** | Automotive | No authentication, broadcast bus, easy message injection |
| **WirelessHART / ISA100.11a** | Process automation | Key distribution complexity, join-time DoS |
| **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT** | Wide-area CPS | Session key reuse, join procedure vulnerabilities |
| **5G URLLC** | Mission-critical CPS | Slicing isolation breaches, signaling storms |

#### 2.1.4 By Physical Consequence

- **Information Disclosure**: Privacy breach, industrial espionage.
- **Degradation of Service**: Reduced quality, delayed actuation.
- **Loss of Control**: Adversary gains command authority over actuators (most dangerous).
- **Physical Damage**: Overpressure, overspeed, thermal runaway, collision.
- **Environmental Release**: Chemical spill, radiation release, gas release.
- **Loss of Life**: Medical device failure, autonomous vehicle crash.

### 2.2 The Vulnerability Taxonomy in CPS Communication

Vulnerabilities in CPS communication arise from the **convergence of legacy industrial protocols (designed pre-cyber) with modern IP-based networking (designed for openness)**. The taxonomy:

#### 2.2.1 Protocol-Inherent Vulnerabilities

These exist by design because protocols were created when air-gapping was the assumed security model.

- **Cleartext Transmission**: No encryption, e.g., MODBUS, classic DNP3, GOOSE.
- **No Authentication**: Any device on the bus can issue commands, e.g., CAN, MODBUS.
- **No Integrity Protection**: No MAC, no digital signature, e.g., most serial fieldbus protocols.
- **Static / Hardcoded Credentials**: Default passwords ("admin/admin"), factory keys.
- **Lack of Replay Protection**: No sequence number enforcement.
- **Weak Random Number Generation**: Predictable nonces in challenge-response.

#### 2.2.2 Implementation Vulnerabilities

- **Buffer Overflows** in protocol stack parsers.
- **Integer Overflow / Underflow** in payload length fields.
- **Format String Bugs** in diagnostic interfaces.
- **Race Conditions** in firmware update mechanisms.
- **Insecure Boot Loaders** allowing arbitrary firmware flash.

#### 2.2.3 Configuration Vulnerabilities

- **Default credentials** never changed.
- **Unnecessary services** enabled (Telnet, FTP, HTTP on industrial devices).
- **Flat network topology** with no segmentation.
- **Open debug ports** (JTAG, UART, SPI) accessible in deployed devices.
- **Unencrypted wireless** (WEP, open Wi-Fi) on plant floor.

#### 2.2.4 Network Architecture Vulnerabilities

- **Insufficient Network Segmentation**: IT and OT networks bridged without DMZ.
- **Converged IT/OT Risk**: Malware pivots from IT to OT (e.g., NotPetya, 2017).
- **Insecure Remote Access**: VPN misconfiguration, exposed RDP/VNC.
- **Lack of Intrusion Detection**: Industrial protocols are opaque to IT-grade IDS.

#### 2.2.5 Supply Chain & Lifecycle Vulnerabilities

- **Counterfeit Hardware**: Trojan-inserted ICs.
- **Vulnerable Third-Party Libraries**: Heartbleed in embedded OpenSSL.
- **End-of-Life Components**: Unpatched legacy systems.
- **Insecure Update Channels**: OTA update not authenticated, allows persistent compromise.

#### 2.2.6 Human & Operational Vulnerabilities

- **Social Engineering**: Spear-phishing targeting engineers.
- **Insider Threats**: Disgruntled operators, bribed contractors.
- **Poor Security Hygiene**: Sticky notes with passwords, shared credentials.
- **Lack of Security Training**: Engineers unaware of threat models.

### 2.3 The Threat Modeling Frameworks (KTU High-Yield)

Three frameworks dominate CPS threat modeling — knowing all three is a **frequent KTU exam question**.

#### A. STRIDE (Microsoft, adapted to CPS)

| Letter | Threat | CPS-Specific Example |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Spoofing | Forging sensor identity to inject false telemetry |
| **T** | Tampering | Modifying actuator command setpoints in transit |
| **R** | Repudiation | Operator denies issuing dangerous command; no audit trail |
| **I** | Information Disclosure | Eavesdropping on patient health telemetry |
| **D** | Denial of Service | Jamming wireless CPS link, causing safety shutdown |
| **E** | Elevation of Privilege | Sensor firmware upgrade grants root shell |

#### B. PASTA (Process for Attack Simulation and Threat Analysis)

A seven-stage **risk-centric** framework: Define Objectives, Define Technical Scope, Application Decomposition, Threat Analysis, Vulnerability & Weakness Analysis, Attack Modeling, Risk & Impact Analysis. Used heavily in industrial CPS deployments.

#### C. ATT&CK for ICS (MITRE)

A curated knowledge base of **adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs)** specifically observed in industrial cyber attacks. KTU students should know it covers 11 tactic categories from Initial Access to Impair Process Control.

### 2.4 Privacy Threats in CPS — A Distinct Domain

Privacy in CPS is fundamentally different from web/IT privacy because of **sensor-driven inference**. Even without explicit PII, CPS data can reveal:

- **Behavioral Patterns**: Smart meter data reveals occupancy, sleep patterns, appliance usage.
- **Health Inference**: Wearable CPS data infers chronic conditions, mental health.
- **Location Tracking**: Connected vehicle CPS reveals daily routes, frequently visited places.
- **Identity Re-Identification**: Anonymized CPS datasets can be re-linked to individuals.
- **Inference Attacks**: Side-channel data (battery drain, network traffic) reveals user activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy-Preserving Computation in CPS**
> KTU 2024 emphasizes three techniques: **Homomorphic Encryption** (compute on encrypted data), **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** (joint computation without revealing inputs), and **Differential Privacy** (add calibrated noise to query results). All three are examineable.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Point | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| CIA Triad → CIAAN | Add Authenticity & Non-repudiation for CPS | **5-mark question** almost every exam |
| Attack Surface Formula | $AS = \sum_{i} I_i \cdot C_i$ where $I_i$ = interface, $C_i$ = connectivity exposure | Quantitative exam |
| Risk | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Foundation for risk assessment |
| STRIDE | 6-letter mnemonic, CPS-adapted | Theory 14-mark question |
| CVSS Score | 0.0 – 10.0 (None → Critical) | Definition question |
| Dolev-Yao Model | Attacker controls the network; passive+active | Exam theory |
| Stuxnet | First publicly known CPS-targeted worm (Iran, 2010) | Real-world case study |
| BlackEnergy 3 | Ukraine power grid attack (Dec 2015) | Real-world case study |
| TRITON / TRISIS | Schneider Triconex SIS attack (2017) | Real-world case study |
| NotPetya | IT-OT propagation, caused OT shutdown (2017) | Real-world case study |
| Oldsmar Water Plant | Florida water treatment tampering (Feb 2021) | Recent case study |
| Colonial Pipeline | Ransomware-induced fuel shutdown (May 2021) | Recent case study |
| Differential Privacy | $\epsilon$-budget controls privacy loss | Privacy question |
| Replay Attack | Captured valid message resent later | Definition question |
| MITM in CPS | Adversary between controller and actuator | Diagram question |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Quantitative Risk Model — Exhaustive Derivation

The fundamental risk equation in CPS security is derived from the intersection of three independent factors. We derive it from first principles:

**Step 1: Define the Threat Event Rate**

Let $T$ be the expected number of threat events per unit time, expressed as:

$$T = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k \cdot P_k$$

where $A_k$ is the activity level of adversary class $k$ (e.g., nation-state, insider, script kiddie), and $P_k$ is the probability of that class attempting an attack per unit time. The sum is over all $n$ distinct adversary classes.

**Step 2: Define the Vulnerability Exposure**

A vulnerability in a CPS communication path has a probability $V$ of being successfully exploited given that an attempt is made:

$$V = \frac{E_s}{E_t}$$

where $E_s$ = number of successful exploitations in a representative period, and $E_t$ = total exploitation attempts in the same period. Empirically, $V$ is bounded as $0 \leq V \leq 1$.

**Step 3: Define the Impact Magnitude**

Impact $I$ is the magnitude of harm (financial, safety, environmental) given a successful exploitation. Normalize it as:

$$I = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{max}}$$

where $C_{actual}$ is the cost of the realized damage and $C_{max}$ is the maximum conceivable damage from any single exploitation event. Thus $0 \leq I \leq 1$.

**Step 4: Combine into Risk**

The expected loss per unit time is:

$$R = T \cdot V \cdot I$$

This is the foundational CPS risk equation. The unit of $R$ is "fraction of maximum damage per unit time" if $I$ is normalized. For absolute financial risk, multiply $R$ by $C_{max}$:

$$R_{absolute} = T \cdot V \cdot I \cdot C_{max}$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

Suppose a CPS gateway has:
- Threat rate $T = 2$ attack attempts per month (combined adversary classes)
- Vulnerability $V = 0.3$ (30% of attempts succeed due to lack of authentication)
- Impact $I = 0.8$ (a successful attack could disrupt plant production for days)
- Maximum loss $C_{max} = \text{INR } 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (1 crore per event)

Then:

$$R = 2 \times 0.3 \times 0.8 = 0.48 \text{ events/month}$$

$$R_{absolute} = 0.48 \times 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = \text{INR } 48{,}00{,}000 \text{ per month}$$

> This quantitative framing is **commonly tested** as a 7-mark numerical in KTU ESE.

### 3.2 Attack Surface Quantification — Full Derivation

The attack surface of a CPS communication system can be quantified across three dimensions: **entry/exit points, channels, and untrusted data items**.

**Step 1: Enumerate the Communication Interfaces**

For a CPS with components (sensors, actuators, controllers, gateways, cloud), list every communication interface. Let $I = \{I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_m\}$ be the set of all interfaces.

**Step 2: Assess Exposure of Each Interface**

Define exposure $E(I_i)$ as the degree to which interface $I_i$ is reachable by an adversary:

$$E(I_i) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } I_i \text{ is physically inaccessible and air-gapped} \\ 0.3 & \text{if } I_i \text{ is on a segmented, monitored network} \\ 0.7 & \text{if } I_i \text{ is on a shared IT/OT network} \\ 1.0 & \text{if } I_i \text{ is exposed to the public Internet} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Aggregate the Attack Surface**

$$AS = \sum_{i=1}^{m} E(I_i) \cdot w_i$$

where $w_i$ is a criticality weight reflecting the privilege level or impact of compromising interface $I_i$. A typical weighting scheme: $w_i = 1$ for sensors, $w_i = 2$ for controllers, $w_i = 4$ for actuators, $w_i = 8$ for safety-instrumented systems (per IEC 62443).

**Step 4: Worked Example**

Consider a CPS with 10 sensors, 2 controllers, 5 actuators, 1 SIS, all on a shared IT/OT network (so $E = 0.7$ for all):

$$AS = (10 \times 1 + 2 \times 2 + 5 \times 4 + 1 \times 8) \times 0.7$$

$$AS = (10 + 4 + 20 + 8) \times 0.7 = 42 \times 0.7 = 29.4$$

Compare to a fully segmented deployment ($E = 0.3$):

$$AS = 42 \times 0.3 = 12.6$$

> The reduction factor is $\frac{29.4}{12.6} \approx 2.33$, meaning segmentation reduces the attack surface by a factor of ~2.3. This is **exactly the kind of calculation** KTU examiners love to set.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Protocol Vulnerabilities

The following matrix is **examination-grade**; reproduce it faithfully:

| Property | MODBUS | DNP3 (Secure) | OPC-UA | IEC 61850 GOOSE | MQTT (TLS) | CAN Bus | Zigbee |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Authentication | ❌ | ✅ (v2) | ✅ (cert) | ❌ | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ | ✅ (key) |
| Encryption | ❌ | ❌ (cleartext) | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ (multicast) | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ | ✅ (AES) |
| Integrity (MAC) | ❌ | ❌ (no MAC) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ | ✅ (MIC) |
| Replay Protection | ❌ | Partial | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ | ✅ (TLS) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Designed Era | 1979 | 1990s | 2008 | 2004 | 1999 (IoT-ized 2010s) | 1983 (Bosch) | 2003 |
| CPS Domain | Industrial | Power grid | Industrial M2M | Substations | IoT/Edge | Automotive | Home/Building |
| Primary Weakness | No security at all | Complex cert mgmt | Misconfig risk | Cleartext multicast | Broker centralization | No auth, no seg | Key distribution |

### 3.4 Threat-to-Vulnerability Mapping Matrix

This mapping is **highly examinable** as a "design" question:

| Threat | Vulnerability Exploited | Attack Vector | CPS Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Cleartext transmission | Wireless sniffing | Privacy breach, IP theft |
| Replay Attack | No sequence number | Captured-packet replay | Outdated setpoint re-applied |
| MITM | No mutual authentication | ARP spoof + proxy | False commands accepted |
| False Data Injection | No integrity check | Malicious sensor forging | Wrong control decisions |
| DoS / Jamming | No redundancy | RF jamming | Safety shutdown |
| Ransomware | Unpatched Windows HMI | Phishing → lateral move | Plant halt |
| Firmware Tamper | Unverified OTA | Man-in-update | Persistent backdoor |
| Side-Channel | EM emanation | Near-field probe | Crypto key extraction |
| Byzantine | No consensus | Multi-sensor disagreement | State desynchronization |
| Sybil | Weak identity binding | Fake node IDs | Voting/aggregation skew |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CPS Communication Security Architecture — Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER - Analytics and Storage"]
    fogLayer["FOG LAYER - Edge Gateways and Brokers"]
    ctrlLayer["CONTROL LAYER - PLCs and RTUs"]
    fieldLayer["FIELD LAYER - Sensors and Actuators"]
    physLayer["PHYSICAL PROCESS - Plant Vehicle Grid Body"]

    subgraph Threats[THREAT DOMAIN]
        threat1["External Adversary"]
        threat2["Malicious Insider"]
        threat3["Compromised Node"]
        threat4["Supply Chain Implant"]
    end

    subgraph Defenses[DEFENSE IN DEPTH]
        def1["Cryptographic Primitives - AES TLS ECC"]
        def2["Authentication and Authorization - PKI RBAC"]
        def3["Network Segmentation - DMZ VLAN"]
        def4["Intrusion Detection - ICS IDS"]
        def5["Hardware Root of Trust - TPM TEE"]
    end

    cloudLayer --> fogLayer
    fogLayer --> ctrlLayer
    ctrlLayer --> fieldLayer
    fieldLayer --> physLayer

    threat1 -.attacks.-> fogLayer
    threat2 -.attacks.-> ctrlLayer
    threat3 -.attacks.-> fieldLayer
    threat4 -.attacks.-> cloudLayer

    def1 --- cloudLayer
    def2 --- fogLayer
    def3 --- ctrlLayer
    def4 --- fieldLayer
    def5 --- physLayer

    style Threats fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style Defenses fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
```

### 4.2 Threat Classification Taxonomy — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root["CPS COMMUNICATION THREATS"]

    root --> cat1["INTERCEPTION"]
    root --> cat2["INTERRUPTION"]
    root --> cat3["MODIFICATION"]
    root --> cat4["FABRICATION"]

    cat1 --> c1a["Eavesdropping"]
    cat1 --> c1b["Traffic Analysis"]
    cat1 --> c1c["Side Channel Leakage"]

    cat2 --> c2a["Jamming and DoS"]
    cat2 --> c2b["Network Partition"]
    cat2 --> c2c["Resource Exhaustion"]

    cat3 --> c3a["Data Tampering"]
    cat3 --> c3b["Command Injection"]
    cat3 --> c3c["False Data Injection"]

    cat4 --> c4a["Identity Spoofing"]
    cat4 --> c4b["Replay Attack"]
    cat4 --> c4c["Sybil Attack"]

    style root fill:#0066cc,color:#ffffff
    style cat1 fill:#ffcc00
    style cat2 fill:#ff9900
    style cat3 fill:#ff3300
    style cat4 fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Attack Vector Flow — MITM Against CPS Field Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Controller PLC"] -->|"MODBUS Command Cleartext"| M
    M["Adversary MITM Proxy"] -->|"Modified Command"| B["Actuator"]
    B -->|"Telemetry Cleartext"| M
    M -->|"Filtered Telemetry"| A

    subgraph Impacts
        I1["False Setpoint Delivered"]
        I2["Operator Sees Normal Telemetry"]
        I3["Physical Process Diverges Silently"]
    end

    M -.causes.-> I1
    M -.causes.-> I2
    M -.causes.-> I3

    style M fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style Impacts fill:#ffe5e5
```

### 4.4 Vulnerability Lifecycle in CPS Communication

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Stage 1: Design Phase"] --> s2["Stage 2: Implementation"]
    s2 --> s3["Stage 3: Deployment"]
    s3 --> s4["Stage 4: Operation"]
    s4 --> s5["Stage 5: End of Life"]

    s1 -.introduces.-> v1["Architecture Flaw - Flat Network"]
    s2 -.introduces.-> v2["Code Bug - Buffer Overflow"]
    s3 -.introduces.-> v3["Config Error - Default Password"]
    s4 -.introduces.-> v4["Operational Drift - Unpatched"]
    s5 -.introduces.-> v5["EOL Risk - No More Patches"]

    s1 --> m1["Mitigation: Security by Design"]
    s2 --> m2["Mitigation: SAST and Code Review"]
    s3 --> m3["Mitigation: Hardening Baseline"]
    s4 --> m4["Mitigation: Patch Management"]
    s5 --> m5["Mitigation: Migration Plan"]

    style v1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style v2 fill:#ffe5e5
    style v3 fill:#ffe5e5
    style v4 fill:#ffe5e5
    style v5 fill:#ffe5e5

    style m2 fill:#e5ffe5
    style m3 fill:#e5ffe5
    style m4 fill:#e5ffe5
    style m5 fill:#e5ffe5
```

### 4.5 Privacy Threat Model — CPS Data Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    c1["COLLECTION - Sensors"] --> c2["TRANSMISSION - Wireless Wired"]
    c2 --> c3["STORAGE - Cloud Edge"]
    c3 --> c4["PROCESSING - Analytics ML"]
    c4 --> c5["SHARING - Third Parties"]

    c1 -.risk.-> p1["Consent Violation"]
    c2 -.risk.-> p2["Interception Eavesdropping"]
    c3 -.risk.-> p3["Insider Breach Database"]
    c4 -.risk.-> p4["Inference Attack Re Identification"]
    c5 -.risk.-> p5["Purpose Limitation Breach"]

    style p1 fill:#ffe5e5
    style p2 fill:#ffe5e5
    style p3 fill:#ffe5e5
    style p4 fill:#ffe5e5
    style p5 fill:#ffe5e5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Distinguish between a threat, a vulnerability, and a risk in the context of CPS communication. Provide one CPS-specific example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Threat** is any potential event or actor that can cause harm. *Example*: A nation-state adversary targeting a power grid SCADA network.
- **Vulnerability** is a weakness that can be exploited. *Example*: A MODBUS-TCP gateway with no authentication enabled.
- **Risk** is the product of threat, vulnerability, and impact. *Example*: The risk that the unauthenticated MODBUS gateway (vulnerability) is exploited by the nation-state actor (threat), causing a substation shutdown (impact).

The trio is logically distinct: a vulnerability can exist without a threat exploiting it; a threat can exist without finding a vulnerability; risk is the realized intersection of all three.

---

**Q2. List the six categories of the STRIDE threat model and give one CPS communication example for any three.**

**Model Answer:**

The STRIDE categories are: **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.

| STRIDE | CPS Communication Example |
|---|---|
| Spoofing | Forging the source address of a sensor in an industrial WSN |
| Tampering | Modifying a GOOSE message in a substation LAN |
| Repudiation | Operator denies issuing a pump shutdown command; no audit log retained |
| Information Disclosure | Eavesdropping on CAN bus traffic in a connected vehicle |
| Denial of Service | Jamming the wireless link between a UAV and ground control |
| Elevation of Privilege | Exploiting a buffer overflow in the fieldbus gateway to gain root shell |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern, Module 4 Coverage]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, classify the major **threats to CPS communication** based on (i) the OSI layer targeted and (ii) the protocol family used. For each category, give at least one real-world attack example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CIAAN security model** as it applies to CPS communication. For each property, illustrate with one CPS-specific scenario demonstrating what happens if that property is violated.

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Part (i) — Threats by OSI Layer**

- **Physical Layer Threats**: Jamming of wireless CPS links (e.g., GPS jamming against autonomous vehicles), EMI attacks on industrial sensors, physical tampering of field devices. *Real-world example*: In 2017, GPS jamming affected ship navigation in the Black Sea.
- **Data Link Layer Threats**: MAC spoofing on industrial Ethernet, ARP poisoning in TCP/IP-bridged SCADA, CAN bus message injection in automobiles. *Real-world example*: Charlie Miller and Chris Valasek's 2015 Jeep Cherokee remote exploit used CAN bus injection.
- **Network Layer Threats**: RPL sinkhole attacks in IoT, BGP hijacking against industrial WAN, IP spoofing. *Real-world example*: The 2008 Hatch nuclear plant shutdown was triggered by a software update that reset the network.
- **Transport Layer Threats**: SYN flooding of SCADA gateways, TCP session hijacking of MODBUS-TCP. *Example*: The 2014 Havex trojan used OPC scanning.
- **Application Layer Threats**: Malformed MODBUS packets, OPC-UA authentication bypass, MQTT broker takeover, DNP3 command injection. *Example*: The 2015 Ukraine power grid attack used BlackEnergy 3 to open substation breakers.

**Part (ii) — Threats by Protocol Family**

Refer to the protocol table in Section 2.5; the student should reproduce at least 4–5 protocol families and their representative vulnerabilities with examples.

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

The CIAAN model extends the classical CIA triad with two CPS-critical properties:

1. **Confidentiality (C)**: Ensures CPS data (telemetry, commands, configuration) is readable only by authorized parties. *Violation scenario*: An attacker eavesdrops on wireless patient telemetry in a hospital IoMT, revealing sensitive health data — a violation of privacy regulations.

2. **Integrity (I)**: Ensures CPS messages are not modified in transit. *Violation scenario*: An attacker modifies a setpoint command in a chemical plant, changing "50°C" to "500°C" — catastrophic overpressure may result.

3. **Availability (A)**: Ensures CPS communication is timely and reliable. *Violation scenario*: Jamming the wireless link to a remote substation prevents control commands from reaching, leading to uncontrolled state drift.

4. **Authenticity (A)**: Ensures the sender of a CPS message is who it claims to be. *Violation scenario*: A spoofed sensor identity injects false "normal" readings, hiding an incipient failure.

5. **Non-repudiation (N)**: Ensures that a sender cannot deny issuing a command. *Violation scenario*: A disgruntled operator issues a dangerous shutdown, then denies it; without signed audit logs, forensic investigation fails.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **vulnerability taxonomy in CPS communication** with reference to protocol-inherent, implementation, configuration, and supply-chain categories. Give at least two examples per category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CPS deployment has 8 sensors, 3 controllers, 6 actuators, and 1 safety instrumented system, all on a shared IT/OT network. Using the attack surface formula, compute the attack surface value. Compare with a fully segmented deployment. Interpret the result.

---

**Model Solution for Q(a):**

The CPS vulnerability taxonomy has six major classes (as detailed in Section 2.2). The student should elaborate each:

- **Protocol-Inherent**: Lack of authentication in MODBUS, lack of encryption in DNP3, no MAC in GOOSE.
- **Implementation**: Buffer overflows in protocol stack parsers, race conditions in firmware update routines.
- **Configuration**: Default credentials, unnecessary services enabled, weak Wi-Fi security on plant floor.
- **Supply Chain**: Counterfeit PLC hardware with embedded trojans, vulnerable third-party crypto libraries (e.g., outdated OpenSSL), end-of-life components without patches.
- **Architectural**: Lack of IT/OT segmentation, insecure remote access via misconfigured VPN.
- **Human**: Social engineering, weak password hygiene, shared credentials.

**Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Given:**
- 8 sensors, weight $w = 1$
- 3 controllers, weight $w = 2$
- 6 actuators, weight $w = 4$
- 1 SIS, weight $w = 8$
- Shared IT/OT network: $E = 0.7$
- Segmented network: $E = 0.3$

**Step 1: Compute weighted interface count**

$$W = 8 \times 1 + 3 \times 2 + 6 \times 4 + 1 \times 8$$

$$W = 8 + 6 + 24 + 8 = 46$$

**Step 2: Compute attack surface (shared network)**

$$AS_{shared} = 46 \times 0.7 = 32.2$$

**Step 3: Compute attack surface (segmented)**

$$AS_{seg} = 46 \times 0.3 = 13.8$$

**Step 4: Compute reduction factor**

$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{32.2 - 13.8}{32.2} \times 100\% = \frac{18.4}{32.2} \times 100\% \approx 57.14\%$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

Segmentation alone reduces the attack surface by approximately **57%**. While significant, segmentation is not a panacea — residual attack surface of 13.8 still requires cryptographic, authentication, and intrusion-detection controls. Defense in depth is essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Conflating "threat" with "vulnerability"**: These are distinct terms. A threat is the actor/event; a vulnerability is the weakness. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Omitting the fifth element in CIAAN**: Many students write CIA (3 elements) instead of CIAAN (5 elements). Non-repudiation and Authenticity are explicitly added for CPS — losing 1 mark each is common.
> 3. **Forgetting the weight in attack surface formula**: Writing $AS = E \times n$ without criticality weights is a 2-mark deduction. SIS and actuators must be weighted higher than sensors.
> 4. **Stating STRIDE without CPS examples**: Generic web/IT examples earn 0–1 mark. Always use a CPS scenario (sensor, PLC, vehicle, substation, etc.).
> 5. **Ignoring privacy in CPS discussions**: Many students focus only on security. Privacy threats (inference, re-identification, behavioral leakage) are explicitly part of the syllabus — ignoring them costs 2–3 marks on a 14-mark question.
> 6. **Naming a real attack without year and target**: "Stuxnet" alone is partial credit. "Stuxnet, 2010, Iranian Natanz uranium enrichment centrifuges" is full credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Before your exam, ensure you can confidently state, define, or sketch each of the following:**

- [ ] **Define** CPS communication security and explain why it differs from IT security (safety-security coexistence).
- [ ] **List** the five elements of the CIAAN model with a CPS example for each.
- [ ] **Differentiate** threat, vulnerability, and risk; write the fundamental risk equation $R = T \times V \times I$.
- [ ] **Classify** threats by OSI layer (L1–L7) and give one CPS example per layer.
- [ ] **Name** the six categories of STRIDE and one CPS example per category.
- [ ] **List** at least five protocol families used in CPS and their inherent security weaknesses.
- [ ] **Categorize** vulnerabilities into protocol-inherent, implementation, configuration, supply-chain, architectural, and human classes.
- [ ] **Compute** attack surface using $AS = \sum E(I_i) \cdot w_i$ with appropriate weights.
- [ ] **Describe** the Dolev-Yao attacker model and the PASTA threat modeling framework.
- [ ] **Recall** the MITRE ATT&CK for ICS framework as a knowledge base of adversary TTPs.
- [ ] **Cite** at least four real-world CPS cyber incidents: Stuxnet (2010), BlackEnergy 3 (Ukraine 2015), TRITON/TRISIS (2017), Oldsmar (2021), Colonial Pipeline (2021).
- [ ] **Explain** privacy threats unique to CPS: behavioral inference, re-identification, location tracking, side-channel leakage.
- [ ] **Name** three privacy-preserving techniques: homomorphic encryption, SMPC, differential privacy.
- [ ] **Sketch** a layered CPS communication security architecture with at least three defense-in-depth layers.
- [ ] **Identify** the threat categories Interception, Interruption, Modification, Fabrication and their CPS examples.
- [ ] **State** why legacy industrial protocols (MODBUS, DNP3, GOOSE, CAN) lack security by design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Measures for CPS Communication (Encryption and Authentication)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT602 - COMMUNICATION FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Measures for CPS Communication (Encryption and Authentication)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is CPS Communication Security?

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are tightly integrated networks of physical actuators/sensors and computational (cyber) elements that communicate over often wireless, resource-constrained, and exposed channels (e.g., industrial IoT, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, medical implants). Because these channels are inherently untrusted, **CPS communication security** is the discipline of protecting the confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, and availability of data exchanged between CPS nodes (sensors, edge gateways, controllers, cloud servers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Module 4):**
> *Security measures for CPS communication refer to the cryptographic, protocol-level, and architectural mechanisms — primarily encryption (symmetric and asymmetric) and authentication (entity and message) — used to defend the bidirectional data flows in a Cyber-Physical System against eavesdropping, tampering, replay, impersonation, and denial-of-service attacks.*

### 1.2 The Two Pillars Covered in This Module

| Pillar | Purpose | One-Line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** | Confidentiality | Converts readable **plaintext** into unreadable **ciphertext** using a mathematical algorithm and a key. |
| **Authentication** | Identity & Integrity Proof | Verifies *who* sent the data and *that* the data was not altered in transit. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Locked Diplomatic Pouch

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Diplomatic Courier"**
> Imagine a CPS sensor in a power substation sending voltage readings to a control center.
> - **Encryption** is like sealing the reading inside a tamper-proof diplomatic pouch that only the control center can open with a special key. Even if a thief steals the truck, he cannot read the contents.
> - **Authentication** is like a signed, government-issued **passport** inside the pouch. When the control center receives it, the officer checks the seal, the photograph, and the signature — confirming (a) the courier is genuine (entity authentication) and (b) the pouch was not opened in transit (message authentication / integrity).
> - A **digital signature** is the ambassador's unique wax seal — anyone holding the public seal can verify authenticity, but only the ambassador possesses the private seal used to stamp.

### 1.4 The Four Pillars of CPS Security (CIA + N)

```
C - Confidentiality   → Encryption (AES, RSA, ECC)
I - Integrity         → Hashing (SHA-256), MAC, HMAC
A - Authentication    → Digital certificates, challenge-response
N - Non-Repudiation   → Digital Signatures (RSA-DS, ECDSA)
A - Availability      → DoS resistance (out of scope of this topic, but vital)
```

### 1.5 Why Standard IT Security ≠ CPS Security

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Examiners:**
> CPS nodes are **resource-constrained** (limited CPU, RAM, battery). Heavy algorithms like full TLS with RSA-2048 may be infeasible on a Class-0 IoT sensor (often < 100 KB RAM, < 1 MIPS). Therefore CPS security uses **lightweight cryptography** (e.g., PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, ASCON) and **lightweight authentication** (e.g., Physically Unclonable Functions, Elliptic Curve Cryptography with small key sizes like secp192r1 / Curve25519).

### 1.6 Core Vocabulary to Memorize (Board-Favorite Terms)

- **Plaintext / Ciphertext** — original vs encrypted data.
- **Cryptographic Key** — secret parameter that controls encryption/decryption.
- **Symmetric Key** — same key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES).
- **Asymmetric Key Pair** — public key (encrypt/verify) + private key (decrypt/sign) (e.g., RSA, ECC).
- **Hash Function** — one-way, fixed-length digital fingerprint (e.g., SHA-256 outputs **256 bits**).
- **Digital Signature** — hash value encrypted with sender's private key.
- **Digital Certificate** — public key bound to identity, signed by a trusted CA.
- **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)** — ecosystem of CAs, certificates, and key management.
- **Replay Attack** — re-sending a valid old message; prevented by **nonces/timestamps**.
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** — attacker secretly relays/alters communication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of CPS Communication Security Measures

### A. Encryption Mechanisms (Confidentiality)

#### A1. Symmetric-Key Encryption
- **One shared secret key $K$** is used for both encryption and decryption.
- Operations are computationally cheap → ideal for **bulk sensor data streams** in CPS.
- Block ciphers operate on fixed-size blocks; stream ciphers operate bit-by-bit.

**Block Cipher Modes (must know for KTU):**
1. **ECB (Electronic Codebook)** — each block encrypted independently. ❌ Insecure (leaks patterns).
2. **CBC (Cipher Block Chaining)** — each plaintext block XORed with previous ciphertext block before encryption. Uses an **IV (Initialization Vector)** for the first block.
3. **CFB / OFB** — turns a block cipher into a stream cipher.
4. **CTR (Counter)** — encrypts an incrementing counter; widely used in modern protocols.
5. **GCM (Galois/Counter Mode)** — CTR + built-in authentication (AEAD — Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data). The **gold standard** for CPS links (used in TLS 1.3, IPsec, MACsec).

#### A2. Asymmetric-Key Encryption (Public-Key Cryptography)
- Uses a mathematically related **key pair**: public key $K_{pub}$ and private key $K_{priv}$.
- **Anyone** can encrypt with $K_{pub}$, but **only the holder of $K_{priv}$** can decrypt.
- Computationally expensive → used to **bootstrap** a session, then hand off to symmetric encryption (this is what TLS does — the **hybrid model**).

**Algorithms you must know:**
- **RSA** — based on the hardness of factoring large integers $n = p \cdot q$.
- **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** — based on the hardness of the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**. Provides **equivalent security with much smaller keys** (a 256-bit ECC key ≈ 3072-bit RSA). Critical for CPS!

#### A3. Lightweight Cryptography (CPS-Specific)
- Algorithms designed for **< 2 KGE (gate equivalents)**, low power, low latency.
- Examples: **PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, ASCON** (NIST winner for lightweight AEAD, 2023).
- Symmetric, block/stream, often 64- or 80-bit keys (sacrificing some security for efficiency).

---

### B. Authentication Mechanisms

#### B1. Message Authentication — Hashing
- A **cryptographic hash function** $H(m)$ maps variable-length input $m$ to a **fixed-length** digest.
- Properties (must know — board question every year):
  1. **Pre-image resistance** — given $h$, infeasible to find $m$ such that $H(m) = h$.
  2. **Second pre-image resistance** — given $m_1$, infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
  3. **Collision resistance** — infeasible to find *any* $m_1, m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
- **SHA-256** output length = **256 bits**. **MD5** is broken — never use.
- A hash alone gives **integrity**, not authentication. For authentication, you need a **MAC**.

#### B2. Message Authentication Codes (MAC)
- **MAC = symmetric-key integrity + authentication.** Computed as $MAC_K(m) = H(K \parallel m)$ or via a dedicated algorithm.
- **HMAC (Hash-based MAC)** — wraps a hash (e.g., SHA-256) with two key-derived pads:
  $$HMAC_K(m) = H\big((K_{out} \oplus opad) \parallel H((K_{in} \oplus ipad) \parallel m)\big)$$

#### B3. Digital Signatures — Asymmetric Authentication + Non-Repudiation
- Sender computes: $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(m))$.
- Receiver verifies with $K_{pub}$: $\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(H(m), \sigma) \to \text{True/False}$.
- Provides: **integrity + authentication + non-repudiation** (the hallmark of a digital signature).
- Algorithms: **RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA (Ed25519)**.

#### B4. Entity Authentication Protocols
1. **Password-based** — weak, vulnerable to brute force / phishing.
2. **Challenge-Response** — verifier sends a random **nonce** $N$; prover returns $f(K, N)$ (e.g., signed nonce). Prevents replay.
3. **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)** — prover convinces verifier without revealing the secret.
4. **PUF (Physically Unclonable Function)** — hardware fingerprint of a chip; used to derive a unique device key in CPS sensors without storing a secret in memory.
5. **Certificate-based (PKI)** — server identity is vouched for by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)** using **X.509 certificates**.

#### B5. Hybrid CPS Authentication Example
- **TLS 1.3 Handshake (CPS Gateway ↔ Cloud):**
  1. ClientHello (with supported ciphersuites + ephemeral ECDHE key share).
  2. ServerHello + ServerCertificate (X.509) + ServerKeyShare.
  3. Client verifies certificate chain (entity authentication).
  4. Both derive a **shared symmetric session key** via ECDHE (forward secrecy).
  5. All subsequent data encrypted with **AES-128-GCM** and authenticated with its built-in MAC.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key / Block Size | Standard | Real-World CPS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Symmetric Encryption** | $C = E_K(P),\;\; P = D_K(C)$ | 128 / 256-bit keys | AES-128/256 | Sensor → gateway bulk data |
| 2 | **RSA Encryption** | $C = P^e \bmod n$ | $\vert n\vert \geq 2048$ bits | PKCS#1 v2.2 | Firmware signing |
| 3 | **RSA Decryption** | $P = C^d \bmod n$ | where $n = p \cdot q$ | — | — |
| 4 | **RSA Key Gen** | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n)$ | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | — | — |
| 5 | **ECC Key Agreement (ECDH)** | $S = a \cdot B = b \cdot A$ on curve $E$ | 256-bit (secp256r1) | NIST SP 800-186 | Lightweight CPS handshake |
| 6 | **Hash Function** | $h = H(m)$, $\vert h\vert = $ fixed | 256 bits | SHA-256 | Integrity of telemetry |
| 7 | **HMAC** | $HMAC_K(m) = H((K\oplus opad) \parallel H((K\oplus ipad) \parallel m))$ | Block size of $H$ | RFC 2104 | Authenticated sensor frames |
| 8 | **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(m))$ | Same as key | RSA-PSS / ECDSA | Code signing in OTA updates |
| 9 | **Nonce-based Challenge-Response** | $R = \text{MAC}_K(N)$ | 128-bit nonce | — | Replay prevention |
| 10 | **X.509 Certificate** | $Cert = \text{Sign}_{CA}(Subject, K_{pub}^{sub}, Validity)$ | — | RFC 5280 | Device identity in PKI |
| 11 | **AES Round Count** | 10 (128-bit) / 12 (192-bit) / 14 (256-bit) | FIPS-197 | — | — |
| 12 | **Birthday Bound (Hash Collision)** | $\approx 2^{n/2}$ for $n$-bit hash | — | — | SHA-256 collision at $\approx 2^{128}$ ops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks "Give the formula for RSA," write $C = P^e \bmod n$ AND $P = C^d \bmod n$ AND the key-generation constraint $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n)$. A partial answer loses 2 of the 7 marks.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in CPS

1. **Smart Grid** — Encryption of smart-meter data prevents privacy leakage of household consumption patterns; signed control commands prevent grid blackouts caused by forged instructions.
2. **Autonomous Vehicles (V2X)** — V2V/V2I messages are **signed with ECDSA** to prevent fake "brake now" instructions; latency budget of < 10 ms forces use of **lightweight ECC**.
3. **Industrial Control (SCADA / Modbus)** — originally plaintext; modernized to **Modbus/TLS** and **OPC-UA with AES-256** to defend against Stuxnet-style attacks.
4. **Medical CPS (Implantables)** — Pacemakers/defibrillators use authenticated telemetry so an attacker cannot trigger a lethal shock via spoofed commands.
5. **OTA Firmware Updates** — Firmware binary is **hashed (SHA-256)** and **signed by manufacturer's private key**; device verifies before flashing. This is the canonical **non-repudiation** use case in CPS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — RSA Key Generation, Encryption & Decryption (Full 7-Mark Derivation)

**Problem (typical KTU Part B):**
Generate the RSA key pair for $p = 5,\; q = 11$, choose $e = 7$. Encrypt the plaintext $M = 9$ and then decrypt the ciphertext. Show every step.

### Step 1 — Compute the modulus $n$

$$n = p \cdot q$$

$$n = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

### Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$

For two distinct primes, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

$$\phi(n) = (5 - 1)(11 - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

### Step 3 — Choose the public exponent $e$

We are given $e = 7$. Verify it is coprime to $\phi(n)$:
- $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✓ (7 is prime, 40 is not a multiple of 7 → co-prime)
- Also $1 < e < \phi(n)$ → $1 < 7 < 40$ ✓

### Step 4 — Compute the private exponent $d$

$d$ is the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod \phi(n) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 7 \cdot d \equiv 1 \bmod 40$$

Use the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$40 = 5 \times 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$$
$$1 = 3 \times (40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 15 \times 7 - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7$$

Therefore $-17 \times 7 \equiv 1 \bmod 40$, so $d \equiv -17 \equiv 40 - 17 = 23 \bmod 40$.

$$\boxed{d = 23}$$

### Step 5 — Publish the public key and retain the private key

$$\text{Public key } (e, n) = (7, 55)$$
$$\text{Private key } d = 23 \text{ (kept secret, but } n \text{ is public)}$$

### Step 6 — Encrypt plaintext $M = 9$

The sender uses the public key $(e, n)$:

$$C = M^e \bmod n$$

$$C = 9^{7} \bmod 55$$

Reduce $9^2 = 81 \equiv 26 \bmod 55$.
$9^4 \equiv 26^2 = 676 \equiv 676 - 12 \times 55 = 676 - 660 = 16 \bmod 55$.
$9^7 = 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 26 \cdot 9 \bmod 55$.
$16 \cdot 26 = 416 \equiv 416 - 7 \times 55 = 416 - 385 = 31 \bmod 55$.
$31 \cdot 9 = 279 \equiv 279 - 5 \times 55 = 279 - 275 = 4 \bmod 55$.

$$\boxed{C = 4}$$

### Step 7 — Decrypt ciphertext $C = 4$

The receiver uses the private key $d$:

$$M = C^{d} \bmod n$$

$$M = 4^{23} \bmod 55$$

By repeated squaring:
$4^1 = 4$
$4^2 = 16$
$4^4 = 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 4 \times 55 = 36 \bmod 55$
$4^8 = 36^2 = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 23 \times 55 = 1296 - 1265 = 31 \bmod 55$
$4^{16} = 31^2 = 961 \equiv 961 - 17 \times 55 = 961 - 935 = 26 \bmod 55$

Now $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1$, so:
$4^{23} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^{4} \cdot 4^{2} \cdot 4^{1}$
$\equiv 26 \cdot 36 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 \bmod 55$
$26 \cdot 36 = 936 \equiv 936 - 17 \times 55 = 936 - 935 = 1 \bmod 55$
$1 \cdot 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 1 \times 55 = 9 \bmod 55$

$$\boxed{M = 9 \; \text{(recovered)}}$$

### Verification — Euer's theorem check

By Euler's theorem, $M^{k\phi(n)+1} \equiv M \bmod n$. Here $7 \times 23 = 161 = 4 \times 40 + 1$, confirming $9^{161} \equiv 9 \bmod 55$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Insight (KTU 7-mark part):**
> - $n$ & $\phi(n)$ → 2 marks
> - $d$ via Extended Euclidean → 2 marks
> - Encryption computation → 1.5 marks
> - Decryption + verification → 1.5 marks

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — ECDH Key Agreement on a Small Curve (Analytical Walkthrough)

Suppose Alice and Bob use the elliptic curve $E: y^2 = x^3 + 2x + 3 \bmod 17$ (illustrative; not standard). Let base point $G = (5, 1)$ of order $n = 19$.

- Alice picks private key $a = 7$, computes public $A = aG = 7G$.
- Bob picks private key $b = 12$, computes public $B = 12G$.
- Alice sends $A$ to Bob; Bob sends $B$ to Alice (publicly, even eavesdroppers can see them).
- Alice computes $S = aB = 7 \cdot 12G = 84G$.
- Bob computes $S = bA = 12 \cdot 7G = 84G$.
- Both arrive at the **same shared secret** $S = 84G = (84 \bmod 19)G = 84 - 4 \times 19 G = 8G$.

**Eve the eavesdropper** sees $A$ and $B$ but cannot compute $a$ from $A$ (would require solving the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem** — infeasible for proper curves with 256-bit order).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway:** ECDH gives a 256-bit shared secret with much shorter key transmission than RSA, which is why it is preferred in CPS handshake protocols.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — HMAC Computation Block-by-Block

To compute $HMAC_K(m)$ with a hash function $H$ of block size $B$ bytes and output size $L$ bytes:

1. If key $K$ is longer than $B$: replace with $K \leftarrow H(K)$.
2. If $K$ is shorter than $B$: right-pad with zeros to length $B$, call this $K_0$.
3. Form $ipad = \text{byte } 0x36$ repeated $B$ times; $opad = \text{byte } 0x5C$ repeated $B$ times.
4. Inner hash: $\text{inner} = H((K_0 \oplus ipad) \parallel m)$.
5. Outer hash: $HMAC_K(m) = H((K_0 \oplus opad) \parallel \text{inner})$.

**Why this construction?** The nested hashing prevents **length-extension attacks** that would otherwise break a naive $H(K \parallel m)$ construction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Board-Favorite Table)

| Property | AES (Symmetric) | RSA (Asymmetric) | ECC (Asymmetric) | SHA-256 (Hash) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Key size (secure 2024+)** | 128 bits | $\geq 2048$ bits | 256 bits | N/A (no key) |
| **Output size** | 128-bit block | 2048-bit ciphertext | ~512-bit ciphertext | 256-bit digest |
| **Speed** | Very fast (~GB/s) | Slow (~1k ops/s) | Fast (~10k ops/s) | Very fast |
| **Primary Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, signatures | Key exchange, signatures (CPS) | Integrity |
| **CPS Suitability** | High (gateway-class) | Low (too heavy for sensors) | High (sensor-class) | High |
| **Confidentiality?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (ECIES) | ❌ No |
| **Authentication?** | ❌ (needs MAC/GCM) | ✅ via RSA-DS | ✅ via ECDSA | ❌ (needs HMAC) |
| **Non-Repudiation?** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End CPS Security Stack (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer[Application Layer SCADA HMI Firmware OTA]
    authLayer[Authentication Layer X.509 Certificates ECDSA Signatures]
    keyLayer[Key Management Layer PKI ECDH Handshake Key Derivation KDF]
    encLayer[Encryption Layer AES 128 GCM ChaCha20 Poly1305]
    macLayer[Integrity Layer HMAC SHA 256]
    physLayer[Physical Layer Wireless Wired LoRa CAN Bus Ethernet]
    appLayer --> authLayer --> keyLayer --> encLayer --> macLayer --> physLayer
    appLayer -.->|protects| authLayer
    keyLayer -.->|feeds keys to| encLayer
    encLayer -.->|provides AEAD| macLayer
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Mutual Authentication in a CPS (TLS 1.3-style)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as CPS Sensor (Client)
    participant Gateway as Edge Gateway
    participant CA as Certificate Authority
    Note over Sensor,CA: Offline Setup - CA issues X.509 certs
    CA-->>Sensor: Cert_Sensor signed by CA
    CA-->>Gateway: Cert_Gateway signed by CA
    Note over Sensor,Gateway: Online Handshake
    Sensor->>Gateway: ClientHello + ephemeral ECDHE key share
    Gateway->>Sensor: ServerHello + Cert_Gateway + ServerKeyShare
    Sensor->>Sensor: Verify Cert_Gateway against CA root
    Sensor->>Gateway: ClientKeyShare + Finished MAC
    Gateway->>Gateway: Verify client Finished MAC
    Note over Sensor,Gateway: Session keys derived via HKDF
    Sensor->>Gateway: Encrypted + Authenticated Telemetry (AES-GCM)
    Gateway->>Sensor: Encrypted + Authenticated Commands
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Cryptographic Primitive to Use in CPS?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Need to secure CPS communication]
    start --> q1{Is the device resource constrained}
    q1 -- Yes Class 0 1 IoT --> light[Use Lightweight Crypto PRESENT ASCON SIMON]
    q1 -- No Gateway Cloud --> std[Use Standard Crypto AES RSA ECC]
    light --> q2{Need to encrypt bulk data}
    std --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> sym[Use Symmetric AES 128 GCM]
    q2 -- No bootstrapping --> asym[Use Asymmetric ECC 256 RSA 2048]
    sym --> q3{Need to prove identity}
    asym --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> cert[Use X.509 Certificates with ECDSA]
    q3 -- No --> mac[Use HMAC SHA 256 for message auth]
    cert --> done[Secure CPS Link Established]
    mac --> done
```

## 4.4 Process Diagram — Digital Signature Lifecycle (Code Signing for OTA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fw[OTA Firmware Image M]
    hash[H = SHA 256 M]
    sign[Sign with Manufacturer Private Key]
    sig[Digital Signature sigma]
    bundle[Signed Package M plus sigma plus Cert]
    deploy[Deploy to CPS Device over network]
    v1[Device computes H prime = SHA 256 M]
    v2[Device verifies sigma with Manufacturer Public Key from Cert]
    accept[Accept and Flash Firmware]
    reject[Reject and Log Security Event]
    fw --> hash --> sign --> sig --> bundle --> deploy
    deploy --> v1 --> v2
    v2 -- valid --> accept
    v2 -- invalid --> reject
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Replay Attack Defense (Nonce + Timestamp)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sender[Sender CPS Node]
    nonceGen[Generate fresh Nonce N and Timestamp T]
    payload[Build Frame ID plus N plus T plus Data]
    mac[Compute MAC = HMAC K ID N T Data]
    send[Transmit Frame]
    receiver[Receiver CPS Node]
    checkT{Is T within acceptable clock skew}
    checkN{Has N been seen before}
    store[Update seen nonces]
    process[Process authenticated frame]
    drop[Drop and alert]
    sender --> nonceGen --> payload --> mac --> send --> receiver --> checkT
    checkT -- No --> drop
    checkT -- Yes --> checkN
    checkN -- Yes --> drop
    checkN -- No --> store --> process
```
